segments.s272-g1: رویه here means 'face/front' (poetic use of 'surface'), and دو رویه = 'two fronts/sides'. ز + noun marks source ('from the army'). برآمد is a simple past/past-stative meaning 'arose/issued forth' and sits after its subject (V-final order is poetic but common). خروش is the thing that rose ('a roar/cry').
segments.s272-g2: This clause is a second clause describing the effect: زمین آمد is an idiomatic past ('the earth came' = the earth was affected/appeared); از marks the cause ('from') نعل اسبان is a genitive noun phrase 'the horses' horseshoes/hooves', به جوش is a prepositional-like phrase meaning 'into boiling/uproar' (جوش = surge/boiling). The verb آمد is simple past and links the ground's state to the horses' action.
segments.s272-g3: سپاه اندر آمد is a past clause: سپاه (the host) + اندر (an archaic preposition/adverb 'in/among') + آمد (past 'came/arrived'). The following short noun phrase ز هر سو گروه ('groups from every side') supplies where the companies came from; ز هر سو = 'from every direction' and گروه is the companies/units (subject for the following verb).
segments.s272-g4: بپوشید is past plural (they) of پوشیدن with the prefix ب- (poetic presentational form) meaning 'they put on' or 'they clad/covered'. جوشن is the direct object 'cuirass/armor'. همه modifies the following nouns as 'all/entire', and دشت و کوه lists the terrain affected: 'plain and mountain'. Word order here is verb → object → modifiers (poetic flexibility).
segments.s272-g5: دو سالار = 'two commanders/chiefs'. هر دو emphasizes 'both of them' (reinforcing duality). به سان is an archaic comparative phrase meaning 'like/as', followed by پلنگ (leopard) as a simile describing their fierceness. The phrase is nominative/attributive to the following verb.
segments.s272-g6: آوریدند is past plural 'they brought'. فراز here is an adverbial/locative 'up/on high' (they brought up/on high), so فراز آوریدند لشکر = 'they brought the army up'. به جنگ is a prepositional phrase 'to/for battle' specifying the purpose/direction of the movement.
segments.s272-g7: به کردار is a frozen poetic phrase meaning 'in the manner of' (کردار used as 'manner/way' here). باران = 'rain'; ز + ابر سیاه = 'from a black/dark cloud' (ز = 'from', سیاه = 'dark/black'). The whole prepositional phrase sets up the simile for what follows.
segments.s272-g8: ببارید is past of باریدن 'to rain/fall', used impersonally or with arrows as subject: 'arrows rained'. تیر is the subject 'arrows'. اندر آن رزمگاه is a locative phrase: اندر = 'in/within', آن = 'that', رزمگاه = 'battlefield/place of contest'. The clause means 'arrows rained down in that battlefield.'
segments.s272-g593: گرفتند is a past 3rd‑person plural verb (‘they seized/took’), so the subject is omitted and understood from the verb. مر is the enclitic object pronoun ‘me’ placed directly after the verb; یکدگر را is a separate definite object marked by را (‘one another’). کنار is an adverbial here meaning ‘to the side/aside’, coming after the objects to indicate position.
segments.s272-g594: به introduces the circumstance or cause (‘with/for’); درد جگر is an idiomatic noun phrase meaning ‘heart‑ache’ (درد = sorrow/pain, جگر = seat of feeling/heart). برگرستند is a past 3rd‑person plural verb meaning ‘they turned back/returned’ (poetic/older form), and زار here describes the manner (‘lamenting/wailing’), functioning adverbially.
segments.s272-g595: برفتند is past 3rd‑person plural ‘they went/departed’. و is the coordinating conjunction ‘and’. بس functions adverbially here (‘just/at once’ or ‘merely’), modifying the immediacy of the following action. روی is ‘face’ (object), and برگاشتند is past 3rd‑person plural ‘they turned back/returned’, so روی برگاشتند = ‘they turned their faces back’.
segments.s272-g596: غریویدن is the infinitive/verbal noun ‘to cry out/roar’ used here in parallel with the finite verb that follows; the coordination signals two related actions. بانگ is the noun ‘cry/shout’ as the object, and برداشتند is past 3rd‑person plural ‘they took up/raised’ (here idiomatically ‘they raised a cry’), so together the clause means ‘they cried out and raised a shout’.
segments.s272-g289: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. از بار آهن is a genitive-like noun phrase 'from/of the iron load' (از + بار + آهن), with آهن in a poetic sense 'iron/iron burden'. برآسوده شد is a passive/perfect-like construction: برآسوده (relieved, at ease) + شد (became/was) — together meaning 'was relieved'. Word order is typical Persian: temporal conjunction → complement phrase → predicate (شد).
segments.s272-g290: This coordinate clause has two verbs linked by و. خورش (here actually the poetic 'roar/cry' — spelled as خورش in the data) is the subject/phenomenon that 'جست' (sprang up, leapt) — جست is a past tense single action verb. The conjunction و links that action to the following passive clause پیموده شد, where پیموده (traversed/covered) + شد (auxiliary 'was') yields 'was traversed/covered'. می‌چند in the middle is an archaic/poetic form whose standard dictionary senses given (plucking/arranging) do not fit straightforwardly here; syntactically it functions to quantify or modify the extent ('many a ...') before پیموده شد.
segments.s272-g291: به تدبیر کردن is an infinitival/nominal phrase meaning 'to devise a plan' or 'for planning' (به + noun تدبیر + light verb کردن). سوی پهلوان is the directional complement 'toward the champion' (سوی = toward, پهلوان = champion). The whole segment functions adverbially/purpose-wise, explaining why they moved.
segments.s272-g292: برفتند is the past tense plural verb 'they went/advanced'. بیدار qualifies the agents as 'awake/alert' and precedes the coordinated nouns پیر و جوان ('old and young'), a compact way in Persian to say 'both the old and the young' acted together; word order places the predicate (برفتند) before its modifiers and subject phrase for poetic effect but the meaning is straightforward: 'the awake old and young went.'
segments.s272-g293: به گودرز پس گفت is an inversion common in poetry: به گودرز 'to Goudarz' (indirect object), پس 'then', گفت is the past-tense verb 'said'. گیو‌ای پدر is the vocative: 'Giv—O father' (گیو with the vocative -ای and پدر 'father'), addressing Goudarz. Note that گفت is listed with noun senses in the data, but here it functions as the verb 'said' (poetic compression/inconsistency in the lemmas).
segments.s272-g294: This is a direct question introduced by چه آمد 'what came' (چه as exclamatory/interrogative + آمد past tense 'came' or 'happened'). مرا is split in the data as م + را; together they mark the experiencer/object 'to me' (م = me, را = object marker). از شگفتی 'from astonishment' gives the cause/quality and به سر 'to (the) head' idiomatically means 'into my mind' or 'upon my head' i.e. 'has come into my mind'. Word order mirrors colloquial Persian question structure: 'چه آمد مرا از شگفتی به سر?'
segments.s272-g295: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. من حمله بردم is a first-person past statement: من 'I' + حمله 'an attack' + بردم (بردم here = 'I carried/waged') — so 'when I made/led an attack'. به توران سپاه is the object/target phrase 'upon the army of Turan' (به + توران + سپاه), with Turan as the opponent.
segments.s272-g296: This main clause uses two coordinated verbal results. دریدم is the perfect/past 'I tore (through)' (first-person), صف 'the ranks/lines' is its object. و links the second verb برگشادند (they opened) which takes راه 'the way' as its object — 'I tore the ranks and (thereby) they opened the way.' Note برگشادند is plural (they opened), reflecting that as a collective result of the action.
segments.s272-g513: پس از + noun is a temporal phrase meaning 'after' (پس here functions with از to make 'after'). جنگ پیشین is 'the earlier battle/war'; که introduces a relative/clausal link 'in which/when'. آمد here is the simple past 'came/occurred' used with شکست (defeat) as its object: 'when defeat came'.
segments.s272-g514: که begins another clause 'that/for which'; توران names the people/land ('the Turanians'); بر آن درد بودند is a copular construction where بودند is past 'were' and بر آن درد means 'under/affected by that pain/sorrow'; پست is a predicate adjective 'low/brought low'. The clause thus states their state resulting from the defeat.
segments.s272-g515: خروشان is a participial/adjectival form 'roaring' placed before پدر ('fathers') to qualify them; بر پسر is the prepositional phrase 'over/upon their sons' (بر here = 'over'); روی زرد is a noun + adjective 'face pale' (زرد used poetically as 'pale/wan'). Word order is poetically compact: modifier + noun, then prepositional phrase, then face + adjective.
segments.s272-g516: This clause uses an elliptic, compressed construction: برادر ز خون برادر = 'brother (because) of the blood of brother' where ز is the poetic preposition meaning 'because of/from'. به درد places them 'in/into pain' with به serving as a prepositional marker and درد as the resultant state ('anguish/sorrow').
segments.s272-g517: همه سر به سر is an idiomatic duplication meaning 'everyone/one and all' (سر here literally 'head' but doubled to form the expression). سوگوار و نژند are two predicates/adjectives 'mourning and ignoble/abject' that describe the whole group; the compounds are simply listed with و 'and'.
segments.s272-g518: دژم گشته is a verbal/adjectival phrase: گشته (perfect/participial of گشتن) with دژم yields 'had become sullen/gloomy'. از introduces the cause 'because of/from' and گشت here means 'turning' (the act of turning); چرخ بلند is a poetic expression for the high wheel/heaven or wheel of fate, with بلند 'lofty'. The whole clause says they became sullen due to the turning of the lofty wheel (i.e., fate).
segments.s272-g519: چو here is the subordinating conjunction 'when'; پیران is the subject 'the elders' and چنان 'thus/so' refers back to the preceding situation. دید is the past 'saw/observed'. لشکر همه follows as the topic 'the whole army' (word order reflects poetic inversion: time/agent/verb/topic).
segments.s272-g520: چو at the start of this simile means 'as/like'. از marks source/reason 'from' (چون 'like' + از = 'as if from/by'); گرگ درنده is a compound 'a ravenous/ferocious wolf' (درنده qualifying گرگ). خسته رمه is 'a flock tired/weary' with adjective after noun, so the army is compared to a flock exhausted by a predatory wolf.
segments.s272-g49: نگه کرد is a past-tense verbal phrase meaning 'looked' (literally 'made a look'); the subject گودرز follows the verb in this line-order for poetic emphasis. تا here means 'as far as / toward' and governs the following locative phrase پشت اوی, where اوی is an older possessive form meaning 'his'.
segments.s272-g50: که is a relative/subordinating particle 'that/who' introducing the clause describing the person. دارد is a present/factual verb 'has' (possesses); ز is the poetic preposition 'from / of / supported by' used before گردان 'troop'; پرخاشجوی is an adjective 'quarrelsome/aggressive' modifying گردان. Word order is typical older Persian: verb (دارد) + prepositional phrase (ز گردان) + adjective (پرخاشجوی).
segments.s272-g51: A sequence of epithets and the name: گرامی 'honored/esteemed' modifies پسر 'son'; شیر 'lion' is an epithet ('lion-hearted') and شرزه 'rebellious/unruly' further qualifies the person; هجیر is the proper name. This is a noun phrase giving title and character before the name.
segments.s272-g52: به here functions as a locative preposition 'at / behind' (towards), so به پشت پدر means 'behind his father'. بود is the past copula 'was / stood' describing position. با is the instrumental preposition 'with' and pairs with تیغ و تیر 'sword and arrows' to indicate arms carried.
segments.s272-g53: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb 'he commanded/said'. تا here expresses result/purpose 'so that / such that', and شد is the past of شدن 'became / came to be' (resulting action). به پشت سپاه is a locative phrase 'at the rear of the army' (به + پشت + سپاه).
segments.s272-g54: This phrase is compressed word-order: بر گیو means 'over/behind Giv' (بر used here as 'over, upon, against' indicating position relative to Giv), followed by the name and title گودرز لشکرپناه in apposition: 'Goudarz, protector of the host' (لشکرپناه is a compound title). The whole names the person who is to occupy that rear position.
segments.s272-g55: بگوید here is an archaic/honorific third-person form used by the poet to introduce reported speech/command ('he said/commanded'); the supplied dictionary senses did not include this reading so its sense is marked unknown. که is the subordinating 'that'; لشکر سوی رود و کوه means 'the army toward the river and the mountain' (سوی governing the directional objects رود and کوه).
segments.s272-g56: به یاری is a purpose phrase 'for aid / to help'; فرستد is a jussive/subjunctive third-person form 'send' used in reported commands (not simple present indicative). گروها گروه is a repetitive adverbial phrase 'group after group / troop upon troop' emphasizing repeated detachments being sent. The order places the purpose (به یاری) before the verb.
segments.s272-g449: ور is the conditional 'if' introducing a supposition. ایدونک is an archaic/poetic particle (here reinforcing the manner or immediacy of the action). The noun phrase لشکر همه همگروه places the quantifier همه before the attributive همگروه, meaning 'the whole army, all of its comrades' with post-nominal attributes common in poetic word order.
segments.s272-g450: به جنگ اندر آید is the verb phrase 'comes into the battle / enters the fight' — به + noun gives direction/goal ('into/towards'), اندر is an emphatic poetic preposition 'in/into', and آید is 3rd-person singular present (narrative) 'comes/enters'. The following به کردار کوه is a simile introduced by به as an adverbial 'in the manner of' (here 'like'), کردار = 'conduct/behavior, manner', and کوه = 'mountain' completes the simile.
segments.s272-g451: ز here is the poetic preposition 'because of / from'. کینه is the object 'hatred/grudge' and همه quantifies the speakers 'all of us'. پاک is used adverbially 'completely/utterly', modifying دلخسته‌ایم, where دلخسته‌ایم is the 1st-person plural present/perfect 'we are weary / we have become weary (of heart)'.
segments.s272-g452: کمر بر میان جنگ را بسته‌ایم is an idiomatic verbal phrase: کمر 'waist' + بستن ('to bind') forms 'to gird one's loins'. بر here is the relational preposition 'upon/for', میان جنگ 'the midst of battle' (میان = middle/among), را is the definite/object marker highlighting میان جنگ as the object, and بسته‌ایم is 1st-person plural perfect 'we have bound / we have girded'.
segments.s272-g453: فدای تو بادا is an optative formula: فدای 'sacrifice for' (used predicatively), تو 'you', and بادا is an optative particle 'may it be' (wish/benediction). The following تن و جان ما lists the things offered: تن 'body' and جان 'life/soul', with ما 'our' attached at the end — typical poetic placement of the possessive.
segments.s272-g454: سراسر is an adverb/adjective 'throughout/entirely'. بر اینست is a copular construction where بر acts as a prepositional/relational marker and اینست = 'this is' (it is thus). پیمان ما 'our pledge' is the predicate nominal; word order places the qualifier before the copula, common in Persian poetry: 'throughout, upon this is our pledge.'
segments.s272-g455: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. گودرز is the subject (proper name). پاسخ بر این‌سان شنود combines پاسخ 'reply' with بر این‌سان 'in/like this' (بر as a locative/relational marker + این‌سان 'thus'), and شنود is an archaic 3rd-person singular present/past-hearing form 'hears/heard' used in narrative contexts.
segments.s272-g456: به دلش اندرون شادمانی فزود is a result clause: به = 'to/into', دلش = 'his heart' (دل + suffix ش = his), اندرون = 'within/inner part', شادمانی 'joy', and فزود is an archaic past/preterite 3rd-person form 'increased' or 'grew'. The verb thus states that joy increased within his heart.
segments.s272-g521: سران را ز لشکر سراسر بخواند is an SOV-style clause where بخواند (past/epic present 'called/summoned') is the main verb at the end. را is the definite/object marker attached to سران (the direct object). ز + لشکر سراسر = 'from the army throughout' (ز = 'from', سراسر = 'throughout/through all'), so the phrase means 'summoned the leaders from throughout the army.'
segments.s272-g522: فراوان سخن پیش ایشان براند: براند (epic/present form of راندن here) = 'he brought/put forward' or more naturally 'addressed'. فراوان modifies سخن: 'many/much speech' = 'at length'. پیش ایشان = 'before them / in their presence'. The verb at the end governs the whole clause.
segments.s272-g523: چنین گفت = 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so'; گفت normally 'said' but the supplied senses are noun-like, so the verb sense is not listed). کای is a vocative 'O'. کار = 'deed/affair' and دیده گوان here functions as a description: دیده (past participle 'seen') + گوان (a formative/adjectival element), giving 'whose deeds have been seen' or 'experienced in deeds.' The whole segment is a vocative clause introducing the speech.
segments.s272-g524: همه سوده رزم پیر و جوان is a noun phrase appositive describing the addressees. همه = 'all/everyone'; سوده here = 'advantageous/profited' or 'skilful/experienced' (literary use), modifying رزم = 'battle'; پیر و جوان = 'old and young' (a conventional pair meaning 'people of every age'). Together: 'all experienced in battle, old and young.'
segments.s272-g525: شما را به نزدیک افراسیاب: شما را is topical/emphatic 'you' (را marking the addressee); به نزدیک = 'near/in the vicinity of'; افراسیاب is the proper name. The phrase sets the location/context for the question that follows.
segments.s272-g526: چه مایه بزرگی و جاهست و آب is an interrogative/exclamatory clause. چه مایه = 'what sort/amount (of)'; بزرگی = 'greatness'; و = 'and'; جاهست here functions as an existential/copular-like predicate ('there is / is possessed'); و آب = 'and water' (here 'water' is figurative for means/resources/wealth in epic usage). Together they ask what greatness, rank and means you possess near Afrasiab.
segments.s272-g527: به پیروزی و فرهی کامتان is an ablative/benefactive phrase introduced by به (= 'by/through' here). پیروزی = 'victory'; و links to فرهی which is an adjective 'noble' modifying کامتان (کام + ـتان = 'your desire(s)/fulfilment'). The phrase means 'by victory and the noble fulfilment of your wishes.'
segments.s272-g528: به گیتی پراگنده شد نامتان is the result clause: به گیتی = 'throughout/in the world' (به used with a locative sense), پراگنده شد = 'became scattered / spread' (شد = past auxiliary 'became'), and نامتان = 'your name/reputation' (نام + -تان). Word order places the verb before the possessed noun (poetic preference), but the meaning is 'your name spread through the world.'
segments.s272-g369: "سبک" is used adverbially here ('lightly/quickly'), placed before the verb phrase; "با سپاه" is a prepositional phrase 'with the army' (با + noun); "اندر آمد" is the past tense verb 'entered / came in' with the archaic locative "اندر" ('into/within'); "به راه" ('onto the road' or 'on the road') is a goal/directional phrase following the verb. The subject is omitted (classical Persian allows a null subject).
segments.s272-g370: "که" introduces a result/explanatory clause ('that/so that'); "هر دو" = 'both' (each of the two); "کندشان" is the verb "کند" + enclitic object "شان" = 'he/it makes/causes them' (classical present/past-tense usage of کند as causative/performative); "به ره" is a directional phrase 'onto/along the road' and "بر تباه" ('to ruin') adds the result/direction of action. Together the clause states a consequence: both of them are brought to ruin on the road.
segments.s272-g371: "بکرد" is the past-tense verbal form 'he/it did' (transitive); "آنچ" = 'that which / what'; "بودش" = "بود + ش(ان)" where "بود" is the copula 'was' and the enclitic "ش/شان" refers back to the subject/object ('it was for him/it was so'); "ز بد دسترس" is a causal phrase 'from/because of bad access' (ز = poetic 'from/because of', "بد دسترس" = 'poor accessibility / bad reach'). The clause therefore says he acted according to what arose from poor access.
segments.s272-g372: "جهاندارشان" = "جهاندار + شان", 'their sovereign/king' with possessive enclitic; "بد نگهدار" is a nominal-predicate phrase 'a bad keeper/poor guardian' (بد modifies نگهدار); "و بس" is a terse connective meaning 'and that is all / nothing more'. The clause judges the sovereign as an inadequate guardian and closes the remark.
segments.s272-g373: از آن پس is a formulaic temporal phrase 'after that'. به کین سیاوش expresses purpose/intention: به + کین + proper name = 'for / with the vengeance of Siavash' → 'seeking to avenge Siavash'. سپاه is the subject 'the army' (noun comes after the purpose phrase in this poetic word order).
segments.s272-g374: سوی is the preposition 'toward'. کاسه رود is the place-name 'Kaseh River' (here a toponym: کاسه + رود); because the dictionary senses given are literal ('bowl', 'portion') the place-name reading does not match those senses. اندر آمد is the compound verbal phrase 'came in/entered' (اندر + آمد); به راه is a locative expression 'onto/into the road' or 'on the way' explaining how they came.
segments.s272-g375: به لاون 'to/at Laun' (place-name). که آمد is the relative/temporal clause marker 'when/that came'. سپاه is the noun 'army/host' and گشن is an attributive adjective 'hungry' (سپاه گشن = 'the hungry army/host'); the adjective follows the noun here, typical in Persian epic phrasing.
segments.s272-g376: شبیخون is the noun 'night-raid' (a surprise attack). پیران in the lexicon list could mean 'elders', but in the Shahnameh context this is most plausibly the proper name Piran (a Turanian commander); the given sense list does not capture the proper-name use. و is the conjunction 'and'; جنگ پشن is 'a battle at the rear / rear-attack' — پشن (dialectal for پشت) means 'back/rear' and here qualifies جنگ as location/type of fight. Grammatically this string lists the actions/events associated with the arrival.
segments.s272-g257: This noun phrase names the agent: سپهدار (head/commander) modified by پیران (of the elders). The preposition ز (from) introduces the source سالارگاه (the royal hall/seat of leadership). Word order is straightforward: head noun + modifier + prepositional phrase marking origin.
segments.s272-g258: Two past actions with the same subject are placed in sequence: بیامد (he came) followed by بیاراست (he set in order/arrayed). قلب سپاه is the direct object phrase 'the heart/core of the army' (قلب = core). The verb بیاراست is an archaic poetic perfect/past form meaning 'adorned/arranged'.
segments.s272-g259: This temporal and locative clause strings prepositions and temporal markers: ز (from) شبگیر تا شب ('from dusk until night') uses تا as 'until'. برآمد is the past tense 'arose/issued forth', and the following ز کوه gives the place 'from the mountain'. The clause order places time expressions before the verb and then the place-of-origin phrase.
segments.s272-g260: This is a nominal phrase giving what rose: سواران ('horsemen') followed by a genitive/associative phrase ایران و توران ('of Iran and Turan') and closed by گروه ('a band/company'). The noun-group-final word گروه functions as a classifier meaning 'troop/band'.
segments.s272-g261: همی is an older continuous/emphatic particle meaning 'continually/kept on'. گرد here is a noun 'dust' that combines with کینه ('hatred') to form 'the dust of hatred' (a poetic image). برانگیختند is the past tense 'they aroused/raised'. The verb follows the object phrase in this poetic word order but takes that phrase as its object.
segments.s272-g262: Again همی gives iterative aspect. خاک (earth/soil) + با (with) + خون (blood) is the object phrase 'earth with blood', and برآمیختند is the past 'they mixed/mingled'. The compound verb برآمیختند is an archaic past tense meaning 'mingled together'.
segments.s272-g263: از introduces the source/list 'from the horses and the men'. همه 'all' quantifies the following predicate. رفته هوش is an inverted order where رفته ('gone') precedes هوش ('awareness/sense'); together they mean 'all awareness/sense was gone'. The past participle/verb رفته conveys resultant state.
segments.s272-g264: This clause lists the effects: دهن خشک 'mouths dry' (adjective phrase). Then و (and) connects to a second predication: رفته ز تن ('gone from the body') modifies زور و توش ('strength and provision/share'). The verb رفته governs the following from-phrase ز تن indicating what has departed and from where.
segments.s272-g193: "چو" is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a clause; word order is canonical for Classical Persian with subject 'گیو' before the locative phrase 'اندر آن زخم او' and the verb 'بنگرید' placed at the end, meaning 'looked (into/at)'. "او" is a 3rd‑person pronoun 'his/him' modifying 'زخم'.
segments.s272-g194: This is a short past clause with an omitted explicit subject (understood from the prior clause). "عمود گران" is the direct object 'a heavy pillar/shaft', "از میان" is a prepositional phrase 'out of/from the middle', and the verb "برکشید" is past 'lifted/raised' (or 'drew up').
segments.s272-g195: The verb "بزد" ('he struck') begins the clause. "چون" here means 'as/like' and introduces a similative phrase "چون یکی تیزدم" ('like one giving a sharp/quick stroke'); the object "اژدها" ('the dragon') follows at the end. Note the compressed, poetic phrasing: "تیزدم" functions as a terse description of the manner (a sharp/quick blow).
segments.s272-g196: "که" introduces an explanatory/result clause. "از دست او" is a prepositional phrase 'from his hand'; the sequence "خنجر آمد رها" places the verb "آمد" ('came') before the predicate adjective/participle "رها" ('loose/freed'), so literally 'a dagger came (being) loose' — i.e., the dagger came free from his hand.
segments.s272-g197: "سبک" is used in the sense 'light (in weight/force)'; "دیگری زد" means 'he struck another (blow)'. The phrase "به گردنش بر" is a doubled prepositional framing ('upon his neck') — "به گردنش" gives the target and the final "بر" is an emphatic/prepositional particle often retained for metrical/rhythmic reasons in poetry.
segments.s272-g198: "که" here links the result or consequence. The verb "ببارید" is past 'rained (down)', and "آتش ببارید بر تنش" means 'fire rained upon his body'. The final trailing "بر" again functions as a metrical/emphatic preposition repeating the idea 'on/upon'.
segments.s272-g199: "بجوشید" is a past intransitive meaning 'gushed/boiled up' (here physical eruption), "خون بر دهانش" marks the destination 'blood into his mouth', and "از جگر" gives the source 'from his liver' — a vivid bodily image typical of epic poetry.
segments.s272-g200: The clause begins with the possessed noun "تنش" ('his body') followed by the adjective "سست" ('weak'), then the verb "برگشت" ('returned/turned back') — literally 'his body turned weak and he withdrew'. "آسیمه سر" is a compound adverbial phrase meaning 'headlong/in panic' ("آسیمه" distraught + "سر" 'head', used adverbially).
segments.s272-g465: The clause begins with the preposition به + addressee (here Shidush) marking the indirect object: 'to Shidush'. فرمود is the honorific/past verb 'he said/commanded'. کای is a vocative 'O' addressing پور من ('my son')—پور (son) followed by من (used possessively here) forms 'my son'. Word order is preposition + addressee + verb + vocative phrase.
segments.s272-g466: This is an object/complement clause specifying the content of the command. به هر کار شایسته = 'in/for every appropriate matter' (به + quantifier + noun + adjective). دستور من = 'my command/order' (دستور noun modified by the possessor من). The whole phrase completes what he told Shidush to do.
segments.s272-g467: تو is the direct vocative/imperative addressee 'you'. با + کاویانی درفش و سپاه is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with the Kayanian banner and host' that specifies what the addressee should take or accompany him. Word order places the addressee first for emphasis, then the accompanying instruments.
segments.s272-g468: برو is the imperative 'go'. پشت لشکر is a locative phrase 'to/at the rear of the army' (پشت = rear/behind + لشکر). تو باش is an imperative construction 'be thou' (تو explicit for contrast/emphasis). The final و پناه links a predicate noun 'and refuge/protection', so the clause means 'be its support and refuge.'
segments.s272-g469: بفرمود پس = 'then he said/commanded' (بفرمود honorific past + پس 'then/afterwards'). گستهم را uses the name Gostaham with the object/topic marker را to indicate the person spoken to. که شو is a short imperative-like sequence where شو is the archaic/imperative 'become'/'be' and که links the reported speech: 'that be...'.
segments.s272-g470: سپه را is 'the army' marked by را (definite/topic). تو باش is an imperative 'you be' (explicit subject for emphasis). این زمان پیشرو places the role and time: 'the forerunner/ vanguard at this time' (این زمان = now/at this time, پیشرو = vanguard/forerunner). Together: 'Be the army's vanguard now.'
segments.s272-g471: ترا here is tokenized as ت + را; together تـَرا = 'for you / you' (object/beneficiary). بود باید is an older/modal periphrastic construction: بود (auxiliary) + باید (must/should) giving a necessity sense 'you ought to be'. به سالارگاه = 'at the command/royal hall' (به marking the location). Overall: 'You should be at the command-hall.'
segments.s272-g472: نگه‌دار بیدار is a noun phrase 'an alert guardian' or 'watchful keeper' (نگه‌دار = guardian, بیدار = awake/alert). پشت سپاه = 'at the army's rear' (پشت = rear/behind, سپاه = army). The whole phrase states the role the addressee must perform: 'be the watchful guardian of the army's rear.'
segments.s272-g401: A coordinated subject phrase: 'من و ... و گیو' lists the speakers/actors joined by the conjunction و. گرد here names the group or assembly ('the circle/company of') that takes the following noun پیران ('elders') as its complement; رویین is presented as another member (or epithet) and گیو is the proper name. Word order is simply a series of coordinated noun phrases functioning together as the subject of the verb in the next segment.
segments.s272-g402: یکایک is an adverb meaning 'one by one' or 'each in turn' modifying the verb بسازیم. بسازیم is first-person plural present (classical usage) expressing volition or future-oriented action ('we will/must make' or 'let us make'). مردان is the direct object 'men' (i.e. warriors/people) and نیو qualifies it as 'new' (here meaning 'new, freshly made'); overall the clause means 'we will make new men, one by one.'
segments.s272-g403: که introduces a clause of explanation: 'that/for' (here 'for' or 'since'). کس is used negatively in this line to mean 'no one' (classical poetry often omits an explicit negative particle, the sense is 'there is no one who...'). در جهان is the locative 'in the world'. جاودانه is an adjective 'everlasting, eternal'. نماند is a present/gnomic form of ماندن 'to remain/last' meaning 'does not remain' or more generally 'fails to endure' in this context.
segments.s272-g404: This clause repeats the locative 'in the world' with به گیتی ('giti' = the world/earth). به ما 'for us' marks the experiencer/possessor. جز is the preposition 'except'. فسانه is 'tale, legend' functioning as the only thing left, and نماند again is 'remains' (here negated by جز ... نماند: 'nothing remains for us except a tale'). Word order places the negated predicate at the end, typical of Persian clauses.
segments.s272-g405: هم آن نام is a demonstrative subject phrase: 'that same name' or 'that very fame.' باید is a modal auxiliary meaning 'must' or 'ought to.' که introduces the subordinate clause whose predicate is ماند بلند, where ماند ('remain') takes بلند ('lofty') as a predicative adjective. The structure amounts to 'That name must remain high/lofty.'
segments.s272-g406: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' مرگ is the agent/subject 'death.' افگند is a past/perfective verb 'threw' or 'cast' (classical epic usage). سوی ما بر کمند is the object phrase: سوی ما = 'toward us' and بر کمند = 'upon/with a snare' (کمند = lasso/snare). The verb governs the following directional phrase meaning 'when death cast a snare toward us.'
segments.s272-g407: زمانه here names the abstract 'time' or 'fate/fortune.' The prepositional phrases به مرگ و به کشتن ('with death and with killing') qualify the domain in which زمانه is 'one' (یکیست = 'is one/the same'). یکیست is the copula 'is one/identical.' The clause asserts that time/fate is united with death and killing.
segments.s272-g408: وفا ('fidelity, loyalty') is the subject. با سپهر ('with the sky/heavens') specifies the sphere in which that fidelity is considered. روان here modifies سپهر as 'moving' or 'in motion' (a poetic epithet for the heavens), and اندکیست is a predicative existential/quantitative statement meaning 'is little' or 'is slight/brief.' The clause says that loyalty under the capricious heavens is small/fleeting.
segments.s272-g473: سپه را بفرمود is a subject–object–verb order where سپه (the host) is marked by را as the definite/direct object of بفرمود (honorific past: 'he commanded/said'). کز is an archaic contraction of که از (that/from), introducing the following locative phrase جای خویش (جای + خوی + ش = جای خویش ‘their/his own place’); خوی and ش are split in the manuscript but together form the possessive.
segments.s272-g474: نگر is an imperative particle meaning 'look' or 'see to it'. ناورید here functions as an imperative (negative sense in older usage) 'do not withdraw/retreat' (an archaic form), followed by the adverb اندکی 'a little' and the noun phrase پای پیش 'foot forward' meaning 'step forward' (literally 'foot before'). Word order is imperative + negative/command + manner phrase.
segments.s272-g475: همه is the vocative/subject 'all' or 'everyone'. گستهم is a poetic/archaic verbal noun meaning 'my spreading/exploits/wanderings' (here referring to the speaker’s deeds) with را marking it as object. کنید is the 2nd-person plural imperative 'do/make', and آفرین functions as an interjection/noun 'praise' or 'acclamation' (i.e. 'make [them] praise').
segments.s272-g476: شب و روز باشید is a 2nd-person plural imperative 'be day and night' (i.e. remain constantly). بر is the preposition 'on/upon', followed by پشت زین 'the back of the saddle' (پشت = back/rear, زین = saddle), giving the locative phrase 'upon the saddle'.
segments.s272-g477: برآمد is a past-tense verb 'arose/issued forth' (3rd singular), followed by the noun خروش 'cry/roar'. از میان سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the middle of the host' (از میان = from among, سپاه = army/host). The verb governs the source phrase, so the whole means 'a cry arose from among the army.'
segments.s272-g478: گرفتند is a past plural verb 'they took/they were seized by' used idiomatically here as 'they burst into' or 'were seized by' (a feeling). زاری is the noun 'lamentation'; بر آن رزمگاه is a locative phrase 'over/on that battlefield' (بر = on/upon, آن = that, رزمگاه = battlefield).
segments.s272-g479: همه 'all' plus the reduplicated idiom سر به سر (literally 'head to head', idiomatically 'one and all' or 'in full force') is the subject, سوی او is the prepositional phrase 'toward him', and تاختند is the past plural verb 'they rushed/charged'. The construction places the direction before the verb, a common order in classical Persian.
segments.s272-g480: همی is an adverb meaning 'continually' or a simple narrative marker 'then/thereupon'. خاک بر سر is an idiom 'dust upon (their) heads' indicating grief or abasement, with برانداختند the past plural verb 'they threw/cast' (here 'they cast dust upon [their] heads'). The object (خاک) precedes the prepositional phrase بر سر in the typical Persian order.
segments.s272-g57: و دیگر بفرمود گفتن به گیو is a single clause: و 'and/and furthermore' links to the previous action; دیگر here means 'in addition/other (further)'; بفرمود is an honorific past verb 'he ordered/commanded'; گفتن is the verbal noun 'to tell' (object of بفرمود); به + گیو marks the indirect object 'to Giv'. Word order is typical Persian: verb (بفرمود) governs the verbal noun and a prepositional indirect object follows.
segments.s272-g58: که introduces a reported/subordinate clause 'that ...'; پشت سپه را is a genitive/accusative phrase 'the rear of the army' with را marking it as definite/object; یکی مرد is an indefinite numeral phrase 'one man/a man' (numeric 'one'); نیو here is an adjective (regional/poetic form of 'نو') meaning 'new' or 'fresh' modifying مرد — so the whole clause says 'that for the army's rear a single new man (be appointed)'. The main verb of this subordinate clause (appointed/chosen) appears in the next sentence.
segments.s272-g59: گزیند is an archaic/historical-present form of 'to choose' (here narratively past: 'he chose'); سپارد is 'he entrusted/left' (past/narrative); بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him' (بد + او fused); جای خویش = 'his place/post' where جای = 'place/post' and خویش is the reflexive/possessive 'his own' (here written in two pieces as خوی + ش). The clause means 'he chose (someone) and entrusted to him his post.'
segments.s272-g60: نهد is the verb 'to place/put' (here narrative past/perfective sense 'he set/placed'); او is the subject 'he'; از آن جایگه is a prepositional phrase 'from that spot/place' (آن = that, جایگه = place/post); پای پیش is an idiomatic phrase 'the foot forward' meaning 'he advanced a step' or 'stepped forward' (پای = foot, پیش = forward/before). Word order places the verb before its subject in this poetic narrative sequence.
segments.s272-g61: هجیر خردمند is a name with an epithet: proper name (هجیر) followed by the adjective 'خردمند' ('wise'); بسته کمر is a participial/adjectival phrase 'having girded his waist' or 'girt his loins' where بسته (bound/tied) modifies کمر (waist); this is a circumstantial participle describing Hajir's readiness before the following action.
segments.s272-g62: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; بشنید is the past tense 'he heard'; گفتار is the noun 'speech/words'; فرخ functions here as a proper name or epithet (lexically 'fortunate') and پدر means 'father' — together 'گفتار فرخ پدر' = 'the words of Farrokh the father' (a genitive relationship: گفتارِ فرخِ پدر). The clause is subordinate (when he heard ... ) and leads into the next action.
segments.s272-g63: بیامد is the past tense 'he came'; به سوی برادر is a direction phrase 'toward his brother' (به سوی = toward; برادر = brother); دوان is an adverb/participle 'running/rushed' modifying the motion verb — together they mean 'he ran/ came running toward his brother.'
segments.s272-g64: بگفت is 'he said' (narrative past); آن کجا is an interrogative phrase 'where is that ...' (آن = that; کجا = where); گفته is a verbal adjective/participle 'spoken of/said' (so 'آن ... گفته' = 'that which has been spoken of'); بد پهلوان is a noun phrase where پهلوان = 'champion/hero' and بد here is used archaically as part of the phrase (in context functioning as a fixed/formulaic modifier often conveying notoriety or emphasis rather than simple 'bad'); the whole clause reports his question: 'Where is that champion spoken of?' Note the word-order is poetic and somewhat elliptical: participle (گفته) stands after کجا to mean 'where is the one spoken of?'.
segments.s272-g17: This is two short predicative phrases placed side by side. زمین لاله‌گون شد is a noun + adjectival predicate with the past copula شد ('became') after the adjective: 'the earth became tulip‑colored.' The second phrase, هوا نیلگون, omits the copula (a common poetic device) and reads as a noun followed by an adjective used predicatively: 'the air [was] indigo.' Word order is noun → adjective (predicate) rather than adjective‑noun.
segments.s272-g18: برآمد is a past/inchoative verb 'arose/issued forth' placed before its noun phrase (verb‑first order for vivid narration). همی is a classical particle here giving iterative/continuous or emphatic coloring to the action. موج دریای خون is a noun phrase in which دریای is a genitive modifier ('sea of') of خون: 'a wave of the sea of blood.'
segments.s272-g19: دو سالار گفتند is a simple past clause: 'the two commanders said.' اگر همچنان (اگر همچنین) introduces a conditional/subordinate clause meaning 'if so/if that is the case.' گفتند is past plural 'they said'; همچنین here means 'thus/so' (not merely 'also').
segments.s272-g20: بداریم is 1st‑person‑plural classical/jussive form (let us keep / we should hold) used for a proposal or resolved action. گردان is the object meaning 'troop' here. بر indicates position 'on/upon' (بر این دشت کین = 'on this plain of vengeance'). دشت کین is an idafa‑like sequence 'plain of vengeance.'
segments.s272-g21: شب تیره را uses را to topicalize/time‑mark the phrase 'that dark night.' کس نماند به جای is a negated past clause meaning 'no one remained (in) his place' — نماند is past negative sense 'was left/remained not.' به جای = 'in place/position.'
segments.s272-g22: جز از introduces an exception: 'except from/for.' چرخ گردان is a compound where گردان is adjectival 'revolving' modifying چرخ (the sky/wheel = heavens/fate). و connects the second item, گیهان خدای, an inverted order meaning 'God of the world' (literally 'world God'). Together: 'except the revolving heavens and the world's God.'
segments.s272-g23: چو here is temporal 'when.' پیران is the plural subject 'the elders.' چنان modifies دید: 'saw/found it thus' — دید is past 'saw/perceived' used in the sense 'considered/found.' جای نبرد is a noun phrase 'place of battle' (جای = place, نبرد = battle) serving as the complement describing the situation they judged.
segments.s272-g24: به marks the indirect object: 'to/for.' لهاک and فرشیدورد are recipients of فرمود ('he said/commanded') — فرمود is past 'he said/commanded.' The coordinating و simply links the two names: 'he told/commanded Lahāk and Farshidvard.'
segments.s272-g457: بر (preposition) introduces the target of the praise ('upon'); آن is the demonstrative determiner 'those/that' modifying نامداران ('the renowned ones'). گرفت is a past tense verb here used idiomatically with آفرین to mean 'took/uttered (praise)' — a poetic compound 'گرفت آفرین' = 'burst into praise' or 'gave acclaim'.
segments.s272-g458: که‌ای functions as a vocative/exclamatory particle here (classical/poetic 'که ای' = 'O you'); نره qualifies شیران, literally 'male' used as an epithet; شیران is a metaphorical plural 'lions' meaning brave men. ایران‌زمین is a compound noun 'land of Iran' identifying whose 'lions' are addressed.
segments.s272-g459: سپه را uses را as the definite/direct-object marker attached to سپه ('the army') and makes سپه the object of بفرمود. بفرمود is the polite/honorific past 'he ordered/commanded'. تا + verb (برنشست) expresses purpose/result here: 'so that they took their seats' or 'to have them sit down.'
segments.s272-g460: همیدون is a proper name (the agent who acts in the second clause). میان را is the direct object ('the middle/center') marked by را. به کینه is an adverbial phrase 'with hatred/with a grudge' (به used to mark the manner), and ببست is the past tense 'he shut/closed' (here 'closed up' or 'sealed').
segments.s272-g461: چپ لشکرش is a locative phrase 'on the left of his army' (چپ 'left' precedes the possessed noun لشکرش 'his army'). جای رهام is 'the post/place of Rohām'; گرد here is a postpositive adverbial meaning 'around' or 'thereabouts' indicating position.
segments.s272-g462: به marks the indirect object/recipient ('to/for'). فرهاد is the personal name; خورشید پیکر is a descriptive epithet 'sun-bodied' (noun + noun used attributively: 'of sun-like body' or 'radiant-bodied'). سپرد is the past tense 'he entrusted/handed over' (placed in charge of).
segments.s272-g463: سوی راست is a locative phrase 'to/on the right side' (سوی 'toward/side' + راست 'right'). جای فریبرز بود is a simple past copular clause 'the place of Fariborz was' — word order places the locative frontally and closes with the past auxiliary بود 'was.'
segments.s272-g464: به introduces the recipient ('to'); کتماره is a proper name (recipient). قارنان here means 'companions/peers' (the direct object of داد). داد is the past tense 'gave' and زود is an adverb 'quickly/soon' modifying داد.
segments.s272-g345: برآمد is a past verb ('arose/occurred') placed first — a common poetic order. بر آن کار او is a prepositional phrase ('on/for that affair of his') giving the cause or domain of what 'arose'. چند سال is a temporal expression ('several years') placed after the phrase to indicate the duration; او is the independent pronoun 'he' used instead of a possessive suffix.
segments.s272-g346: بد functions as an adverb/adjectival modifier meaning 'evil/ill' and combines with the verb انداخت (past of انداختن) meaning 'cast/caused' — here 'caused misfortune'. یزدان is the logical subject ('God') and بردو/بر آن بدسگال is the prepositional phrase ('upon that ill‑fated one') indicating the target. The order (modifier + verb + subject + prep phrase) reflects poetic inversion.
segments.s272-g347: This is a name with attributive epithets: فریدون (proper name) followed by فرخ شه, where فرخ modifies شه ('fortunate king'), and دادگر is an appositive epithet ('the just one'). In Persian poetic usage epithets often follow or cluster after the name.
segments.s272-g348: ببست is the past form of بستن ('he girded/tied'); the doubled b- is a poetic/formal variant. کمر ببستن ('to gird the waist') is an idiom meaning 'to prepare oneself'. اندر آن پادشاهی is a locative/purpose phrase ('in/for that kingship'), with کمر as the direct object (what he girded).
segments.s272-g349: همه is 'all/every', بند here means 'bond/fetter' (not stanza), and آهرمنی is an adjective 'Ahrimanic, demonic'. برگشاد is the past verb 'opened/loosed' (بر‑گشاد), so the phrase means 'he opened/loosed every demonic bond'; the subject is implied from context (Fereydun).
segments.s272-g350: بیاراست is a past verb 'he adorned/arranged' (بیاراستن), گیتی 'the world' is the object, سراسر 'throughout/entire' modifies the scope, and به داد means 'with justice' or 'in justice' (به + noun). Word order places the verb before its object in poetic narrative.
segments.s272-g351: چو functions as a temporal/concessive conjunction 'when/as'. ضحاک is the proper name; بدگوهر ('of bad essence') and بدمنش ('ill‑mannered') are coordinating epithets describing him. Adjectival epithets follow the name in this poetic register.
segments.s272-g352: که here introduces the subordinate clause and functions like 'that/so that'; کردند is the past plural 'they did/made' (here part of the compound predicate 'سرزنش کردند' = 'they reproached'), شاهان is the subject 'the kings', بدو is the dative 'to him', and سرزنش is the noun 'reproach' used with کردن to form the verb 'to reproach'. The literal order is verb + subject + indirect object + noun, a poetic inversion; meaning: 'the kings reproached him.'
segments.s272-g569: This is a string of rhetorical questions. چرا (why) opens the question; سر بپیچیم is verb-before-subject: بپیچیم is the present-subjunctive/hortative first-person-plural form of پیچیدن used here as a modal 'should we turn/turn (our) heads'; ما follows the verb for emphasis; خود modifies کییم ('who/what are we ourselves?'), with کییم an archaic poetic interrogative 'are we who/what'.
segments.s272-g570: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the clause; بنده شه is an optative/hortative construction meaning 'become a slave/servant' where شه is a compressed/poetic form of 'شود' (become) — an archaic literary usage; ز بهر introduces purpose/reason 'for the sake of / for what', and چییم is the interrogative 'what/why are we' completing the rhetorical question.
segments.s272-g571: بگفتند is simple past 'they said' (narrative). وز here is a short archaic/poetic connective meaning roughly 'then' or 'from there' (not the common modern senses), and پیش 'before/thereupon' combines with برخاستند 'they rose' (past plural), so the clause means 'they said it and then rose up.'
segments.s272-g572: به پیکار is a prepositional phrase 'for/to battle'; یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/at once' modifying the verb; بیاراستند is past third-person-plural 'they arrayed/armed/readyed themselves' (bi- + آراستن), so overall 'they armed themselves completely for battle.'
segments.s272-g573: همه شب 'all night' is a temporal phrase; همی + ساختند expresses a habitual/continuous past 'they kept making/they were constantly composing' and این سخن 'this saying/phrase' is the direct object — i.e. they repeatedly composed or uttered this line through the night.
segments.s272-g574: که introduces reported speech or a clause 'that ...'; افگند here is literary 'uttered/let fall (a word)' and takes سالار 'the commander' as its subject. بیدار بن is an archaic imperative: بیدار 'awake' + بن (archaic imperative of 'to be' = 'be'), so it renders the quoted command 'Be awake!'.
segments.s272-g575: به شبگیر is a temporal phrase 'at nightfall / as night came'; آوای 'the sound/voice of' takes the instruments that follow as its genitive objects: شیپور و نای 'the trumpet and the ney (reed flute)'. Word order is typical literary Persian: temporal preposition + noun phrase 'the sound of ...'.
segments.s272-g576: ز پرده برآمد is a simple past passive/semi-transitive idiom 'issued forth/rose from (behind) the curtain' where ز is the poetic 'from'; به هر دو سرای means 'into/between both halls' (هر دو = both). Here سرای means 'hall/chamber' (a sense not listed in the provided senses), so that specific sense is marked separately.
segments.s272-g145: This noun phrase identifies the agent: همیدون (a personal name) followed by a measure/phrase سه چوبه and the locative/agentive phrase بر اسب‌سوار. In classical Persian poetry the name can precede a descriptive phrase; بر here means "on/upon" and اسب‌سوار is a postpositive apposition meaning "the horseman" (i.e., Hemidun as a rider).
segments.s272-g146: بزد is a past-tense, 3rd-person singular verb meaning "(he) struck/hit"; it takes گیو as its object (Giv). پیکان آهن is a noun phrase "iron arrow" where آهن qualifies پیکان, and گذار here appears as an additional epithet/element attached to the arrow (the exact nuance is poetic/compound). The whole clause reads: "(he) struck Giv with an iron arrow."
segments.s272-g147: نشد is the negative past form meaning "did not become" or "did not (come to be)"; here it negates خسته (tired) with اسب as its logical subject: "the horse did not become tired." The following نه پیران نیو is another negative clause coordinated by نه ("nor"), referring to the elders; نیو is anomalous/unclear in meaning here (textual or dialectal) and acts as part of that coordinated phrase.
segments.s272-g148: بدانجا رسیدند is a compound past verb phrase: بدانجا "to that place" + رسیدند (3rd‑pl. past of "to arrive") — "they arrived there." یاران گیو is the subject phrase in inverted order: "Giv's companions" (یاران = companions, followed by the possessor گیو). Together: "Giv's companions arrived there."
segments.s272-g149: چو here functions as a subordinating conjunction "when." پیران چنان دید is a relative/time clause: "when the elders saw it so/that." برگشت زود follows as the main clause: برگشت (they turned back/returned) with زود as an adverb "quickly/at once." The sequence thus means "When the elders saw that, they quickly returned."
segments.s272-g150: This clause describes pursuit: برفت is an action verb meaning "(he) went off/set off" (poetic past), از پسش "after him/behind him," گیو is the pursuer, and تازان چو دود is a simile: تازان (an epithet meaning fierce/pressing/rushing in context) + چو دود "like smoke." The line therefore says Giv set off after him, rushing like smoke. Note that some words here are poetic and their dictionary senses are limited, so the grammatical roles (verb + prepositional phrase + simile) are the key signals.
segments.s272-g151: به نزدیک is a locative phrase "to/near" and is followed by گیو آمد (Giv + came), but word order here is inverted for poetic effect: the verb آمد "came" applies to the following patient/figure آنگه پسر (then the son). Read naturally: "Then the son came up close to Giv." آنگه is a temporal adverb "then."
segments.s272-g152: This is a relative/appositive clause introduced by که‌ای (archaic/poetic combination of که + ای) meaning "who/that." نامبردار qualifies the following noun as "renowned/famed," and فرخ پدر names the father (Farrokh) — i.e., the son is being identified as the son of the famed Farrokh. Grammatically, this is an attributive relative: "the son who was of the renowned Farrokh (father)."
segments.s272-g305: بدو (a prepositional clitic = 'to him') comes before the verb phrase for emphasis; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' with the subject name گودرز placed after it (a common poetic word order: verb + subject). کو is an interrogative/poetic particle here functioning like 'which/what/that moment' or pointing to 'the time/fate' (used elliptically). را is the postposition often marking a definite/ topical element (here attaching to کو for emphasis), and زمان is the noun 'time/moment' that the speaker claims to control.
segments.s272-g306: به دست منست is an idiomatic phrase: به دست (literally 'in the hand') + منست (here functioning as 'is mine' or 'is in my power' in an archaic/poetic contraction) — together 'is in my power'. ای پسر is a direct vocative 'O son', and بی‌گمان is an adverbial qualifier 'undoubtedly'. Word order places the predicate ('is in my power') before the vocative and the emphatic adverb.
segments.s272-g307: که introduces a clause of result or intention ('that/so that' or 'which'), ز (poetic form of از) means 'from' or 'because of' and links to the following object (here indicating the source/target of vengeance). کین is an abstract noun 'vengeance/hatred', پور is 'son' (used collectively here), and گزین is the infinitive/stem 'to choose' (used as part of the speaker's declared action: 'I will choose'). Note the compact, elliptical ordering typical of epic Persian: objects and motives often precede the verb phrase.
segments.s272-g308: بخواهم is a first-person present-subjunctive/intentional form meaning 'I will/wish to' (expressing determination here). به زور is an instrumental phrase 'by the strength/might (of)', and جهان‌آفرین is a compound epithet for the Creator/God acting as the instrument of that might. The clause thus states the speaker's determined action and the means invoked.
segments.s272-g309: از آن پس is an adverbial temporal phrase 'after that / thereafter' (از + demonstrative آن + پس 'after'). به روی سپه is a directional phrase literally 'to the face of the army' meaning 'toward the host/army'. بنگرید is a past-tense poetic form of 'to look' (here used as a simple past or narrative perfect: 'he looked').
segments.s272-g310: سران را is the definite direct object 'the chiefs/leaders' marked by را. همه گونه is an intensifying expression meaning 'in every way / wholly / all manner', modifying پژمرده which here means 'pale/withered' (a physical state from suffering). دید is the past-tense 'he saw' and completes the clause: 'he saw the leaders all in every way pale.'
segments.s272-g311: ز رنج نبرد links two causes introduced by the poetic preposition ز ('from/because of'): رنج نبرد = 'the hardship/suffering of battle' (رنج 'suffering' + نبرد 'battle' as a genitival sequence). The second coordinated phrase ز خون ریختن uses ریختن ('to pour/ to shed') as a verbal noun meaning 'the shedding of blood'. The parallel structure lists causes for the leaders' condition.
segments.s272-g312: به هرجای is an adverbial 'to/at every place / wherever'; با دشمن is the prepositional phrase 'with/against the enemy'; آویختن is an infinitive/gerund meaning 'to cling to / to grapple with / to go at' — here describing the leaders' repeated physical engagement with the enemy across the field. The verb is nominalized/elliptical, completing the description begun in the previous segment.
segments.s272-g89: که is the relativizer 'that/who' linking this clause to the preceding line. پشت here means 'back/support/protector' (a figurative use: someone who is the backing of others). همه شهر توران is a genitival/attributive sequence 'all the cities of Turan' (word order: quantifier + noun + place name). بدوست is an older/poetic fused form: here it functions predicatively with implied 'است' (is), meaning 'is a friend/protector to him/it' or 'serves as the support of'.
segments.s272-g90: چو is the temporal 'when' (classical/poetic usage). روی تو is 'your face' (noun + possessive pronoun). بیند is the classical 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive of 'دیدن' used here in a temporal clause = 'when he sees'. بدردش پوست is a compact, slightly idiomatic phrase: بدرد (here causative/passive sense 'to rend/cause to split or ache') + -ش (his) + پوست (skin) — literally 'his skin is rent/warped' or 'his skin quivers/tears' as a physical reaction on seeing the addressee's face.
segments.s272-g91: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. دست‌یابی is a deverbal noun meaning 'attainment/access/ability to reach'. بر او marks the object/target 'upon/to him' (classical use of بر with دست‌یابی = 'to gain access to, to reach'). کار بود is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'would be feasible/possible' — here بود functions as the copula/auxiliary in a counterfactual conditional ('there would be a thing to do' = 'it could be done').
segments.s272-g92: This is a nominal predication with two coordinated allies: جهاندار 'the world-ruler/sovereign' and نیک اخترت 'your fortunate/auspicious star' (note اخترت is 'your star' — the -ت is the enclitic 2nd-person possessive). یار بود 'would be (your) ally' uses بود as auxiliary/copula in the conditional result clause; the possessive relation is implicit (i.e., 'would be your ally').
segments.s272-g93: بیاساید is a poetic form meaning 'to rest/be at ease' (here in a jussive/optative sense tied to the conditional/result). از + رنج و سختی marks the source/reason 'from pain and hardship'. سپاه is the subject 'the army/host'. Word order places the clause's verb-like element first (poetic inversion), but the grammatical relation is 'the army is relieved from suffering'.
segments.s272-g94: شود is the 3rd-person singular of شدن 'to become' used as a future/resultive auxiliary here: 'would become / will be'. شادمانه is an adverb/adjectival predicate 'joyfully/cheerful'. جهاندار و شاه are the coordinated subjects/title holders 'the sovereign and the king'. The clause states the effect: they would be joyful.
segments.s272-g95: شکسته is a past-participle/adjectival form 'broken'. شود is the auxiliary 'become/will be' (resultive). پشت افراسیاب is a genitival phrase 'the back of Afrasiab' (word order: noun + name). Together they mean 'the back of Afrasiab will be broken' — a poetic depiction of defeat/injury.
segments.s272-g96: پر از خون کند is a causative/result phrase 'makes (him) full of blood' where پر از خون = 'full of blood' and کند (from کردن) is the verb 'to make/do' giving 'makes his heart full of blood'. دل دو دیده پر آب: دل (heart) and دو دیده (literally 'two eyes' but here a compact phrase linking emotions and sight) with پر آب 'full of water' (idiomatically 'eyes full of tears'). The clause strings physical and emotional consequences: heart filled with blood (injury/anger), and both eyes filled with water (tears).
segments.s272-g33: به marks the indirect object 'to' (he said to someone). 'لهاک' here is a personal name (addressed recipient) rather than the dictionary senses given, so I mark it separately below. فرمود is the polite/past narrative verb 'he said/ordered'; it functions as the main reporting verb. تا introduces a purpose clause ('so that / in order that'), and سوی کوه is a prepositional phrase 'toward the mountain' giving the destination.
segments.s272-g34: برد here functions as a verb 'took/led' (poetic/archaic past of بردن) governing the object that follows. لشکر خویش را is the definite object: لشکر 'army', خوی + ش = خویش 'his own' (possessive formed by خوی + enclitic ش), and را is the Persian definite/object marker attaching to the object. همگروه modifies the action indicating accompaniment ('with his comrades' / 'together'). Word order is somewhat inverted in verse: verb precedes its object and the accompaniment phrase.
segments.s272-g35: همیدون is a personal name (here the agent of the motion). سوی رود فرشیدورد is a compound direction: سوی 'toward' + رود 'river' followed by the proper name فرشیدورد identifying that river (or a place-name). In poetry, place-name follows the common noun without additional particles.
segments.s272-g36: شود is a form of شدن used here as a narrative/modal auxiliary meaning roughly 'he went/was made to go' or 'so that he should' (common in epic diction). تا introduces the purpose/result clause 'so that / in order that'. برآرد is a present/future-oriented subjunctive-like form meaning 'bring/raise' or 'attain/manage' (here 'bring'). به خورشید گرد is a prepositional phrase where به 'to' + خورشید used poetically as 'the sun' or 'the sun's splendour' and گرد (lit. 'around') functions as 'circle'/'sphere' — together 'into the sun's circle' or 'into solar splendour.'
segments.s272-g37: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when' (common in narrative). آن names or points to 'those' (the people just mentioned). نامداران is plural adjective/noun 'renowned ones' (famous men). توران identifies their origin (Turan). سپاه follows as the noun 'host/army' — together: 'those renowned men of Turan's host'.
segments.s272-g38: گسستند is the past plural verb 'they broke/separated' (from a group); the stem idea is 'break' used as an intransitive verb here. زان = از + ان 'from that' (pointing back to the previously mentioned force). لشکر repeats 'host/army' and کینه‌خواه is an adjective/noun 'vengeful' or 'avenging' qualifying that host. The whole clause means they broke away from that vengeful host.
segments.s272-g39: نوندی (نوند) here is a technical/poetic term — likely a named musical signal or cry — rather than the dictionary senses given, so it is treated as a special signal/name. برافگند is a past tense verb 'threw up/sent forth' (برافکندن), and بر + دیده‌بان indicates the target 'upon/to the sentinel' (بر = upon/toward; دیده‌بان = sentry/watchman). The verb + بر + object pattern is typical for 'send/throw toward someone'.
segments.s272-g40: از آن دیده گه is a prepositional phrase 'from that watch-post' (از = from; آن دیده‌گه = that lookout; note دیده + گه form the compound 'dide-gah' = lookout). تا here introduces the reach/purpose/result sense 'so that / until', and در پهلوان 'into/unto the champion' (در = into/in front of; پهلوان = the hero) indicates the intended recipient or the direction of the signal. In epic diction these short particles link the loci of sentry, signal, and the hero succinctly.
segments.s272-g41: The phrase is a nominal group: نگهبان 'guard' followed by the possessor گودرز خود. Here خود after the name is a possessive/intensive marker meaning 'his own' or 'Goudarz himself'. با سپاه attaches as a prepositional phrase 'with the army' modifying نگهبان (i.e. who/with whom the guard was).
segments.s272-g42: همی داشت is a periphrastic past indicating continued action ('was keeping / kept'); نگه داشتن (here shortened to نگاه + داشت) means 'to keep watch'. هر سو 'every direction' is an adverbial phrase (هر = every, سو = side/direction). ز دشمن is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from/against the enemy', and نگاه is the object/phrase 'watch, gaze'.
segments.s272-g43: دو رویه is a poetic plural phrase indicating 'two ranks/columns/groups' (رویه here names two forces). چو is the simile/introducer 'like/namely' and links the following names as examples or identities: لهاک و فرشیدورد are presented as the two leaders/units. The construction is appositive: 'two columns, namely Lahak and Farshidvard.'
segments.s272-g44: ز راه کمین is a prepositional phrase 'from an ambush on the road' (ز = from, راه = road, کمین = ambush). برگشادند is the past plural verb 'they opened / burst forth' (classical past form), and گرد here functions adverbially 'around/about' describing the movement or spread.
segments.s272-g45: سواران ایران is a genitive phrase 'the horsemen of Iran' (سواران = riders/horsemen, ایران = Iran/Iranians). برآویختند is a past plural verb meaning 'they leapt upon / sprang to attack' (an abrupt, forceful action).
segments.s272-g46: همی at the start gives a continuous or emphatic coloring ('they did so / they then') and pairs with the past verb برآمیختند 'they mingled/combined'. خاک با خون is the coordinated noun phrase 'earth (dust) with blood' — i.e. dust and blood were mixed in the mêlée.
segments.s272-g47: نوندی is a named signal or musical/call term (here 'Navand' — a shout or sounding). برافگند is a past verb 'he/she/it cast forth / sent out' and هر سو دوان is an adverbial phrase 'running in every direction' (دوان = running/ rushing), describing the spread of the signal.
segments.s272-g48: به آگاه کردن is an infinitival/causative phrase 'for making aware / to alert' (به = to/for, آگاه = aware, کردن = 'to make/do' used as causative). بر پهلوان means 'to/toward the champion' (بر here = toward/to), so the whole phrase expresses purpose: the signal was sent out to inform/alert the hero.
segments.s272-g353: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' introducing origin. افراسیاب is the place/name of origin. آمد is the simple past 'came' (3rd sg.). آن بد خوی is the noun phrase 'that ill‑disposed one' (آن = demonstrative + بدخوی = disposition/adjective + noun); word order is somewhat inverted for poetic emphasis (prepositional phrase + verb + noun).
segments.s272-g354: همان here means 'that very / the same' and introduces a string of epithets. غارت and کشتن are nouns (plunder, killing); کشتن is the masdar/infinitive used as a noun 'killing'. بدگوی is an agentive compound meaning 'one who speaks ill'/'slanderer'. The items are coordinated with و.
segments.s272-g355: که is the relative/connector 'who/that' attaching this clause to the previous. در شهر ایران is the locative 'in the city of Iran' placed before the verb for emphasis. بگسترد is an older/poetic form of 'گسترد'/'گستراند' meaning 'spread' (the prefixed ب- is common in classical diction). کین (postverbal) is the object 'hatred/vengeance' — 'he spread hatred in the city.'
segments.s272-g356: بگشت here functions as 'turned/was turned / departed' (classical usage), with از marking the source ('from the path of...'). ره داد و آیین و دین is a coordinated noun phrase 'the way/path of justice, custom, and religion' (داد = justice; آیین = custom/ritual; دین = religion). Note classical word order and the poetic use of ب‑ verbs.
segments.s272-g357: سیاووش را is the definite object marked by را (Siyâvash = object). هم adds 'also'/'too'. به فرجام کار is a prepositional/time phrase 'at the end/result of the affair' (به + فرجام = 'at the end', کار = 'affair/matter'). The phrase sets the time/context for the following verb.
segments.s272-g358: بکشت is an archaic/poetic past form meaning 'he killed' (here used as the main past verb). و is the conjunction 'and'. برآورد functions as 'brought/caused' (classical usage 'to bring about, produce'); از ایران marks the affected party 'upon/from Iran'; دمار is the noun 'ruin/devastation' — 'he brought ruin on Iran.'
segments.s272-g359: وزان (و + از آن) is a compound poetic connector 'and from that / from that time on'. پس means 'after/then' (temporal). کجا is the interrogative 'where'. گیو is the proper name Giv. ز ایران 'from Iran' (ز = from). براند is a form of راندن 'to drive away/expel' (3rd sg.); the clause asks rhetorically where Giv, driven from Iran, would go/be driven to.
segments.s272-g360: چه مایه is an interrogative phrase 'with what means / by what resources'. به سختی is an adverbial phrase 'with hardship / severely'. به توران is the locative 'to/in Turan'. بماند (3rd sg.) is a present/subjunctive form 'would remain / could remain' used here in a rhetorical/questioning sense about possibility/endurance.
segments.s272-g265: چو is a temporal subordinating particle here meaning “when.” The phrase روی زمین (lit. “face ground”) places the noun phrase (روی) with its locative specification (زمین) before the verb; شد is the past-tense verb “became/turned.” به رنگ is the prepositional construction meaning “in the color of / turned to the color of,” with به linking رنگ (color) and the following color noun.
segments.s272-g266: برآمد is a past tense verb “arose/issued forth.” ز is the poetic preposition equivalent to از (“from”), and هر دو سپه = “each (i.e. both) armies/hosts” is an idiomatic plural expression. The instruments bough (بوق) and کوس follow in coordination (bough and drum) as what rose from the hosts.
segments.s272-g267: This noun cluster places ابر (“cloud”) before a locative/possessive phrase پشت پیلان (“at/behind the elephants”) and then a participial phrase تبیره زنان where زنان is a present-participle sense “striking/struck.” In poetic word order the participle follows what it modifies; overall the phrase describes a cloud at the elephants’ rear accompanied by the action of striking (i.e. drums being struck).
segments.s272-g268: از آن رزمگه is a prepositional phrase “from that battlefield,” with آن as the demonstrative determiner. بازگشت is a past-tense verb “returned/withdrew,” and آن زمان (“that time”) is an adverbial time expression placed after the verb; the whole clause reports the action in the past narrative.
segments.s272-g269: بر آن بر نهادند is an idiom: the repeated preposition بر plus آن (that) and the past verb نهادند (third-person plural) mean “they set themselves upon that / they resolved upon that.” هر دو سپاه (“each/both armies”) is the subject placed after the verb in this poetic line.
segments.s272-g270: که introduces the content clause (that …). شب is the temporal adverb “by night/at night.” بازگردند here is a non-past/subjunctive form used in subordinate reported clauses after a past main verb to indicate intended or future action (“they would return”). ز + آوردگاه is the prepositional phrase “from the battlefield.”
segments.s272-g271: گزینند is 3rd-person plural present (they choose/they select); in epic Persian present forms often describe a single completed action. شبگیر is a compound/noun used here as 'night-watchers' (agents chosen to work by night). مردان مرد is a stock epithet: مردان (plural 'men') followed by مرد (here intensive apposition) = 'men of men' = 'the bravest/most manly of men' (an emphatic, epic idiom). Word order stacks the role (شبگیر) with the epithet that qualifies who those night-watchers are.
segments.s272-g272: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. از ژرف دریا is a prepositional phrase 'from the deep sea' (ژرف = 'deep' modifies دریا). برآرند is 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive-form meaning 'they bring up / they raise / they fetch up' (بر- آر- + -ند). گرد here is ambiguous in Classical Persian/epic diction: grammatically it can function as an adverb ('around') or as a nominal object; in this context it most likely serves as the object/thing they are to bring up from the deep (a poetic/obscure noun), so it links as the verb's object.
segments.s272-g81: 'به بیژن چنین گفت کای شیرمرد' is ordered as: preposition + addressee, then 'thus said', then the vocative. 'به بیژن' = 'to Bijan'; 'چنین گفت' = 'said thus/so' (classical narration formula); 'کای' is an archaic vocative 'O' and 'شیرمرد' is a compound noun 'lion-man' = 'brave man/hero'. The verb 'گفت' is the narrator's past speech verb and the vocative that follows is the addressee of the quoted words.
segments.s272-g82: 'توی شیر درنده روز نبرد' is a verbless predicative phrase with an elided copula ('thou art ...' or 'thou standest ...'). 'توی' = 'in/among'; 'شیر درنده' = 'ferocious lions' (noun + postpositive adjective); 'روز نبرد' = 'the day of battle' ('روز' head + 'نبرد' as 'battle'). Together it means 'among ferocious lions on the day of battle' and supplies the content of the vocative utterance.
segments.s272-g83: 'کنون شیرمردی به کار آیدت' = 'Now a hero/champion will be of use to you.' 'کنون' = 'now'; 'شیرمردی' is indefinite ('a hero'); 'به کار آیدت' is an idiom where 'آید' (comes) plus clitic 'ت' means 'be useful/helpful to you' (literally 'come to work/aid you').
segments.s272-g84: 'که با دشمنان کارزار آیدت' is a relative clause modifying 'شیرمردی': 'who (که) will go into/enter battle (کارزار آیدت) with the enemies (با دشمنان) on your behalf.' 'که' = 'who/that'; word order places the preposition+object 'با دشمنان' before 'کارزار آیدت' (the action of entering battle).
segments.s272-g85: 'از ایدر برو تا به قلب سپاه' is an imperative direction: 'From here (از ایدر) go (برو) as far as/to (تا به) the heart/core of the army (قلب سپاه).' Note 'ایدر' is an archaic form of 'اینجا' (here) and 'قلب سپاه' = 'the army's core/heart' (possessive relation is implicit).
segments.s272-g86: 'ز پیران بدان جایگه کینه‌خواه' parses as an imperative instruction built with the prefix+verb 'ب' + 'دان' = 'بدان' ('know, learn'): 'ز پیران' = 'from the elders'; 'بدان' (here written split as 'ب'+'دان') = 'know/learn'; 'جایگه کینه‌خواه' = 'that place (is) of the avenger' or more naturally 'that the avenger is in that place.' In short: 'Learn from the elders that the avenger is there.'
segments.s272-g87: 'از ایشان نپرهیز و تن پیش‌دار' is two imperatives joined by 'و': 'از ایشان نپرهیز' = 'do not shrink back from them / do not fear them' (نپرهیز = negative imperative of 'پرهیز' = refrain/avoid), and 'تن پیش‌دار' = 'keep/put your body forward' i.e. 'advance yourself' or 'expose your person/in the front'. 'تن' = 'body/person' and 'پیش‌دار' functions as an instruction to keep it forward.
segments.s272-g88: 'که آمد گه کینه در کارزار' gives the reason: 'for the time/occasion of vengeance has come in the battlefield.' 'که' = 'because/for'; 'آمد' = 'has come' (narrative/present sense); 'گه' = 'occasion/time'; 'کینه' = 'vengeance/grudge'; 'در کارزار' = 'in the battle.' The noun phrase 'آمد گه' is an idiomatic way to say 'the time has come.'
segments.s272-g273: A noun phrase: همه (all/every) + نامداران (renowned ones) with the adjective پرخاشجوی (quarrelsome) following the noun, as is common in Persian poetry where attributive adjectives often come after the noun without an explicit ezāfe. Together it means 'all the famed, quarrelsome men.'
segments.s272-g274: یکایک (one by one) is an adverb modifying the verb phrase. به روی اندر آرند روی is poetic word order: آرند is a classical 3rd-person plural form of آوردن/آوردن meaning 'bring/turn' (here 'turned' or 'brought forward'); به روی (towards the face/front) and the repeated روی emphasize 'their faces forward.' اندر is a locative/adverbial particle ('in/into') that is common in older Persian; the whole phrase reads 'each one turned his face forward.'
segments.s272-g275: This is a poetic, inverted clause: ز پیکار (from battle) + یابد (3rd-person singular 'finds' or 'attains') + رهایی (release/freedom) + سپاه (the army). The subject سپاه comes after the verb for metrical reasons; syntactically it means 'the army finds release from battle.'
segments.s272-g276: نریزند is a negative present/optative 3rd-person plural form of ریختن ('to pour/ to shed') with the negative prefix ن-. Here it means 'they do not shed' or 'they do not spill.' خون سر بیگناه is the direct object 'the blood of the innocent' (خون = blood, سر = 'head' used idiomatically/poetically with خون, بیگناه = innocent). The verb agrees with سپاه earlier, 'the army does not shed the blood of the innocent.'
segments.s272-g277: بکردند is an archaic/perfective-looking form meaning 'they did' or 'they made' (بـ- prefix + کردَند). پیمان is 'a pact' (object). و گشتند باز is 'and they returned' where گشتند (they turned/they became) with باز ('back/again') forms the compound 'باز گشتند' = 'they went back/withdrew.' The clause reads 'they made a pact and withdrew.'
segments.s272-g278: گرفتند is 'they took/seized' but here functions causatively as 'they made (it)...' followed by adjectives: کوتاه رزم دراز literally 'short [the] long battle' — Persian allows an adjective (کوتاه) placed before the noun (رزم) while دراز ('long') characterizes that battle; the sense is 'they shortened the long battle' or 'they made the long fight short.'
segments.s272-g279: دو سالار هر دو is an emphatic doubling: 'two leaders — each of the two' (هر دو = both). ز کینه (from hatred) and به درد (into/with pain) are two short prepositional/causal phrases giving motive: ز + noun = 'from/through' and به + noun = 'to/with' — here indicating 'through hatred and with sorrow.' This whole segment gives the reason why the leaders acted.
segments.s272-g280: همی is an archaic emphatic/adverbial particle used before verbs in epic Persian (here sense 'indeed/just' or marking the action). روی برگاشتند is 'turned their faces back/away' (برگاشتند = they turned back/returned), and از نبرد 'from the battle' is the source phrase. Overall: 'they straightaway/indeed turned away from the battle.'
segments.s272-g577: نشستند is the 3rd-person-plural past of نشستن (‘to sit’/‘mount’), here giving the main action ‘they mounted/sat’. بر introduces the location (on/upon) and takes زین ‘saddle’ as its object. سپیده دمان is a poetic compound meaning ‘the break/edge of dawn’ (سپیده = dawn, دمان = skirt/edge), so the whole phrase means ‘they mounted on the saddles at dawn’.
segments.s272-g578: همه (‘all/everyone’) modifies نامداران (‘the famed/renowned ones’). به here functions as a locative/relational preposition (‘on/at’), linking کمان ‘bow’ to بازو ‘arm’ — literally ‘a bow on the arm’. The word order is poetic but means ‘all the renowned men, (with) a bow on the arm’.
segments.s272-g579: که is the subordinating conjunction ‘that’ (introducing reported speech). از marks source/agentive cause ‘from/of’ and links نعل اسبان ‘the horses’ horseshoes’ as the originating sound/occasion. تو is the 2nd-person pronoun ‘you’, and گفتی is the verb ‘you said’ (2nd-person singular past) introducing the reported clause; زمین (‘the earth/ground’) is the subject of the following verb (it will be the thing that ‘wears’ the cloak).
segments.s272-g580: بپوشد is the present/subjunctive form of پوشیدن with the prefix ب- used here in reported clause sense (‘should/would cover/wear’); it expresses what the earth is said to do. همی is an emphatic/continuous particle (poetic) meaning roughly ‘indeed’/‘ever’. چادر آهنین is a metaphorical object: چادر here ‘cloak/cover’ and آهنین ‘iron(ly)’ — ‘an iron cloak/covering’ that the earth will don.
segments.s272-g581: سپهبد is the title/name of the commander (spahbed). به introduces the addressees (‘to/unto’) and links the two names that follow as indirect objects/recipients of the speech. لهاک and فرشیدورد are proper names here (personal names), not the common nouns listed in the sense array, so they should be read as names.
segments.s272-g582: چنین is an adverb meaning ‘thus/so’ and modifies گفت (‘said’). گفت here is the past-tense verb ‘he said’ (the provided lemma senses list does not give the verbal sense, so that is marked -1). کای is a vocative particle ‘O!’ addressing someone; نامداران ‘the renowned ones’ follows as the addressee, and مرد here functions as ‘men/heroes’ (a heroic appellation). The clause is the spahbed’s vocative address: ‘Thus he said: “O renowned men...”’.
segments.s272-g583: شما را uses شما (‘you’, plural/polite) plus the accusative/definite marker را; here the phrase functions as the logical topic/target of the predicate. نگهبان is a predicative noun ‘guardian/guard’, and توران سپاه is a genitive noun phrase ‘Turan’s army/host’ (noun-noun sequence meaning ‘the army of Turan’). Together: ‘you (as) the guardians of Turan’s army’.
