segments.s272-g584: The sequence همی بود باید is a poetic/modal ordering: همی بود is past/continuous ‘there was / it was’ and باید is the modal ‘must/ought to’; together they convey obligation/necessity in a past/setting sense (‘you should be / were to be’). بدین is the fused preposition + demonstrative (ب- + این) meaning ‘in/at this’, and رزمگاه is the locative noun ‘battlefield’. So the clause means ‘(you) ought to be here at this battlefield as guardians’.
segments.s272-g585: یکى (a certain/one) introduces an indefinite singular; دیده‌بان is the nominal head meaning 'watchman/sentinel'. بر سر کوهسار is a locative phrase (preposition بر + noun سر 'summit' + modifier کوهسار 'mountain') meaning 'upon the mountain's summit'. Word order is noun phrase + prepositional/location phrase.
segments.s272-g586: This is a nominal apposition describing the sentinel: نگهبان روز 'keeper/guard of the day' and ستاره‌شمار 'star-counter/reader'. روز and ستاره‌شمار are coordinated by و; the second element is a compound noun meaning one who counts/observes the stars.
segments.s272-g587: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایدونک is an archaic/poetic particle meaning roughly 'now' / 'at this moment'. ما را uses the object marker را after the pronoun to mark the affected party ('upon us' / 'to us'). ز گردان سپهر is a prepositional phrase: ز 'from' + گردان 'revolving/turning' modifying سپهر 'sky/heaven' (a poetic image of the turning heavens).
segments.s272-g588: بد آید is literally 'ill/unfavorable comes' (آید = 'comes' / 'befalls'); in poetic register this introduces the misfortune as subject. ببرد is a 3rd-person singular verbal form 'carries/takes away' (subsequent action by that ill fate). ز ما marks the source/thing affected 'from us'. پاک مهر is the direct object 'pure affection/loyalty' (پاک qualifying مهر). The two verbs refer to the same impersonal/abstract subject (an ill omen).
segments.s272-g589: شما is the addressed subject 'you (pl.)'. جنگ را puts 'war' in focus with the object marker را. کس here functions as an indefinite/comparative 'no one' (implying 'no one more than you'). متازید is present/past-form meaning 'you rush/charge', and زود is an adverb 'quickly/soon' placed after the verb to indicate the comparative sense 'sooner / more quickly'. The clause implies a comparison ('no one rushes to war sooner than you').
segments.s272-g590: به توران شتابید is a past/action clause 'you hastened to Turan' (به + proper place name + verb شتابید 'hurried'). بر سان دود is a simile: بر + سان = 'upon/as' + دود 'smoke', so 'like smoke' (moving swiftly as a column/blast of smoke). The prepositionaliser بر + سان forms the poetic comparative 'like'.
segments.s272-g591: کز is a poetic preposition/conjunction here meaning 'from' / 'since' (introducing origin). این تخمه ویسگان is a genitive/compound: این 'this' + تخمه 'seed/offspring' referring to the lineage, modified by ویسگان 'of Vis' (a proper/tribal name). کس نماند means 'no one remained' (کس used negatively here). The clause says that from that family/line no person remained.
segments.s272-g592: همه کشته شد is a succinct past passive/result clause 'all were killed' (کشته as past participle/ adjective + شد as auxiliary). جز شما 'except you' introduces the lone exception, and بس نماند is an idiomatic elliptical phrase meaning 'none remained' or 'there remained nothing/none' (بس 'enough/any' with negated نماند). Together: 'All were slain; none remained except you.'
segments.s272-g137: بجوشید and بگشاد are two past actions describing what happened to the lips: بجوشید is a past/perfect verb meaning “gushed/erupted,” and بگشاد is a past transitive/causative form “opened.” لب را uses the object marker را to mark “the lip(s)” as the object of بگشاد even though poetically the same noun is undergoing both actions; ز بند is a prepositional phrase “from the bond/restraint.” Word order (verb – verb – object – prepositional phrase) is typical of classical Persian poetic compression.
segments.s272-g138: به introduces the agent/instrument or cause here (“by/with”), so به نفرین means “by/with the curse.” دژخیم دیو نژند is a chain of epithets modifying دیو: دژخیم (an oppressive/cruel quality) and نژند (base/worthless). The whole phrase gives the cause: “by the curse of the cruel, base demon.”
segments.s272-g139: بیفگند is a past verb ‘he cast/throw away’ (بی- here part of the verbal form); it takes نیزه as its direct object. The second clause کمان برگرفت is a coordinated past action: برگرفت (past of گرفتن) “took up/raised” with کمان as its object. The two short clauses narrate consecutive actions.
segments.s272-g140: یکی here functions as the numeral/indefinite “one/a single.” درقه کرگ is a compound noun phrase “a scrap/piece (درقه) of down (کرگ).” بر سر گرفت is a common verb phrase: بر (on/upon) + سر (head) + گرفت (took/placed) meaning “he put/took it upon his head.” The verb گرفت is the simple past of گرفتن used transitively.
segments.s272-g141: کمان را uses را to mark the bow as definite object. به زه کرد literally “made/with the string,” here meaning “he strung the bow (with the bowstring)” — کرد (to do/make) used to mean ‘to equip/affix.’ و links the next action; بگشاد is a past-form “drew/opened” and بر is the preposition “against/on/at,” indicating direction or application of that action.
segments.s272-g142: که introduces a result/purpose-like clause (“so that/as if”). با دست پیران is an instrumental phrase “with the hands of the elders/old men”; بدوزد is the present/subjunctive form of دوخت used here to express potential/resultive action “would/should sew,” and سپر is the object “shield.” The clause therefore reads roughly “(as if) to sew the shield with the hands of the elders,” a figurative way of saying the bow was drawn so strongly/skillfully.
segments.s272-g143: بزد is the simple past “he struck/hit.” بر سرش is the prepositional phrase “upon his head” (بر + سر + pronominal clitic ش = his). چارچوبه خدنگ is a noun phrase where چارچوبه (frame/haft) specifies خدنگ (dagger) — here meaning something like “the dagger’s haft/hilt” with which he struck.
segments.s272-g144: نبد is a negative/existential past form meaning “was not / there was not / did not (occur).” کارگر modifies تیر and here means “effective/operative,” so نبد کارگر تیر means “there was no effective arrow” or “the arrow had no effect.” بر کوه سنگ is the locative/directional phrase “against/on the stone mountain.” The whole clause says that the arrow accomplished nothing against the rocky mountain.
segments.s272-g9: This clause is a poetic simile with an omitted copula: جهان (subject) + چون شب تیره introduces 'like a dark night'. از تیره introduces a comparative/genitive nuance ('darker than / from the darkness of'), and میغ (blade) supplies the image contrasted with 'darkness'. There is no finite verb; the participial/nominal structure and 'از' give the sense '(was) darker than the darkness of the blade.'
segments.s272-g10: چو introduces another simile ('as/like'), ابری is the noun 'a cloud' and که begins a relative clause 'which/that...'. باران او literally 'its rain' (او is a poetic pronoun used for 'it' = the cloud). The predicate is elliptical: 'its rain (was) تیر و تیغ' — 'arrows and swords' — so the clause means 'a cloud whose rain was arrows and swords.'
segments.s272-g11: آهنین کرده is a past-participial/resultative phrase ('made iron' / 'turned to iron'), with اسبان as the agent; word order is poetic (no explicit auxiliary). به نعل is an instrumental phrase 'by (means of) horseshoes' (به here = 'by/with'). The literal structure reads 'the earth (was) made iron by the horses with horseshoes.'
segments.s272-g12: بر او = 'upon it' (referring back to the earth). دست‌گردان is an epithet ('the roving‑handed' / fighters whose hands move), followed by به خون گشته لعل where گشته is the perfect participle 'have become/turned' and لعل is a metaphor 'ruby' (i.e. blood‑red). The clause means 'upon it the roving‑handed had their hands turned ruby with blood.'
segments.s272-g13: ز بس (literally 'from so much/so many') is a quantitative/causal preface meaning 'so many' or 'because there were so many'. خسته ترک is the noun phrase 'exhausted Turks' and اندر آن رزمگاه gives the location 'in that battlefield'. This whole fronted phrase sets up the quantity/context for the following result clause.
segments.s272-g14: بریده modifies سرانشان to give 'their severed heads' (بریده = 'cut off'). فگنده is a past participle meaning 'thrown/scattered' used predicatively, and به راه marks location 'onto/the road'. The verb 'were' is omitted (poetic); the sense is 'their severed heads lay/threw about on the road.'
segments.s272-g15: بر آوردگه is a locative prepositional phrase 'on/at the place of arrival/assembly'. جای گشتن 'place of going/turning' is an accusative/nominal phrase meaning 'space to move or turn', and نماند is the negative existential 'there remained not' — together, 'there was no space left to move/turn.'
segments.s272-g16: پی اسب را is a phrase indicating 'for the horse' / 'in the wake/passage of the horse' with را marking definiteness/emphasis. برگذشتن is the infinitive 'to pass by', and نماند again is 'there remained not' — so 'there was no passing left for the horse' (i.e. no way for horses to get through).
segments.s272-g321: به سالار is the indirect object phrase 'to/for the commander' (به + noun). بر here is a directional/covering particle ('toward/on/at') attached before the verb phrase; خواندند is past‑tense 3rd‑person plural 'they called/ shouted', used here to report an acclamation. آفرین is the exclamation they shouted ('bravo'/'acclaim'). Word order is object (به سالار) + verb phrase (بر خواندند آفرین), a common poetic inversion.
segments.s272-g322: که‌ای is a vocative/intensifying form used to address someone ('O ...'); it functions to call attention before the epithet. نامور is an adjective 'renowned', followed by پهلوان 'champion/hero' and زمین 'earth/land' forming the noun phrase 'renowned champion of the earth'. The order is typical poetic apposition: vocative + adjectival phrase + head noun + genitive.
segments.s272-g323: شبت خواب is literally 'your night (your night’s) sleep' (possessive + noun). چون here is the interrogative 'how', and بود is the past tense 'was' — together forming 'how was your sleep'. و links to the next clause; the second چون + خاستی is a parallel question 'and how did you rise', where خاستی is 2nd‑person past 'you rose/get up'.
segments.s272-g324: ز پیکار ترکان is a prepositional phrase 'from/for the battle of the Turks' (ز = 'from/with/regarding' in poetic use + پیکار 'battle' + ترکان 'the Turks'). چه is the interrogative 'how/what way' and آراستی is 2nd‑person past 'you prepared/arrayed/armed (yourself)', so the clause asks how he made himself ready for the Turks' fight.
segments.s272-g325: بدیشان ('to them') marks the indirect object/recipients; چنین 'thus' introduces the manner of speaking. گفت is the past 3rd‑person singular verb 'he said' (here the data's listed senses do not include the verbal sense, so the appropriate verbal sense is indicated). پس 'then/after that' gives temporal sequence, and پهلوان 'the champion' appears at the end by poetic inversion: literally 'to them thus said then the champion.'
segments.s272-g326: که‌ای is the vocative 'O' addressing the listeners. نیک‌مردان is the plural noun 'virtuous/noble men'. و is the conjunction 'and'. فرخ گوان is a compound epithet: فرخ 'fortunate/blessed' plus گوان (a deverbal/derivative element meaning 'those like/ones'), giving 'the fortunate ones' or 'happy fellows'. The whole is an address: 'O virtuous men and fortunate ones.'
segments.s272-g327: سزدگر is a conditional/optative particle 'if it be fitting' (literally 'fitting‑if'); it introduces what should be done if appropriate. شما is the 2nd‑person plural/formal 'you'. بر before جهان‌آفرین marks direction 'to/toward the Creator' (جهان‌آفرین = 'world‑creator' = the Creator). Thus the clause sets the condition and object: 'If it befit you to the Creator...'.
segments.s272-g328: بخوانید is a 2nd‑person plural imperative/subjunctive 'you should recite/call' (here 'recite' or 'call aloud'). روز و شبان is the poetic phrase 'day and night' (روز = day; شبان here functions as the poetic counterpart 'nights/ night‑times'), and آفرین is the thing to be called/recited — the acclamation 'Afarin'. The verb governs the object phrase: 'Recite/call
segments.s272-g217: بدیشان is a contracted prepositional phrase (به ایشان) meaning 'to them' and functions as the indirect object. نهاد is the past tense of نهادن 'to put/lay/send' (3rd sg.) and here means 'cast/sent'. از دو رویه is a prepositional phrase 'from/taking two faces/sides' (poetic use of رویه = 'face/side'), and نهیب is the direct object 'a thunderous cry/rebuke' that was sent.
segments.s272-g218: نیامد is the past negative 'did not come' (3rd sg.); یکی را uses یکی as 'one (person)' with را marking the affected/definite direct object; سر اندر نشیب is a prepositional/locative phrase 'head into the descent' placed after the verb for poetic word-order — literal: 'one's head did not come into the hollow/decline.'
segments.s272-g219: به دل گفت is an idiom 'he said to his heart' = 'he thought/said to himself'; گفت is the past verb 'said' with an implicit subject. کاری نو آمد به روی is literal 'a new action/work came upon (the) face' — here 'به روی' functions as 'upon' or 'before' and anticipates the object which appears in the next segment.
segments.s272-g220: مرا ز این دلیران پرخاشجوی supplies the object and source: مرا (me) + را (object marker) marks the person affected by 'آمد' in the previous segment; ز = 'from' indicates the source, and این دلیران پرخاشجوی = 'these brave, quarrelsome ones' (دلیران modified by پرخاشجوی).
segments.s272-g221: نه negates the clause that follows: 'not from the city of the Turks did leaders come.' از شهر ترکان is a prepositional phrase 'from the city of the Turks'; سران is the plural subject 'the chiefs/leaders'; آمدند is the plural past 'came.' Word order is topical/poetic but follows Persian SOV patterns with the verb final.
segments.s272-g222: که introduces the contrasting clause ('but/that'); دیوان مازندران is the noun phrase 'the dīvān/dīvs of Mazandaran' (here 'dīvān' refers to the supernatural/monstrous beings in the Shahnameh context), and آمدند repeats the past plural 'came.'
segments.s272-g223: سوی راست = 'to the right' (directional phrase). گیو is the proper name 'Giv' (subject). اندر آمد is the past 'entered/came in' (اندر is a poetic preposition 'into/in'). چو گرد = 'as/like a circle/turning' (چو + گرد sets up the simile that continues in the next segment).
segments.s272-g224: گرازه is a simile 'a boar/young boar' (the image used to describe his motion); به پرخاش here means 'with an assault/attack' or 'in aggressive charge'; فرشیدورد is the proper name 'Farshidvard' and is the target toward which the boar-like movement is directed. The whole segment completes the simile introduced by چو گرد.
segments.s272-g409: شما (you, plural/formal) is the addressee; باید is a modal verb meaning 'must/ought to'. که introduces a subordinate clause giving what is required. هم ز این نشان is an adverbial phrase: هم 'also/even', ز (poetic z) = 'from/because of', این نشان 'this sign/mark' referring back to the preceding context.
segments.s272-g410: ابا here functions as a verb meaning 'to refuse/avoid' (classical usage), taking as its objects 'نیزه و تیغ' (spear and sword). مردم کشان is a compound: مردم 'people' + کشان (participle/suffix) forming 'people‑slaying' or 'that slay people'. The whole segment says to avoid the man‑slaying spear and sword.
segments.s272-g411: به کینه is an instrumental/causal prepositional phrase: به + کینه 'with hatred/vengeance'. ببندید is 2nd person plural imperative/subjunctive of بستن 'to bind' (here 'gird'); یکسر 'utterly/at once' modifies the action; کمر 'waist/loins' is the direct object and here idiomatically means 'gird your loins/prepare yourself'.
segments.s272-g412: هر آن کس که هست از شما نامور is a relative clause 'every one who is (هست) of you (از شما) and is نامور (renowned)'. هر آن کس que (that) + relative که links back to 'every one'; the predicate 'نامور' qualifies that person. Word order is slightly marked (poetic) but means 'who among you is famous'.
segments.s272-g413: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause. دولت 'fortune/sovereignty' is the theme; گرفتست is a perfective/past form meaning 'has been taken/seized' or 'has suffered', and از ایشان indicates source/possessors 'from them'. نشیب (descent/decline) comments that fortune has taken a downward turn — 'fortune has fallen from them.'
segments.s272-g414: کنون (now) is temporal; the verb and modal are inverted for poetic effect: کرد (to do/act) + باید (must) together mean 'now it is necessary to do' — i.e. 'you must now do X'. به کین 'with vengeance' (prepositional phrase) and بر نهیب 'strike/attack with a thunderous cry' (بر here 'against/upon', نهیب a loud rebuke or shout). The construction places the obligation (باید) after کرد for emphasis.
segments.s272-g415: به توران is locative ('in/among Turan'); چو is a simile particle meaning 'like/as'; هومان is the proper name Houman; سواری 'rider/horseman'; نبود is past negative 'was not/there was no'. Poetic word order places the simile and noun before the copula: 'In Turan there was no horseman like Houman.'
segments.s272-g416: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'سواری' ('who'). با 'with' marks the opposed/paired fighters; بیژن and گیو are the names Bijan and Giv; رزم آزمود is a two‑word verbal phrase where آزمود (past of آزمودن) means 'tried/tested' and with رزم ('battle') yields 'tested battle' → 'engaged/fought in battle'.
segments.s272-g561: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). سر بپیچد is a poetic ordering where سر ("head") is the theme and بپیچد is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive form of پیچیدن meaning 'turn (away)'; note the subject کس follows the verb (inversion common in poetry). از گفت من is a prepositional phrase 'from my saying / from my words', modifying 'turn away'.
segments.s272-g562: بفرمایمش is a verb with the object-clitic -ش ('him') attached; in classical/poetic usage بفرمایم can mean 'I order' or 'I will say (to do)' — here it functions as 'I will command'. سر بریدن ز تن is an infinitival nominal clause 'to cut the head from the body' (سر = head, بریدن = to sever/behead, ز = 'from' in poetic register, تن = body) and serves as the content/object of the command.
segments.s272-g563: گرفتند is simple past 3rd-person-plural 'they took/they seized' and here means 'they took up/deployed'. گردان is the direct object 'troop(s)/division'. به پاسخ شتاب is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'in a hurried reply' (به = 'in/with', پاسخ = 'reply/answer', شتاب = 'haste'), modifying گرفتند.
segments.s272-g564: که here introduces the reported speech/utterance ('that' / 'saying:'). ای پهلوان is a vocative ('O hero/champion'). رد افراسیاب is a noun phrase 'the passage/passing of Afrasiab' (رد = passage/allowing to pass, افراسیاب = name) — the clause is elliptical and functions as what they said to the champion.
segments.s272-g565: تو is the vocative/2nd-person pronoun 'you'. از دیرگه باز is a poetic order meaning 'back/from the old abode' (از = 'from', دیرگه = 'old abode/tomb', باز = 'back/again' placed after the noun). با گنج خویش = 'with your treasure'; خویش is formed by خوی + the enclitic ش (here written separately), giving the reflexive/possessive 'your own'.
segments.s272-g566: گزیدستی is past 2nd-person 'you chose' (گزیدن). از بهر is an idiomatic compound 'for the sake of' (از + بهر). ما is 'us'. رنج خویش = 'your own hardship/suffering' (رنج = suffering, خویش formed by خوی + -ش again), so the clause means 'you chose your suffering for our sake'.
segments.s272-g567: میان بسته is a compound meaning 'closed the middle/way' (میان = 'middle/way', بسته = 'closed'); بر پیش ما = 'in front of us / before us' (بر = 'on/upon/toward', پیش ما = 'before us/in our presence'). چون رهی is a simile/adverbial 'as/like a wayfarer' (چون = 'like/as', رهی = 'wayfarer' or 'one on the road'), giving a sense of acting like a traveler while blocking the path.
segments.s272-g568: This clause lacks an explicit subject (you is understood). پسر = 'son' as the patient/affected person; با برادر literally 'with/by (his) brother' marks the agent here ('by his brother'). به کشتن دهی is a periphrastic causative: به + verbal noun (کشتن = 'killing') + دهی (2nd-person present/subjunctive of دادن) meaning 'you cause/allow (him) to be killed' — دهی here = 'cause/inflict'.
segments.s272-g113: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning “when.” رویین is an adjective placed before پیران, literally “iron-hard” = “mail-clad” modifying پیران (“elders”); ز is the poetic preposition equivalent to از meaning “from,” and پشت سپاه means “the rear/behind of the army.” The whole segment sets the time and subject for the verb that follows.
segments.s272-g114: بدید is a past tense verb “(he) saw.” آن is the demonstrative “that” pointing to the following nouns. تکاپوی is a noun here meaning “bustle/commotion,” و is the coordinating conjunction “and,” and گرد سیاه is a noun phrase “black dust/cloud” with گرد used as “dust” and سیاه as the color adjective after it.
segments.s272-g115: بیامد is the past tense verb “(he) came.” به here marks motion/toward (“to/towards the rear”), پشت سپاه بزرگ is the locative phrase “the rear of the great host,” with بزرگ modifying سپاه. Word order (verb — prepositional phrase) is common in classical Persian narrative.
segments.s272-g116: ابا is used here in an adjectival/participial sense derived from the verb ‘to refuse / be reluctant,’ so ابا نامداران functions adverbially or as a reduced clause meaning “shunning / spurning the renowned ones.” به کردار گرگ is a simile “in the manner/behavior of a wolf,” where به + noun-phrase indicates manner.
segments.s272-g117: برآویخت is a past tense verb meaning “(he) sprang up / leapt upon.” بر + سان introduces a simile: بر سان = “upon / like,” سان meaning “like/as.” شرزه modifies پلنگ (an epithet: “rebellious/impetuous leopard”), so the clause describes a sudden, leopard-like attack.
segments.s272-g118: بکوشید is past tense “(he) tried / strove.” و هم links and adds emphasis (“and yet / even so”). بر نیامد is an idiomatic negative result: نیامد “did not come” with بر giving a sense of ‘avail / succeed against,’ so بر نیامد به جنگ means “it did not avail/it did not turn out (in the fight).” به جنگ provides the context “in battle / in the fight.”
segments.s272-g119: بیفگند is past tense “(he) threw/cast.” شمشیر هندی is the object “Indian sword” (هندی qualifying شمشیر). ز مشت uses the poetic preposition ز (= از) with مشت “fist,” so ز مشت indicates the origin/hold — “from his fist.”
segments.s272-g120: به نومیدی از جنگ is an adverbial phrase “in despair at/from the battle,” with به marking the state and از جنگ specifying the cause. بنمود is a past-tense verb “(he) showed / displayed,” and پشت here means “back” (showing his back = turning away / fleeing). The clause therefore describes his despairing flight.
segments.s272-g153: من (I) is the first-person subject pronoun. ایدون is an archaic/adverbial form here introducing the report (‘just/recently’); it is followed by شنیدستم, an archaic perfect/past verbal form meaning “I heard / I was told.” از + شهریار marks the source of the report: از = 'from' and شهریار = 'the king.' Word order is subject–adverb–verb–prepositional phrase (typical in poetic/classical Persian).
segments.s272-g154: که introduces a subordinate clause (‘that’). پیران here names the man Pīrān (written like a common noun in older Persian); فراوان functions adverbially (‘often/much’) qualifying the verb کند. کند is 3rd person singular present tense (classical poetic form) meaning ‘does/makes’ — here ‘wages’ — and کارزار is the object ‘battle.’ The whole clause = ‘that Pīrān often wages battle.’
segments.s272-g155: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from.’ چنگ means ‘claw’ (not the instrument here) and takes بسی/following modifiers: بسی is an intensifier in older Persian (‘very’ / ‘much’) and تیزچنگ is a compound adjective ‘sharp‑clawed’ (literally ‘sharp‑claw’). اژدها names the creature ‘dragon.’ Together: ‘from the dragon’s very sharp claw(s).’
segments.s272-g156: مر is an archaic pronominal/ethical particle here marking the beneficiary (roughly ‘for him’ / ‘to him’ in classical usage). او is the third‑person pronoun ‘he’; را is the definite object/contrast marker attached after او (classical word order may place را after a pronoun). بود is the past copula ‘was’; روز سختی = ‘a day of hardship’ (روز = day, سختی = hardship/severity). رها is a past participle/adjective ‘freed’ or ‘released.’ The clause thus says roughly ‘for him there was a day of hardship — (and) [he] was freed.’
segments.s272-g157: سرانجام is an adverb meaning ‘finally’ or ‘in the end.’ بر is the preposition ‘on/over/to,’ here introducing the phrase بر دست گودرز = ‘upon Gōdarz’s hand’ (an idiomatic locution meaning roughly ‘to Gōdarz’ or ‘in Gōdarz’s possession’). هوش is the noun ‘awareness/consciousness.’ Together they prepare the subject for the following verb: ‘finally, consciousness (returned) to Gōdarz.’
segments.s272-g158: برآید is a 3rd‑person present/subjunctive form meaning ‘arises/returns’ and here completes the clause ‘هوش برآید’ = ‘consciousness returned.’ تو‑ای is the poetic vocative ‘you, O…’; باب is a vocative appellation here (an archaic address; see translation note) and چندین is an adverb ‘again and again / many times.’ مَکوش is a prohibitive imperative ‘do not try’ (negative command). So the second half addresses someone directly: ‘You — O X — do not try repeatedly.’
segments.s272-g159: پس is the temporal/conclusive particle ‘then.’ اندر is a locative/prepositional adverb ‘in/among/into’ used here with رسیدند. رسیدند is 3rd‑person plural past tense ‘they arrived.’ یاران گیو = ‘Gīv’s companions’ (possessive/genitive relation: noun followed by owner). The clause reads: ‘Then Gīv’s companions arrived.’
segments.s272-g160: پر از is the adjectival phrase ‘full of’ (پر = full, از = ‘of’ here). خشم و کینه = ‘anger and hatred’ (two coordinated nouns joined by و). سواران نیو = ‘the horsemen of Niyū’ (a genitive/attributive phrase identifying which horsemen). The whole segment modifies the arriving companions: ‘(they) — the horsemen of Niyū — full of anger and hatred.’
segments.s272-g393: از آن نامداران برآریم گرد: برآریم is an optative/hortative first-person-plural form ('let us bring/raise'); it follows the prepositional phrase "از آن نامداران" ('from/those renowned ones'). گرد is a poetic/archaic noun meaning a band or host (here used after the verb to name what is raised). Word order is Verb after the source-phrase: 'from those famed men let us raise a host.'
segments.s272-g394: بدانگه که سازد همی او نبرد: بدانگه ('thereupon') introduces the purpose/result clause; که is the subordinating 'that/so that'; سازد is 3rd-person singular present ('he makes/causes'); همی is the classical continuous/aspectual particle (here marking the ongoing or emphatic aspect of سازد); او is the third-person pronoun; نبرد is the object 'battle/war'. The clause reads 'thereupon that he may wage battle.'
segments.s272-g395: ور ایدونک پیران از این رای خویش: ور is the conditional 'if'; ایدونک is an archaic/emphatic particle (here marking contingency or attention); پیران is the subject 'the elders'; از این رای خویش = 'from this counsel of theirs' (رای = counsel/plan; خویش + ش is the possessive 'their/own'). The whole phrase means 'if the elders, then, do not depart from this counsel of theirs...' (negation appears in the next segment).
segments.s272-g396: نگردد نهد رزم را پای پیش: نگردد is negative-present/subjunctive (here 'does not change/turn'); نهد is the verb 'to place/set' (3rd-person present: 'he/they put'); رزم را is the definite object 'the battle'; پای پیش literally 'foot forward' = 'set foot forward' or 'advance into battle'. Together: '...they will not put a foot forward into the battle.'
segments.s272-g397: پذیرفتم اندر شما سر به سر: پذیرفتم is a past tense first-person singular verb 'I accepted' or 'I agreed'; اندر شما indicates the social locus 'among you'; سر به سر is an idiomatic reduplication meaning 'one and all' / 'all of you' or 'completely/one by one' — here it emphasizes that the speaker accepts unanimously or entirely among them.
segments.s272-g398: که من پیش بندم بدین کین کمر: که introduces the content clause 'that...'; من is the subject 'I'; پیش بندم is a present/subjunctive verb phrase 'I will bind/gird before (you)' (بندم ← بستن 'to bind/gird'); بدین here is the classical form 'with this' (به + این in older spelling/compounding) and introduces the instrument; کین کمر = 'a belt (کمر) of vengeance/hatred' where کین modifies کمر. The clause means 'that I will gird (my loins) with this vengeful belt.'
segments.s272-g399: ابا پیر سر من بدین رزمگاه: ابا here functions as the older/poetic form of با (with) (the initial alef is prosthetic in poetry); پیر سر من = 'with the elder at my head' (پیر = elder/leader, سر من = 'my head' meaning 'leading me' or 'at my head'); بدین رزمگاه = 'to/into this battlefield' (بدین = 'to/with this', رزمگاه = 'battlefield'). The phrase states accompaniment and destination: 'with the elder at my head to this battlefield.'
segments.s272-g400: به کشتن دهم تن به پیش سپاه: به کشتن دهم is a light causative/volitional construction 'I give to killing' → 'I give (myself) to be killed' (دهم 'I give'); تن is 'body/self' (the object given); به پیش سپاه 'before the army' (به پیش = in front of, سپاه = the host/army). Together the clause means 'I will give my body to be slain before the host.'
segments.s272-g209: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; دیدند is the past 3rd-person-plural 'they saw'. In this poetic word order the verb precedes its subjects (لهاک و فرشیدورد), which are two coordinated proper names joined by و.
segments.s272-g210: چنان is an adverb/determiner 'such'; پایداری is a noun 'steadfastness/stability' describing the quality perceived. از introduces the source ('from/in'), and آن شیرمرد is the demonstrative + noun 'that champion/brave man' marking whose steadiness is meant.
segments.s272-g211: ز here (= از) is a preposition 'from/because of'; بس is an intensifier 'so much/so great' (with خشم = 'anger'); گفتند is the past-plural 'they said/spoke'; یک با دگر is an idiomatic expression 'one with another' = 'to one another', with یک used distributively and دگر 'the other/another'.
segments.s272-g212: که introduces the indirect question or reported speech; ما را uses ما 'we' plus the object/emphatic marker را; چه آمد is the question 'what has happened/come' (آمد used in the sense 'befall/happen'); ز اختر 'from the star' gives the presumed cause/fate; به سر is an idiom 'to the head' meaning 'upon us'.
segments.s272-g213: بر این زین is a prepositional phrase 'upon this saddle' (بر + demonstrative + زین). همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily/truly'. که introduces the descriptive clause; کوهست is the poetic contraction of کوه + است 'is a mountain', and و links a parallel predicate (the verb 'هست' is understood to apply to both coordinated parts).
segments.s272-g214: This clause states a general negation with ندرد: here the form is an older/poetic present-tense negative (ن‑ + درَد) meaning 'does not/ cannot tear' or 'is not torn'. بر او بر marks 'upon him' (reduplicated prepositional emphasis), جز از means 'except from', and شیر پوست 'a lion's skin' names the sole thing able to tear him while on that saddle.
segments.s272-g215: ز یارانش is an ablative 'from his companions' (یاران + possessive suffix ش). گیو is the proper name 'Giv'; آنگهی is an adverb 'then/thereupon'. نیزه خواست is a two-word predicate 'asked for/wanted a spear' (خواست past of خواستن).
segments.s272-g216: همی is a continuous/aspective particle 'kept/continued to'; گشت is past 'turned/roamed' (here 'kept turning/looking around'). هر سو 'every direction' (هر = every, سو = side/direction). چپ و دست راست is a paired phrase 'left and right (hand)'—دست qualifies راست as 'right-hand'—meaning 'on both left and right sides'.
segments.s272-g105: به کردار گرگان به‌روز شکار is a prepositional simile phrase: به + NP (کردار گرگان) = 'in the manner/behavior of the wolves' (literally 'to/after the conduct of the wolves'); به‌روز here means 'on the day (of)' and combines with شکار 'hunt' to yield 'on a day of hunting'. Word order is typical poetic Persian: the similative preposition به comes first, then the describing noun phrase, and a temporal specification (به‌روز شکار) follows to set the scene.
segments.s272-g106: بر آن بادپایان اخته زهار is the target phrase introduced by the preposition بر 'upon/against'. آن is a demonstrative determiner 'those/the', بادپایان (بادپا + plural -ان) = 'the fleet‑footed/quick‑running ones'. اخته is an adjective or epithet meaning 'castrated/eunuch' modifying بادپایان; زهار here is an oath/exclamatory epithet (pejorative), appended after the noun phrase. The whole phrase functions as the object/target of the action implied earlier (they fell upon ...).
segments.s272-g107: میان سپاه اندرون تاختند is a locative + verb clause: میان سپاه 'into the midst of the army/host' (میان = 'among/midst' + سپاه), اندرون reinforces the interior/inward sense ('within/inside'), and تاختند is past plural of تاختن meaning 'they charged/galloped/attacked'. The verb comes at the end, as usual, and the adverbial اندرون intensifies the direction.
segments.s272-g108: ز کینه همی دل بپرداختند is an adverbial-causal clause: ز 'from/because of' + کینه 'hatred' gives the cause; همی is an archaic/epic particle here functioning as an intensifier ('indeed/so/thereupon'); دل is the direct object 'heart' (here metonymically 'their courage/feelings'); بپرداختند is the perfect/past plural of پرداختن used poetically — literally 'they paid' but in this context idiomatically 'they gave/let forth/exerted (their hearts)' or 'they poured out their hearts/they put their hearts into the action'. The clause therefore means 'driven by hatred, they threw themselves/put their hearts into it.'
segments.s272-g109: همه دشت برگستوانور سوار is a locative noun phrase: همه دشت 'the whole plain' (subject/setting). برگستوانور is an obscure compound treated here as a descriptive compound attached to the following noun (the text's dialectal/poetic form); سوار 'riders/horsemen' names who occupy the plain. In normal word order the place comes first, then the describing elements and the group (sوار), so the phrase reads 'the whole plain (was) of the ... horsemen.'
segments.s272-g110: پراگنده گشته گه کارزار is a verbless/nominal clause with a participial predicate: پراگنده 'widely scattered' (adjective/participle) + گشته 'has become' (perfect participle used as auxiliary/predicative) = 'has become scattered/widely dispersed'. گه کارزار is a genitive/nominal compound meaning 'the place/spot of battle' (گه = place/occasion, کارزار = battle). Together: '(the plain) has become widely scattered — a place of battle.'
segments.s272-g111: چه مایه فتاده به پای ستور is an exclamatory clause: چه (exclamative 'what/how') + مایه ('amount, quantity' used idiomatically in exclamation = 'how many/what a lot') + فتاده (perfect participle of افتادن 'to fall', here 'has fallen') + به پای ستور 'at/to the feet of the beasts/steeds' (prepositional phrase). The whole yields 'How many have fallen under the hooves of the beasts.'
segments.s272-g112: کفن جوشن و سینه شیر گور is a noun list followed by a nominal predication: کفن 'shroud' and جوشن 'cuirass/armor' are listed objects; و links to سینه شیر گور, where سینه شیر 'the lion's chest/breast' is followed by گور 'grave'. In classical Persian such nominal strings can omit a copula; here گور functions as a predicative noun for 'سینه شیر' meaning 'the lion's breast (is) a grave'. The line therefore catalogs what lies where: shroud, armor, and the lion's breast as a grave.
segments.s272-g505: چهارم (ordinal/adverb) marks the sequence — 'the fourth (to arrive)'. خود is an emphatic reflexive pronoun 'himself' placed before the verb. آید is the present/historic-present form of آمدن 'to come' used narratively; the verb takes the goal phrase به پشت سپاه ('to the rear of the army') where به + noun (پشت) indicates direction/position and سپاه (army/host) is its complement.
segments.s272-g506: شه نامبردار is a nominal phrase: شه (an older/poetic form of 'king') modified by نامبردار 'renowned/aforementioned' — together 'the renowned king'. با introduces accompaniment ('with'), followed by پیل 'elephant(s)'. و is the coordinating 'and'; گاه here appears as a noun but its senses given (time/occasion) do not fit the likely sense 'camp/place' in this line, so the precise semantic match is not in the provided sense-list.
segments.s272-g507: چو here functions as a temporal conjunction 'when'. گفتار گودرز is the noun phrase 'Goudarz's speech' or 'the words of Goudarz' (گفتار = utterance/speech; گودرز = proper name). زان سان 'in that manner' is a frozen phrase (زان = 'from that'; سان = 'so/like'). شنید is the past form of شنیدن 'to hear' — 'he heard'.
segments.s272-g508: سرشکش is a poetic noun for 'teardrop' (compound of سر + شُک suffix); ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. مژگان (eyelashes) is the source. به رخ بر چکید is a locative-directional phrase plus verb: به رخ 'onto the cheek/face' (به + رخ) with the preposition بر reinforcing 'upon', and چکید is the past 'dripped/fell'. The order places the path/goal before the verb, as is normal in Persian poetic phrasing.
segments.s272-g509: پذیرفت is the past tense 'he accepted' (پذیرفتن). سر تا به سر is an idiomatic intensifier meaning 'from head to head' → 'completely/entirely'; سر is literally 'head' and تا 'as far as' links the two instances of سر. پند اوی = 'his advice' where پند is 'advice' and اوی is an archaic third-person possessive/pronoun 'his'.
segments.s272-g510: همی is the archaic narrative particle (here emphatic/continuative). جست is from جستن; in this context it means 'he sought/tried' (not 'leapt'). از آن کار marks the source/subject-matter 'from that affair' (از = from/concerning; آن = that; کار = affair/deed). پیوند اوی 'his bond/link' — پیوند = bond/link, اوی is possessive 'his', so the clause says he sought to form/bind himself to that undertaking.
segments.s272-g511: به سالار گفت = 'he said to the commander' (به = to; سالار = commander/chief). آنچ is a relative/demonstrative 'that which/whatever'. فرمان دهی combines فرمان 'command' with دهی, the (present/subjunctive) form of دادن 'to give' addressed to 'you' — here rendering 'whatever command you give'. Note: the printed sense-list for گفت in the data appears to list 'conversation' rather than the verb 'said', so the semantic sense in that list does not match the actual verbal use here.
segments.s272-g512: میان بسته دارم is a compact expression: میان 'between', بسته 'closed/sealed' (here 'kept secret'), دارم 'I have' used idiomatically for 'I will keep/hold'. به سان 'in the manner of/like' + رهی (رهی = 'a wayfarer' or 'one on the road') — i.e. 'I will keep it sealed between us, as a traveler would' (an idiomatic promise of discretion).
segments.s272-g25: This clause is a compressed interrogative/relative phrase. که here introduces the query ('who/that'); چندان modifies the scale of the following noun ('so great / to that extent'); کجا here functions adverbially ('anywhere / is there') and sits before با شما ('with you'); لشکرست is a poetic contraction of لشکر + است ('an army / there is an army'). Word order is inverted for rhythm: the predicate 'لشکرست' closes the clause.
segments.s272-g26: کسی is the indefinite subject 'anyone / someone'. کاندر is a fused/archaic locative 'که اندر' meaning 'who/that in' or 'in which'; این رزمگه is the locative 'in this battlefield'; درخورست is a nominal/adjectival predicate (درخور + است) meaning 'is fitting / worthy'. The clause therefore asks whether any person present is fit for this field.
segments.s272-g27: سران را: سران 'the chiefs' with را as the definite/direct-object marker. ببخشید here is an exhortative/imperative form meaning 'spare' or 'forgive' (classic literary imperative). تا introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that'), and بر سه روی means 'on three sides' (بر + سه + روی, i.e. occupy three fronts).
segments.s272-g28: بوند is the past/plural copula 'they were' (here used to state their placement/role). اندر این رزمگه is the locative 'in this battlefield' (اندر = in). کینه‌جوی is a predicate adjective/noun 'vengeful / seeking revenge', describing their rôle once spared.
segments.s272-g29: وز ایشان is an archaic fused form (و از ایشان) meaning 'and from them / and among them'. گروهی is an indefinite noun 'a group / some', followed by a relative clause که بیدارتر 'who are more alert' (بیدارتر = comparative/derived adjective 'more awake/alert').
segments.s272-g30: سپه را marks 'the host/army' as the thing to be protected (را = object marker). ز دشمن is the prepositional phrase 'from the enemy' (ز = from, poetic). نگهدارتر functions as an agent/predicate 'guard / keeper' (literary nominal/predicative form: one who keeps/guards). The phrase as a whole assigns the group's function.
segments.s272-g31: بدیشان is contracted (به ایشان) 'to them / for them'. سپارید is an imperative/command 'entrust / assign' (second person plural in literary style). پشت سپاه is the object phrase 'the rear (پشت) of the army' (noun + genitive), placed after the verb as is common in older prose/poetry.
segments.s272-g32: شما is the vocative/subject 'you (pl.)'. بر دو رویه is a prepositional phrase 'on two sides / on two fronts' (بر = on; رویه = surface/side). بگیرید is an imperative 'take' (second person plural), and راه 'way / road' is the direct object — 'take the road on two fronts.' Word order keeps the verb after the prepositional modifier for metrical/literary effect.
segments.s272-g281: This clause uses the distributive/alternating particle یکی ('one [of them]') to introduce the first of two parallel actions. سوی + noun is the prepositional phrase 'toward the mountain.' کنابد is an archaic/poetic 3rd‑person singular verb form (‑ad ending) meaning 'suffers, toils' and here describes the manner/state as he moves; برفت is an older past form 'he went away/left' (verb with the prefix بر‑ + رفت). The sequence shows manner (کنابد) followed by the motion verb (برفت).
segments.s272-g282: Another parallel clause introduced again by یکی 'one (other)'. سوی 'toward' governs the following place/name. زیبد here is likely a proper name or place (not the verb 'to become beautiful' in this context), and خرامید is a past tense verb 'he strode/proudly paced' (3rd‑person past). تفت at the end is probably a place-name or metrical/end word; its senses in the supplied list do not match this usage.
segments.s272-g283: همانگه is an adverb 'at that moment / immediately.' طلایه 'vanguard' is the direct nominal being discussed. ز + لشکر = 'from the army' (poetic preposition z = from/of + noun). به راه is the direction/goal 'onto the road / on their way' and forms the complement to 'send.'
segments.s272-g284: فرستاد is the simple past 'sent' (VSO order typical of classical Persian narrative). In this line the subject follows the verb: گودرز is the proper name (subject) and سالار شاه is an appositive/title 'the king's commander' (literally 'commander of the king'). The whole means 'Goudarz, the king's commander, sent...'.
segments.s272-g285: ز here is the poetic preposition 'of/from.' جوشنوران appears to be a compounded/poetic form referring to armors/brightness (the supplied senses do not fit cleanly); هرک is an indefinite 'whoever/whichever' or 'some' in this context and فرسوده بود is past 'was worn out/was exhausted.' Together: 'some of the armor was worn out.'
segments.s272-g286: ز خون 'from/with blood' indicates the impurity/source; دست و تیغش is a coordinated phrase 'his hand and his sword' (تیغ + possessive suffix ‑ش = 'his sword'). بیالوده is negative/privative (بی‑) + آلوده 'stained' → 'unstained / unsoiled'; بود is the past auxiliary 'was.' The clause means 'his hand and sword were unbloodied.'
segments.s272-g287: همه 'all/every' introduces a list of armor items. جوشن is 'cuirass/armor.' خود in this passage is an item-name (historically 'khud' = helmet), though the supplied senses give only 'self/own' and do not capture the technical meaning here. ترگ and زره are further garments/armor pieces ('cloak/rag' and 'mail/armor' respectively). The sequence enumerates the equipment that was handled.
segments.s272-g288: گشادند is a past plural 'they loosened/opened' (the meaning 'loosened' or 'undid' suits here). مر is an archaic enclitic pronoun 'my' (classical form corresponding to modernِ من/ـم), placed before the noun. بندها 'the straps/fastenings' is the plural object; را is the definite/object marker marking بندها as the object; گره 'knots' is in apposition/description — the phrase says they loosened the knots of my straps. Note that word order in classical Persian often differs from modern prose; verb precedes subject and object markers and possessive enclitics may appear in positions that look archaic.
segments.s272-g121: سپهدار is a title meaning 'commander' and governs the following noun phrase. پیران و مردان خویش = 'the elders and his men' where و is the conjunction 'and'. خویش is formed of خوی (kin/own) plus the enclitic ش meaning 'his', so the phrase reads 'the elders and his own men.' Word order is title + possessor(s).
segments.s272-g122: به جنگ اندرون = 'into the inner battle' with به + noun phrase giving direction; پای بنهاد is an idiom 'set foot' (بنهاد, past of نهادن, 'placed/set'); پیش at the end functions adverbially as 'forward/in front'. The verb بنهاد is simple past third-person, so the whole clause means 'he set foot forward into the inner battle.'
segments.s272-g123: چو at the start is a temporal conjunction 'when'; آن زمان is an emphatic temporal phrase 'at that moment'. روی پیران = 'the faces of the elders' (روی 'face' followed by its possessor پیران). بدید is simple past 'saw' (archaic/poetic past form).
segments.s272-g124: عنان 'reins/bridle' + سوی او 'toward him' gives a directional phrase; جنگ را برگشید = 'the battle turned/was turned back' with را marking the definite object/emphasis and برگشید (poetic past) meaning 'turned/returned.' The clause is elliptical: the action of turning the reins produces the result that the battle swung toward the indicated person.
segments.s272-g125: از آن مهتران = 'from those chiefs' (از indicating source/out of). پیش پیران = 'in front of/in the presence of the elders' (پیش as locative). چهار 'four' follows the descriptive phrase and enumerates how many were involved (numeral after the noun phrase is common in poetic prose).
segments.s272-g126: به نیزه = 'by/with spear' (به here instrumental), ز اسب = 'from (their) horse(s)' (ز = from), اندر افگند = 'threw down/caused to fall' (افگند past of افکندن); the verb places the chiefs off their horses. خوار is an adjective 'brought low/despised' describing the condition of those thrown down. Verb is past tense and the instruments/direction are given before it.
segments.s272-g127: به زه کرد کمان = 'he made (the) bowstring [to the] bow' i.e. 'he strung the bow'; کرد is the general verb 'did/made' used here as the past action 'strung'. پیران ویسه is a name/title sequence (Piran Viseh); in poetry titles and names can appear in this order attached to the verb phrase.
segments.s272-g128: همی is a progressive/emphatic particle meaning 'kept on/continually'; تیر بارید literally 'arrows rained' (بارید used intransitively = 'fell/rained down'), and بر بدگمان = 'upon the enemy/suspicious one' with بر indicating the target. The construction pictures arrows showering down on the foe.
segments.s272-g433: بر + او introduces the target of the shout: بر here means toward/to (a poetic/prepositional use). سر به سر is an idiom ‘one after another’ (lit. head-to-head) modifying خواندند (they shouted/uttered). خواندند is past tense, 3rd person plural, here ‘they called/sang/cried out’; آفرین is the acclamation they voiced.
segments.s272-g434: که introduces a reported clause (‘that’/‘saying that’). چون تو = ‘like you’ (چون used as ‘like’). کسی نیست is the existential negative ‘there is no one’ (کسی ‘no one’ + نیست ‘is not’). پر داد و دین is a nominative pair describing the quality lacking in any other: پر (here ‘full/abounding (of)’) qualifies داد (used as a quality — ‘justice/giving’) and دین ‘religion/piety’; the clause states that no one equals you in those qualities.
segments.s272-g435: پرستنده ‘devotee/worshipper’ is the thing possessed; چون تو ‘such as you / like you’ postmodifies it. فریدون is the possessor (subject) and نداشت is past tense ‘did not have’ (3rd person singular past of داشتن). Word order (object phrase before possessor plus past verb) is common in classical Persian.
segments.s272-g436: که introduces a relative clause (‘who/that’). گیتی سراسر = ‘the whole world’ (گیتی ‘world’ + سراسر ‘entire’). به شاهی گذاشت is a verbal phrase: گذاشت is past simple ‘placed/put’ and به شاهی = ‘into kingship/under rule’ (به + شاهی, i.e. ‘to/under kingship’). The clause describes the action associated with the referenced person.
segments.s272-g437: ستون سپاهی is a compound/title: ستون ‘pillar’ + سپاهی ‘of the army’ (سپاهی here adjectival/military sense), and سالار شاه = ‘commander/lord of kings’ (سالار ‘commander’ + شاه ‘king(s)’). This segment lists honorifics/titles in nominal apposition.
segments.s272-g438: فرازنده is an agentive present-participle/adjective ‘one who raises or exalts’. It takes the following coordinated objects تاج و گاه و کلاه — ‘crown, [seat/throne], and cap’ — as things he exalts or sets up. Note: گاه here functions as a literary/archaic noun in the sequence (sense of ‘seat’/‘place’), not its common senses of ‘time’; no matching sense for that meaning is listed, so treat it as a poetic usage.
segments.s272-g439: فدی کرده is a compound perfect/participial phrase: فدی (ransom/sacrifice) + کرده (done/has made) = ‘having sacrificed’ or ‘having offered as ransom’. It is followed by a list of direct objects جان و فرزند و چیز (‘life and child and possessions/things’) that have been offered.
segments.s272-g440: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from’. سالار شاهان = ‘the lord/commander of kings’ (سالار ‘lord/commander’ + شاهان ‘kings’). چه جویند نیز is a rhetorical question: چه = ‘what’, جویند = present plural ‘do (they) seek/search (for)’, and نیز = ‘also/anymore’. Together: ‘what do they still ask from the lord of kings?’
segments.s272-g361: نهالیش is نهال + possessive -ش, a poetic/archaic possessive meaning “his X”; here the poet uses نهال (poetically) to refer to the person’s resting‑place or young person belonging to him. بد is an adverbial/qualifying word meaning “badly/poorly.” خاک follows as the noun “earth/soil,” so the sequence means “his bed/place was the earth.” بالینش (بالین + -ش) likewise is “his pillow” and سنگ is the noun “stone”; there is no copula in Persian, so the predicate is understood (“was”).
segments.s272-g362: خورش is a noun meaning a cooked dish or stew; here it functions as the subject/topic “his food.” گوشت is the direct noun “meat” and نخچیر specifies the kind: “game/prey.” The conjunction و links to پوشش پلنگ, where پوشش is “covering/cloak” and پلنگ names the leopard (i.e. a leopard‑skin used as a cloak). Again the copula is omitted: “(his) stew (was) meat of game, and (his) covering (was) a leopard.”
segments.s272-g363: همی رفت is an iterative/imperfective construction meaning “kept going” or “went on.” گم بوده is a stative/perfect construction “had been lost” or “was lost.” چون here functions as a comparative/temporal conjunction (“as/when/like”), and بیهشان is the archaic compound meaning “without them” (بیه + -شان). The segment thus describes continuous wandering while being lost and apart from the others.
segments.s272-g364: That-clause of purpose/result: که introduces a subordinate clause, and یابد is the 3rd‑person present/subjunctive of یافتن “to find” (here with a purposive/expectational force: “may/so that he may find” or simply “to find”). ز is the poetic preposition “from,” کیخسرو is the proper name, آنجا is the locative “there,” and نشان is “sign/mark.” The clause therefore means “so that he might find a sign there from Khosrow.”
segments.s272-g365: یکایک is an adverb meaning “one by one” or “each in turn.” چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning “when.” The phrase نزدیک خسرو رسید places the locative/complement (نزدیکِ خسرو) before the verb رسید “arrived/reached/approached,” so: “when (they) came near Khosrow.”
segments.s272-g366: بر او آفرین کرد is literal word order “upon him (بر او) acclamation (آفرین) made (کرد),” i.e. “he praised him” or “he lauded him.” The relative/identifying phrase کو را بدید follows: کو here functions as an archaic relative/pronoun (“whom/which”) and را is the definite object marker; بدید is the past of دیدن “to see.” Together: “he praised each one whom he saw.”
segments.s272-g367: وزان پس is a temporal connective meaning “and/from that (time) then/after that.” به ایران is the directional phrase “to Iran.” نهادند is the 3rd‑person plural past of نهادن “to place/set/put” and with روی (“face”) forms the idiom نهادند روی = “they set/turned their faces (toward).” روی here denotes “face/direction.”
segments.s272-g368: خبر شد is a light/verbal phrase meaning “news became” → “news reached/it became known.” به introduces the experiencer “to/for,” پیران is plural “elders,” and پرخاشجوی is an adjective “quarrelsome/aggressive,” qualifying the elders. So: “the news came to the quarrelsome elders.”
segments.s272-g185: به is used instrumentally here ('by/with'); word order places the instrument before the object (به نیزه). زره (armor) is the direct object and بردرید is a past-tense perfective verb 'tore apart/cut'. از نهیب is a secondary prepositional phrase 'from/on account of the shock/clamor' modifying the action.
segments.s272-g186: نیامد is a past negative 'did not come'; برون 'out' follows it to indicate outward motion. پای گیو ('Giv's foot') is the subject/thing that failed to move, and از رکیب uses از + noun to mean 'from/out of his mount' (classical poetic ordering places the prepositional phrase after the noun).
segments.s272-g187: بزد is simple past 'he struck/struck at'; the phrase places the weapon (نیزه) near the verb. پس 'then/after' sequences the action. گیو بر اسب اوی literally places the addressee ('Giv') before the prepositional phrase 'on his horse' and the enclitic اوی (archaic) here is possessive 'his' referring back to Giv.
segments.s272-g188: ز (poetic short form of از) marks cause 'from/because of'; درد is the cause 'pain'. اندر آمد is a compound-like phrase meaning 'came into / happened' used intransitively here so that تگاور (the rider/horseman) is the subject; به روی means 'to/upon the face', describing how he fell (fell forward onto his face).
segments.s272-g189: پیاده شد is a light-verb construction meaning 'got down / dismounted' (پیاده = 'on foot', شد = 'became/got'); از باره here is a prepositional/causal grouping 'because of/on account of' followed by لهاک 'haste/eagerness' which modifies the action; مرد is the agent 'the man' (subject).
segments.s272-g190: فراز آمد is a verbal phrase 'came up' or 'came forward' with فراز 'up/on high' placed before آمد; از دور is the source/location 'from afar' and فرشیدورد is the proper-name subject/agent arriving from that distance.
segments.s272-g191: This is a compact, poetic noun cluster: ابر نیزه 'a cloud/shower of spears' (metaphorical), followed by the name گیو and تیغی 'with a blade/sword' where تیغی is an adjectival/verbal-form indicating the sword-action; چو باد is a simile 'like the wind', describing the swiftness of the blade. Poetic syntax omits an explicit verb here, which appears in the next segment.
segments.s272-g192: بزد is past 'he struck'; the following نیزه names the target. ببرید is a perfective here meaning 'cut off/cleaved' (poetic past action), and the conjunction و links that action to برگشت شاد 'he returned joyful' (برگشت = returned; شاد = glad). The sequence gives a tight action series: strike — cut — return rejoicing.
segments.s272-g545: همان لشکر است + این‌که introduces a nominal clause: 'همان لشکر است' = 'it is that same army'; این‌که is the relative/introducing particle 'that' ('the one that...'). از جنگ ما ('from our battle') is a prepositional phrase marking the thing they turned away from; word order is topical subject + copula + relative clause.
segments.s272-g546: بپیچید is a past-tense, 3rd-person plural verb meaning 'they turned aside/withdrew'. و is the conjunction 'and'. بس کرد is a light verbal phrase where بس ('enough') + کرد ('made/did') = 'put an end to / ceased'; آهنگ ما ('our intention/aim/attack') is the object of بس کرد. Word order follows verb then object in the relative clause.
segments.s272-g547: بدین رزمگه is a prepositional/locative phrase 'at/by this battlefield' (بدین = بِ + این, archaic 'with/at this'); بست باید میان is inverted poetic word order where باید بست = 'one must bind/set' and میان ('between') marks the relation — literally 'it must be bound between (them)'. In classical/style Persian the auxiliary/modal باید can follow the main verb for emphasis.
segments.s272-g548: به کینه شدن is a result/infinitival phrase: به + noun + شدن = 'to become hatred' i.e. 'to become hostile / for hatred to arise'. پیش ایرانیان = 'in the presence of / before the Iranians' (پیش here = 'before/in front of'). Together they express purpose/result: 'so that hatred arises before the Iranians.'
segments.s272-g549: چنین کرد = 'thus did / so acted' (adverb + past verb). گودرز is the agent (proper name) and پیمان is the object 'a covenant/pledge'. که introduces the content clause (reported intention) that follows. Word order here places the result/act before the content clause.
segments.s272-g550: سران 'leaders' is the direct object of برگزینم. برگزینم is 1st-person singular present/subjunctive (classical/persian literary form) meaning 'I will choose / I shall select.' از این انجمن 'from this assembly' is the source phrase following the verb. The verb-before-object order here is a stylistic choice common in epic diction.
segments.s272-g551: یکایک 'one by one / each in turn' is an adverbial. به روی اندر آریم روی is a poetic phrase meaning 'we set/bring (them) face to face' — اندر (in) + روی (face) with آریم (1st plural present/subjunctive 'we bring/put') form the motion toward confrontation; the repeated روی emphasizes 'face-to-face.' The verb form is literary and corresponds to 'we shall place/bring.'
segments.s272-g552: دو لشکر 'the two armies' is the subject; برآساید is a 3rd-person singular/jussive form meaning 'will be appeased / will find ease' (literary diction). از گفت و گوی is an ablative/instrumental phrase 'by/from talk and discourse' (گفت و گو = discussion). Together: 'the two armies will be pacified through discussion.'
segments.s272-g249: خدنگش is خدنگ + the enclitic -ش meaning “his dagger.” بدرید is a past tense (poetic) form of دریدن, here ‘tore/cut’; the verb governs the following locative expression. برگستوان should be read as بر گُستوان (“on/against the upper arm/shoulder”) — a locative phrase indicating where the dagger struck.
segments.s272-g250: بماند is a classical/poetic past-form meaning ‘remained’ (3rd sg.); آن زمان is a temporal phrase ‘at that time’ placed before the noun it modifies; بارگی is a diminutive/figurative noun ‘little limb/leaf’ and is the subject that 'remained'. بی روان is a two-word adjective phrase ‘without soul / lifeless’ (بی = without, روان = soul), modifying بارگی.
segments.s272-g251: پیاده شد is a light/periphrastic verb phrase meaning ‘became on-foot’ → ‘dismounted’ (شد is the auxiliary/verb). ازباره (classical form of از باره) is an adverb ‘again’. مرد سوار is the noun phrase ‘the mounted man / rider’ (mرد = man, سوار = rider) and is the subject undergoing the action.
segments.s272-g252: سپر بر سر آورد is a straightforward verbal clause: سپر (shield) is the object, بر سر is the locative prepositional phrase ‘on (his) head’, and آورد is the past tense ‘brought/put’ (here ‘placed/put on’). و links clauses; بر ساخت کار is another verbal clause in which بر + ساخت (past ‘made/did’) with کار (‘work/task/effort’) expresses ‘set about the task’ or ‘made preparations’ — a conventional idiom meaning he prepared or began to act.
segments.s272-g253: ز ترکان is a source phrase ‘from the Turks’ (ز = from). بر آمد here functions as the compound verb برآمد ‘rose up / arose’ (بر + آمد); سراسر غریو is the noun phrase ‘a roar throughout / a shout all around’ that follows the verb in inverted poetic order.
segments.s272-g254: سواران is the subject ‘the horsemen’. برفتند is the past plural of رفتن with the prefix بر- (here ‘they rode/advanced/charged’). بر سان دیو is a simile: سان = ‘like/as’, دیو = ‘demon/fiend’, and the particle بر here introduces the comparative phrase — ‘they advanced like devils.’
segments.s272-g255: مر is the dative/clitic-like pronoun ‘me / to me’ (classical order places it before the object). او را is the accusative object ‘him’ (او + object marker را). به چاره is a purpose phrase ‘for a remedy / for treatment’. ز آوردگاه is a source phrase ‘from the battlefield’ — together these mark that they brought him from the battlefield to the speaker for remedy.
segments.s272-g256: کشیدند is the past plural verb ‘they pulled/drew/dragged’ (کشیدن). از پیش روی is a prepositional phrase ‘from before / out of the front of’ (از = from, پیش = before), with روی سپاه ‘the face/front of the army’ completing the locative — they pulled him out from in front of the army. The verb here connects back to the object او را mentioned immediately before.
segments.s272-g65: چو بشنید is a temporal clause: چو + past verb (بشنید) = "when he heard." Subject (گیو) appears after the verb for poetic inversion. بردمید is an archaic/past verb here meaning something like "rose/took up action" or "seized (motion)" and links this clause to the following action; it is a past, narrative form.
segments.s272-g66: ز لشکر is a prepositional phrase "from the host/army." یکی نامور is a nominal phrase "a certain renowned one" (یکی = "a certain/someone" modifying نامور). برگزید is a past-tense transitive verb "he chose/selected," with its subject omitted (understood from context). The sequence is typical Persian order: prepositional phrase → noun phrase → verb.
segments.s272-g67: This clause identifies the man: نام او بود فرهاد گرد = "his name was Farhad-gird." بود is the past of "to be" and links subject (او) to the predicate (فرهاد گرد). کجا at the start functions in a poetic/relative sense introducing this identification (literally 'where' in the list of senses but here acting to mark the following identification). Note that گرد here is part of the proper name.
segments.s272-g68: بخواند here is used in the sense "he summoned/called together" (an older narrative form). The coordinating و links that action to سپرد, where سپرد is a past transitive "entrusted/handed over." سپه یکسر = "the army, entirely/wholesale," and او را + را marks the indirect object being entrusted ("to him").
segments.s272-g69: دو صد کار دیده is a pre-nominal participial phrase: دو صد ("two hundred") + کار ("deeds/battles") + دیده (past participle "having seen") — together modifying دلاور سران ("valiant chiefs"). This is a compact poetic way to say "the brave leaders who had seen two hundred deeds."
segments.s272-g70: بفرمود is a polite/honorific past form meaning "he commanded/said." تا here introduces purpose/result ("so that / in order that"). زنگه شاوران is a noun phrase meaning roughly "the summons/bell (zangah) of the advisers" or more broadly "to call together the advisers/council;" شاوران is the advisers/counsellors. The clause therefore reads "he commanded (them) so as to summon the advisers."
segments.s272-g71: برد تاختن is a literary verbal phrase meaning roughly "to take up a charge / to begin to ride/charge." تاختن is the infinitive/masdar-form ("to gallop/charge") and سوی + name gives the direction: سوی فرشیدورد = "toward Farshidvard." The combination is a compact narrative: "he made a charge toward Farshidvard."
segments.s272-g72: برانگیزد is a present/future-stem form used in narrative sense here meaning "he roused/raised (up)." از رود = "from the river," and وز آب گرد is a compact poetic phrase meaning "and from the water round/about it" (وز is an archaic connective here, and گرد = "around"). The clause describes raising forces or stirring men from the river and its surrounding waters.
segments.s272-g161: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. پیران چنان دید is a simple past clause ('he saw the elders thus'); دید is the past-tense verb. برگشت is a past action 'he turned/returned' and زوی (= ز + وی) is a prepositional phrase 'from them/from that place' attached to برگشت.
segments.s272-g162: سوی لشکر خویش is a directional phrase 'toward his army' (سوی = toward; لشکر = host/army). خوی + ش together form خویش 'his own' (possessive). بنهاد is the archaic/past form of نهاد 'put/placed/set' and here combines with روی 'face' to form the idiom 'بنهاد روی' = 'set/turned (his) face (toward)'.
segments.s272-g163: خروشان is a participial/adjectival form 'roaring/tumultuous' modifying the (implied) subjects. پر از درد is a fixed phrase 'full of pain' (پر + از + noun), with درد here indicating anguish/pain. رخساره زرد is a noun + adjective 'faces pale' (adjective follows the noun, typical poetic order).
segments.s272-g164: به نزدیک is a locative expression 'near' or 'close to' (به + نزدیک). The following coordinated names/terms (لهاک و فرشیدورد) mark the place/persons near whom they appeared; فرشیدورد is a proper name here. (لهاک in this line functions as a name; it does not use the common-noun senses given, so sense is marked -1.)
segments.s272-g165: بیامد is a past verb 'he came/appeared' used here to introduce direct speech. که‌ای is a poetic junction where که introduces the forthcoming utterance and ای (vocative) addresses the audience; نامداران من is a vocative nominal phrase 'my renowned ones' with the possessive مُن (من) placed after the noun in poetic order.
segments.s272-g166: This is a continuation of the vocative list: دلیران و خنجرگزاران من = 'my brave ones and my dagger-bearers.' The plural nouns are coordinated by و; من again functions as a postposed possessive 'my' (common in classical/poetic Persian). خنجرگزاران is a compound meaning those who bear daggers (here fellow warriors).
segments.s272-g167: شما را is the marked object 'you' with the definite/object marker را (here topicalized/emphatic). ز بهر چنین روزگار is a purpose/benefit phrase 'for the sake of such a time' (ز بهر = 'for' in classical usage; چنین روزگار = 'such a time/day/fate').
segments.s272-g168: همی is an archaic/adverbial particle meaning 'always/still' that frequently appears before past-tense verbs for continuous or habitual sense. پرورانیدم is first-person singular past 'I reared/fostered' (پروراندن). اندر کنار is a locative phrase 'at/near (my) side' or 'in my company' (کنار here = accompaniment/company).
segments.s272-g329: که introduces a subordinate clause meaning 'that' or 'for/that which'. تا این زمان is a temporal phrase ('until/ up to this time') that precedes the verb phrase. هرچ + past verb رفت forms an indefinite relative: 'whatever went' or 'all that went forth', and از نبرد is the source phrase 'out of/from battle'. Word order is the typical Persian subordinate clause: conjunction + temporal phrase + indefinite subject + verb + source.
segments.s272-g330: به کام دل ما is a prepositional phrase 'to/for the desire of our heart' with کام دل as the object of به and ما marking possession. همی before the verb is an archaic/poetic continuous/habitual marker (here 'always/ever'); گشت is the past of گشتن 'turned/was turned' and گرد functions adverbially 'about/around' or as part of the verbal phrase meaning 'turned to/around'. Together: 'has ever turned to the desire of our hearts.'
segments.s272-g331: فراوان modifies شگفتی and means 'many/abundant' (here 'many wonders'). رسیدم is first-person past of رسیدن; in this context it carries the idiomatic sense 'befell me / came to me' (i.e. I encountered). به سر links forward and will combine with جهان in the next segment ('upon the head/of the world' → 'in the world').
segments.s272-g332: جهان را is the definite object/topic 'the world' marked by را. ندیدم is simple past negative 'I did not see'. مگر here means 'except' (exclusion). بر گذر is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in passing / on the way' (گذر 'passing'/'passage'); together: 'I saw nothing of the world except in passing.'
segments.s272-g333: ز is the poetic form of از meaning 'from'. بیداد و داد is an antithetical pair 'injustice and justice'. آنچ ('that which/what') is a relative/indefinite pronoun and آمد is the past 'came/befell'; به شاه marks the experiencer/recipient 'to the king'. The clause literally: 'What came to the king from injustice and justice.'
segments.s272-g334: بد و نیک را هم means 'both the bad and the good' with را marking the phrase and هم emphasizing 'alike/also'. راه is the predicate 'the way/path'. بدویست here is an archaic/poetic predicate form; in this line it functions to assert sameness or a characteristic of the way for both (rendered 'was the same' or 'was alike'). Word order places the topic 'بد و نیک را هم' before the predicate 'بدویست راه.'
segments.s272-g335: چو here introduces a temporal clause 'when'. ما is the object 'us'. چرخ گردان is a compound subject 'the turning/rotating sky (heaven/fate)'; گردان is an adjectival participle 'revolving'. فراوان modifies the following verb/creation: 'created abundantly.' سرشت is the past form meaning '(he) created' (archaic/poetic past). The clause reads: 'When the revolving sky created us abundantly...'.
segments.s272-g336: درود is an exclamatory/nominal 'blessing/greeting' used idiomatically like 'blessed (be)'. آن کجا is 'that place/where' (آن + کجا). بآرزو is a compound prepositional/ adverbial 'with desire / in hope' and خود specifies 'his/its own'. بکشت is an archaic past verb form (ب + کشت) meaning 'did/caused/destroyed/killed' depending on context — here an agent 'acted' or 'slain' by his own desire. The whole segment is an evaluative clause: 'Blessed is that place where he acted by his own desire.'
segments.s272-g97: بگفت is a past narrative verb meaning “said” (poetic past). The sequence این سخن پهلوان is the direct object phrase “these words (of) the hero,” and با پسر marks the addressee (with/to his son) — here با functions to indicate the person being addressed. Word order follows Persian: verb + object + addressee.
segments.s272-g98: This is an addressed command in compressed, poetic word order. پسر is vocative (“O son”), جنگ را uses the object marker را to indicate the battle as the definite purpose/object (“for the battle”), and تنگ بسته کمر is a participial/compound description literally “waist tightly bound” (= “gird your loins tightly”); the past participle بسته with تنگ yields an exhortation here.
segments.s272-g99: سواران is the subject “the riders/horsemen.” که بودند forms a relative clause “who were,” and بر میسره is a locative/adverbial phrase; میسره is an archaic/poetic noun here meaning a state of readiness or ease, so بر میسره indicates the riders’ condition/location (“who were on/at readiness”).
segments.s272-g100: بفرمود is the honorific past verb “he commanded/ordered.” خواندن is a verbal noun/infinitive “to read/recite,” and همه یکسره modifies it: همه “all/everyone,” یکسره “straightaway/at once.” The construction means “he ordered that all should recite at once.”
segments.s272-g101: گرازه برون آمد is a straightforward past clause: گرازه (a sow/boar) + برون آمد “came out/forth.” The conjunction و links the next word; گستهم is an archaic/poetic verb form expressing agitation or dispersal (here conveying alarm or sudden movement) that connects to the following actors.
segments.s272-g102: هجیر سپهدار is a name plus title (“Hejīr, the commander”) in apposition, و بیژن adds “and Bijan.” به هم is a short adverbial phrase meaning “together / toward one another” indicating mutual or joint action; the whole names the persons who react to the sow.
segments.s272-g103: وزآنجا is a fused adverb “and from there/thereupon.” سوی introduces direction (“toward”), قلب here means “heart/core” (i.e., the central region) and توران is the place-name Turan; together they form the destination phrase “toward the heart of Turan,” while سپاه is the noun ‘army’ placed at the end of the clause in poetic word order.
segments.s272-g104: گرانمایگان is an epithet modifying سپاه in the previous segment — “the illustrious/noble ones (i.e., the noble army).” برگرفتند is the past plural “they took up/they set out,” and راه is the object “the road/way” (i.e. they set out on the route). The verb agrees with the plural army.
segments.s272-g169: كنون (now) + چون (here 'when/as') introduces a temporal clause. The prepositional phrase به جنگ اندر آمد places the verb آمد (past 'came') at the end in typical classical/poetic order, with اندر ('into/within') marking entry and سپاه (the subject 'the host/army') placed after the verb by inversion.
segments.s272-g170: جهان شد is a verbless/auxiliary-like phrase 'the world became/turned (out)'; به ما بر uses به + pronoun + بر ('against') to mean 'turned against us.' The final phrase ز دشمن سیاه uses poetic ز ('from/by') to mark the source/agent 'from the black enemy.' شد is simple past/inchoative 'became/turned.'
segments.s272-g171: نبینم is a negative present/past sense 'I do not see / I see no' introducing an indefinite object; کسی here in negative scope means 'no one.' کز is the relative 'who/that,' and پی + noun means 'in pursuit of / after' so کز پی نام و ننگ = 'who in pursuit of name and shame.'
segments.s272-g172: به پیش سپاه is a locative phrase 'before/in the presence of the host' (به + پیش), اندر آید is the verb 'enters/comes in' with اندر marking 'into,' and به جنگ finalizes the complement 'into battle.' آید is third‑person present/habitual (classical use) inside the relative clause.
segments.s272-g173: چو here is temporal 'when.' آواز پیران بدیشان رسید places the noun phrase 'the elders' call' before the verb رسید ('reached/arrived') and uses بدیشان ('to them') as the dative/benefactive—typical word order in classical poetry.
segments.s272-g174: دل نامداران is the subject 'the hearts of the famed ones.' ز introduces the cause/agent 'by/from vengeance,' and بردمید is a past verb meaning 'seized'/'took hold of'—so literally 'the hearts of the famed were seized by vengeance.' Note the passive/affected meaning conveyed by this construction.
segments.s272-g175: برفتند is simple past 'they went/departed.' و connects the clauses. گفتند‌گر is the verb 'they said' plus the conditional particle گر ('if'), introducing the conditional clause that follows; جان پاک is the conditional's subject 'a pure soul.'
segments.s272-g176: نباشد is the negative subjunctive/present 'there be not / is not' used inside the conditional (گر ... نباشد). به تن is a locative 'in the body' and نیستمان ('is not ours') expresses possession/absence: 'we do not have'—so نیستمان بیم و باک = 'we have no fear or dread.' The pair بیم و باک are synonymous nouns 'fear / dread.'
segments.s272-g481: که introduces a relative/clause ('who' / 'that') linking this clause to the previous narrative. با + noun phrase marks accompaniment ('with'); پیر is an epithet meaning 'old' or 'veteran' modifying the following noun phrase. سر here functions as a title 'chief/head' (compound 'سر پهلوان' = 'chief champion'), and سپاه names the force ('the army'). The whole group is a prepositional phrase 'with the old chief champion of the army'.
segments.s272-g482: کمر بست is an idiom 'to gird one's loins' (کمر = belt/waist + بست = bound), i.e. prepare for action; و simply links the two coordinated actions. شد here is the past verb meaning 'went/turned/proceeded' (classical usage), سوی = 'toward', and آوردگاه = 'battlefield'.
segments.s272-g483: سپهدار is the subject 'the commander'; پس is a temporal adverb 'then' following the subject. گستهم is the proper name 'Gostaham' (here the recipient) and is marked by the object particle را. بخواند is an archaic/past form used here in the sense 'summoned/called'.
segments.s272-g484: بسی here intensifies the following nouns ('much' or 'many') and combines with the paired nouns پند و اندرز 'advice and counsel' (a common formula). با او indicates the recipient 'to him', with the verb براند (archaic form of راندن) meaning here 'addressed/gave' or 'uttered to him'—an older poetic use rather than the literal 'to drive away'.
segments.s272-g485: بدو is an enclitic prepositional phrase 'to him' ('unto him'); گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here followed by direct speech). زنهار is an exclamatory warning 'beware!' and بیدار باش is an imperative 'be alert' (باش = imperative 'be').
segments.s272-g486: سپه را is the definite object 'the army' (را marks definiteness/accusative), ز + دشمن = 'from the enemy' (z is the poetic preposition 'from'), and نگهدار باش is an imperative construction 'be a guardian / guard' (نگهدار used as an imperative or verbal noun plus باش for command).
segments.s272-g487: شب و روز ('night and day') is an adverbial phrase meaning 'constantly'. در جوشن places the action 'in (your) armor' (جوشن = cuirass/armor). کینه‌جوی is an adjective 'seeking revenge' or 'vengeful', describing the warrior's disposition while armored.
segments.s272-g488: نگر is an imperative 'look/see/guard' (here 'take care'). تا introduces a subordinate purpose/limit clause ('so that' / 'lest'); گشاده = 'open' and ندارید is the present negative 'you do not have' (نداشتن = 'to have' negated), with روی = 'face' as the object. The clause literally says 'take care so that you do not have an open face' i.e. 'do not show an unguarded face'.
segments.s272-g297: به پیران رسیدم is a typical Persian past-tense clause with the preposition به + object (پیران) followed by the 1st-person past رسیدم “I arrived/reached.” نوندم is an uncommon/archaic token here (listed as a rare form/name) inserted before the locative phrase به جای; به جای is a prepositional phrase meaning “into/in the place” or “in the stead of” that links to the following predicate.
segments.s272-g298: فروماند is a past/formal verb meaning “remained/was left” or “fell/was worn out” in classical style; و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” ننهاد here is the negative past (ن + نهاد) of نهادن “to place/put,” i.e. “did not place” or “did not set.” از پیش پای is a prepositional phrase “from/before the feet,” giving the spatial relation for the verb (literally “did not set [himself/it] from before the feet”).
segments.s272-g299: چنانم is چنان “so/such” with the clitic -م marking the experiencer/possessor (“to me/for me”), a typical poetic/old construction. شتاب آمد is a light-verb construction: noun شتاب “haste” + آمد “came,” yielding the sense “haste came (upon me)” (i.e. I became hurried). از کار خویش is an ablative/cause phrase “because of/from my own affairs,” where خویش is the possessive form (خوی + ش).
segments.s272-g300: که introduces a result/explanatory clause (“so that/that”). گفتم here should be the 1st-person past “I said” (the provided lemma senses list is mismatched for this token). نباشم is the negative subjunctive/present form meaning “that I should not be” or “I would not be,” used in indirect speech after گفتم. دگر یار خویش = “any longer (دگر) [be] a companion (یار) to/of my own (خویش)” — word order is standard: verb phrase (نباشم) followed by the complement.
