segments.s272-g301: پس is the connective “then/so.” آن گفته is the noun phrase “that saying/utterance”; شاه بیژن is a genitival apposition “the king, Bijan.” به یاد is a fixed prepositional phrase meaning “in memory / in (his) mind” and indicates that the following verb will describe holding that saying in memory.
segments.s272-g302: همی داشت is the imperfect/continuous past sense formed from همی (a classical continuous/stative particle) + داشت (here functioning like ‘was holding/keeping’): “he was keeping/reminding himself.” وان دم “that moment” (وان = that, دم = moment) is a temporal phrase; مرا را یاد داد is literal “gave remembrance to me” (مرا = me, را = object marker, یاد داد = gave memory/ reminded), i.e. “it reminded me.” Note some provided lemmas for clitics are irregular in the list and have been interpreted by their role in the clause.
segments.s272-g303: که introduces reported content (“that …”). پیران is the subject “the elders.” به دست تو is an instrumental/agent phrase “by your hand” (به + دست + تو), where به here conveys agent/instrument (“by”). گردد is the 3rd-person present/subjunctive or optative of گشت/گردیدن used to express future/result (“will become / may become”), and تباه is the predicate adjective “ruined/ ruined.” The clause is a classical subordinate clause stating a prophetic/resultant outcome.
segments.s272-g304: از اختر is a prepositional phrase literally “from the star(s),” idiomatically indicating fate or astrological origin (“by/from the stars”). همین is the demonstrative “this very/just so”; بود is the past copula “was”; گفتار شاه is the noun phrase “the king's saying/utterance.” The whole segment states that the foregoing was exactly the king's saying, as declared (or foretold) by the stars.
segments.s272-g385: 'سران را' is the definite object phrase ('the chiefs' marked by را). 'همی خواهد' is a poetic present-future construction: همی is an old aspectual/emphatic particle and خواهد is third-person singular ('he wants/wills'); together they mean 'he now wishes/means'. 'اکنون' is the temporal adverb 'now'. 'به جنگ' is a goal phrase 'to/into battle' (به + جنگ). Word order: object - verbal phrase - adverb - goal; the subject (he) is implied by the verb خواهد.
segments.s272-g386: 'یکایک' is an adverb meaning 'one by one / each and every'. 'بباید شدن' is an older periphrastic modal construction: بباید (ب + باید) = 'must/ought' followed by the infinitive شدن 'to become' → 'must become'. The predicate noun phrase 'تیز چنگ' ('sharp talon/claw') follows the verb, so the whole clause says the chiefs (the sentence's subject) must 'become sharp talons' — a poetic way of saying each must act as a fierce warrior.
segments.s272-g387: 'که‌گر' is an archaic conditional particle meaning 'if'. 'ما' is the subject 'we'. The following phrase 'بدین کار' (here written as ب + دین + کار in the tokenization) means 'in this matter' or 'in this business' (بدین = 'in this', کار = 'matter/affair'). 'سستی کنیم' is a present-subjunctive clause 'we do/show laxity' (کنیم is the subjunctive form of کردن), so the whole clause means 'if we show slackness in this matter.'
segments.s272-g388: 'وگر' (in 'وگر نه') functions as 'but if / otherwise'. Again 'بدین' ('in this') + 'پیشدستی' ('initiative; pre-emptive action') forms the object phrase 'take the initiative in this'. 'کنیم' is the subjunctive/imperative 'let us do / we should do'. The clause therefore says 'or else we should take the initiative in this.'
segments.s272-g389: 'بهانه کند' = 'he makes an excuse' where کند is third-person singular 'does/makes'. 'بازگردد' is a present/subjunctive-form verb meaning 'he returns/turns back' (باز + گردد). 'ز جنگ' is the poetic form of 'از جنگ' = 'from the battle'. The sequence 'بهانه کند بازگردد ز جنگ' thus describes making an excuse and withdrawing from the fight.
segments.s272-g390: 'بپیچد' (from پیچیدن) means 'he turns' or 'he turns aside' (the ب- prefix is the older verbal prefix still used in poetry). The direct object is 'سر' ('head'), and 'از کینه و نام و ننگ' lists the things he turns his head away from: 'از' = 'from/out of', 'کینه' = 'hatred/grudge', 'نام' = 'name/reputation', 'ننگ' = 'shame/disgrace'. The clause means he averts his head from these (i.e., withdraws/avoids involvement because of hatred, reputation, and shame).
segments.s272-g391: 'ار' is an archaic conditional 'if' (equivalent to 'اگر'). 'ایدونک' is an old/poetic particle meaning 'so/thus/in this way'. 'باشید' is the (plural/polite) imperative/subjunctive 'be' ('be with me' here). 'با من یکی' literally 'be one with me' — an invitation to join as one man. The conditional frames the imperative: 'If you are thus, be one with me.'
segments.s272-g392: 'از ایشان فراوان' uses 'از' in a partitive/comparative sense 'of/from them (there are) many'; 'ایشان' = 'they/them'; 'فراوان' = 'many/plentiful'. 'و ما اندکی' = 'and we (are) few/small in number' (ما = 'we', اندکی = 'a little/few'). The short clauses contrast the enemy's greater numbers with the speaker's smaller force.
segments.s272-g129: سپر بر سر آورد is a verbal clause meaning “put/raised a shield on (one's) head”: سپر (object) + بر سر (prepositional phrase 'on the head') + آورد (past of آوردن, 'brought/put'). The subject follows in typical epic word‑order: گیو سترگ is the name plus epithet (Giv, ‘large‑eyed’), with the adjective/epithet after the name.
segments.s272-g130: به نیزه درآمد is an idiomatic verbal phrase: به (toward/into) + نیزه (spear) + درآمد (past of درآمدن, here 'came upon'/'charged at'); the sense is ‘advanced upon the spear’. به کردار گرگ is a following prepositional phrase of manner: به + کردار (‘in the manner/behavior’) + گرگ (wolf) = ‘like a wolf’. Note درآمد is used as a past verbal action and the manner phrase follows it.
segments.s272-g131: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. آهنگ here means 'intention/aim' and together آهنگ ... کرد is an idiom 'made/laid an intention' or 'set his mind/turned his attention to (something)'; کرد is the past auxiliary 'did/made' that completes the verbal expression.
segments.s272-g132: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause 'who/that'. جوید (classical present used in narration) means 'sought/desired' (here past in narrative sense). به آورد is an infinitival/complemental phrase 'to bring' or 'to cause to come about', and با او نبرد is the object phrase 'battle with him' ('با او' = with him, نبرد = battle). The clause as a whole: 'who sought to bring battle against him.'
segments.s272-g133: فروماند is a past predicate 'grew weary/failed/collapsed'. In classical epic order the verb precedes the subject, so اسبش همیدون 'his horse, Hamidun' is the subject (اسبش = his horse; همیدون a proper name). به جای is a locative phrase 'to/at the place' specifying where it collapsed.
segments.s272-g134: از آنجا که is a causal conjunction 'because (of the fact) that'. بد modifies the following verb as an adverb 'badly/poorly', پیش here functions adverbially 'forward/forwardly', ننهاد is the past negative of نهادن 'did not place/put', and پای is the object 'foot'. The clause literally reads 'because there it did not put its foot well forward.'
segments.s272-g135: یکی here is an indefinite subject 'someone / a certain one'. تازیانه is the object 'a whip', followed by the prepositional phrase بر آن تیز رو 'upon that swift‑faced one' (آن = that; تیز‑رو = 'swift‑running' / 'quick of face' used as epithet). Word order is object + PP + (following) verb.
segments.s272-g136: بزد is the past verb 'he struck'. خشم را has خشم 'anger' marked by the definite/object marker را; here the phrasing suggests the striking was associated with arousing or directing 'anger'. نامبردار گو is a short clause where نامبردار ('renowned' / 'notable') is combined with گو (say/call) — literally 'call (it/him) namabardar', i.e., 'call (it/him) renowned'. The construction is terse/poetic and the object marker-ra shows inversion/emphasis.
segments.s272-g313: دل پهلوان گشت is a nominal subject (دل پهلوان, “the champion’s heart”) followed by the past intransitive verb گشت “became.” زان is a poetic connective meaning “from that / because of that,” and پر ز درد is an adjectival phrase “full of pain,” where پر (“full of”) takes the following ز + noun construction (ز درد) to mark the content of fullness. گشت is a past-state verb “became.”
segments.s272-g314: که introduces a subordinate clause of reason (here: “for / because”), with an elided subject referring back to the champion. رخسار آزادگان is the object/phrase “the countenances of the free-born,” followed by the verb دید “saw.” The adjective زرد comes after the verb and characterizes رخسار (i.e. “(he) saw the faces (as) pale”); in poetry adjectives often follow the verb in such perceptual constructions.
segments.s272-g315: بفرمودشان is a past, polite/honorific verb meaning “he commanded/ordered them,” with the enclitic شان marking the object. بازگشتن is the infinitive “to return,” functioning as the complement of the order. به جای means “to/into the place (or presence) of” and marks the destination.
segments.s272-g316: سپهدار نیک‌اختر is a title + name (the commander Nīk‑Akhtar) and و رهنمای appends another person “and the guide.” These are the nouns governed by به جای (destination); there is no additional verb in this segment — it completes the destination of the previous infinitive.
segments.s272-g317: بدان is a short connective roughly “with that / to that” (here introducing the purpose clause), followed by تا which explicitly marks purpose “so that / in order that.” تن is the subject “body / bodies,” رنج is the noun “suffering / toil,” and بردارشان is a verb form meaning “(may) take away them” (بردار used here in an hortative/subjunctive/causative sense) with the enclitic شان referring back to the people. In sum, this is a purposive clause: “with that, so that their bodies — the suffering — may be taken away.”
segments.s272-g318: برآساید is a present/jussive-like verb meaning “be appeased / find ease / be relieved” and serves as the resultant verb of the purpose clause. از جنگ و پیکارشان marks the source or thing from which relief is hoped: از + جنگ (war) and پیکارشان “their fighting/battles” (with شان as their enclitic). Together: “may (their bodies’) suffering be relieved from war and their fights.”
segments.s272-g319: برفتند is the simple past “they went / departed.” و links clauses. شبگیر is an adverbial/qualifying word meaning “by night / at night” or “nocturnal,” and it modifies بازآمدند “they came back / returned” (بازآمدند is the past plural of بازآمد). The two verbs describe the same subjects in sequence.
segments.s272-g320: پر از X آمدند is a fixed pattern “they came / arrived full of X.” Here پر “full (of)” takes از + noun to show content: از کینه “of hatred / rancor” and و رزمساز joins a second nominal quality. رزمساز functions as a noun describing a warlike or war‑making disposition; آمدند is the main verb repeated to stress their arrival in that state.
segments.s272-g529: به یک رزم کآمد شما را شکست is built in classical word order: به + noun ('به یک رزم' = 'in/at one battle'), then the verb 'کآمد' (an archaic past form meaning 'came/occurred'), followed by the object phrase 'شما را شکست' where 'را' marks 'شما' as the one affected and 'شکست' is the abstract noun ‘defeat’ used as what befell you. Literally: 'At one battle that came, defeat to you.'
segments.s272-g530: 'کشیدید یکسر ز پیکار دست' contains the main verb 'کشیدید' (2nd person plural past — 'you drew/withdrew'), with the noun 'دست' (hand) as the thing drawn/withdrawn; 'ز پیکار' is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from the battle/struggle' and 'یکسر' is an adverb 'completely/entirely' modifying the action. Together this is the idiom 'دست کشیدن ز پیکار' = 'to abandon/cease from fighting.'
segments.s272-g531: 'بدانید' is the 2nd person plural imperative/hortative 'know' or 'be aware'; 'یکسر' is an intensifying adverb 'completely/for good'; 'کز' here introduces the content clause ('that' / 'that from'), linking to the following phrase 'این رزمگاه' ('this battlefield'). So this phrase means roughly 'know this fully, that from this battlefield...'.
segments.s272-g532: This is a conditional clause: 'اگر' = 'if'; 'بازگردد' is 3rd singular present/subjunctive of 'بازگشت' ('returns/turns back'); 'به سستی' = 'into weakness' (the preposition 'به' + noun 'سستی'); 'سپاه' (army) appears after the predicate as the subject (a common classical inversion), so the sense is 'if the army returns from this battlefield in a weakened state.'
segments.s272-g533: 'پس' ('then/therefore') opens the sentence; 'اندر ز ایران' is a poetic locative/partitive 'from among Iran' (اندر = 'in/among', ز = 'from'); 'دلاور سران' is the noun phrase 'valiant leaders/chiefs' (دلاور modifying سران). Word order places the place/partitive before the agent phrase.
segments.s272-g534: 'بیایند' is 3rd person plural present/subjunctive/hortative ('may they come' / 'let them come'); 'با' = 'with' marks the instrumental accompaniment; 'گرزهای گران' is the plural noun phrase 'heavy (or weighty) maces' ('گران' = 'heavy' modifying 'گرزهای'). The mood here is exhortatory — calling for the chiefs to come armed.
segments.s272-g535: The object phrase 'یکی را ز ما زنده اندر جهان' comes before the verb: 'یکی را' ('one [person]' with object marker 'را') + 'ز ما' ('of us') + 'زنده' ('alive') + 'اندر جهان' ('in the world'). Together it means 'one of us alive in the world' as the thing which should not be seen.
segments.s272-g536: 'نبیند' is the negative form (ن + بیند) of the present/subjunctive of 'دیدن' and here functions as a prohibitive/wish ('may not see' / 'let none see'); 'کس' with negation means 'no one'; 'از مهتران و کهان' is a partititive/agent phrase 'from among the chiefs (مهتران) and the ancients/elders (کهان)'. The clause therefore expresses the wish that none of those great men see one of us alive.
segments.s272-g177: ببندیم is the 1st‑person‑plural present/optative form (ب‑ + بند + ‑یم) meaning “let us bind/close.” دامن (noun) follows and means “hem/skirt.” اندر دگر is a compact prepositional phrase where اندر = “into/in” and دگر = “other/another,” so the whole phrase literally says “let us bind one hem into the other.” The word order (verb first, then object and prepositional phrase) is typical of Persian poetic compression.
segments.s272-g178: نشاید is an impersonal classical verb meaning “it is not fitting/becoming” (negative judgment). It governs an infinitival clause that follows: گشادن is the infinitive “to open/undo.” بر این کین کمر is a prepositional + noun phrase: بر here indicates the object/target (rendered “to/against”), این = “this,” کین = “hatred/vengeance” modifying کمر; کمر is used as “girdle/belt” here, so the phrase means “to open/undo this girdle (belt) of hatred.”
segments.s272-g179: سوی is a preposition meaning “toward/to” and introduces the goal of motion; it is followed by a list of names: گیو (Giv), لهاک and فرشیدورد. In this context لهاک appears as a proper name; the simple juxtaposition with و marks a coordinated list of people toward whom they move.
segments.s272-g180: برفتند is the simple past (3rd‑pl) “they went/proceeded.” The sequence و جستند uses جستند (past plural of جستن, here meaning “leapt/sprung” in a martial sense) to indicate rapid movement or an attack. با او نبرد places با او (“against/with him”) as the opponent marked by با and نبرد (battle) as the noun: idiomatically “they leapt to fight him” (i.e. engaged him in battle).
segments.s272-g181: برآمد is a past tense verb meaning “arose/advanced/issued forth.” It takes بر + a person to indicate direction/target (بر گیو = upon Giv). The following لهاک نیو appears as a name or epithet attached to the subject (an individual called Lahak‑Niu); in classical verse such epithets may appear after the verb for metrical or emphatic reasons.
segments.s272-g182: یکی is an indefinite pronoun here meaning “someone/one (of them).” نیزه زد is a straightforward verb phrase: نیزه (spear) + زد (past of زدن, “struck/ thrust”). بر کمرگاه گیو is a prepositional phrase where بر = “at/on,” کمرگاه = “small of the back/waist,” and گیو is the possessive/marked noun (Giv): “he struck at Giv’s waist/ small of the back.” The verb is past and active.
segments.s272-g183: همی خواست is a common classical/perfective+progressive construction meaning “he was trying/kept wanting.” کو را here is an archaic/poetic accusative: کو functioning as an interrogative/indefinite “who/whom” used objectively and followed by the object marker را. رباید is the present/infinitival form of ربودن used after خواست to indicate the attempted action “to seize/snatch.” ز زین is the poetic preposition “from the saddle” (ز = from, زین = saddle).
segments.s272-g184: نگونسار is an attributive adjective/noun meaning “ill‑fated/woeful” used as the subject (the mournful/ill‑fated one). از اسب افگند is a past active clause: از اسب = “from the horse,” افگند = past of افکندن “threw/cast.” بر زمین completes the motion phrase “onto the ground.” Word order places the cause/source (از اسب) before the verb and the goal (بر زمین) after it, a normal pattern in Persian.
segments.s272-g241: ز (poetic preposition) marks source: 'from'. The noun phrase توران سپاه اندریمان is genitive/heaped: 'the host (سپاه) of Andariman from Turan' (Persian often places the possessor after the possessed). چو introduces a simile/temporal clause ('when/as'); گرد here is a concrete simile 'dust/cloud' describing how the host arrived.
segments.s272-g242: بیامد is a simple past 'came'. دمان (variant of دامن) is a locative 'skirt/foothill' and follows the verb here (verb before locative is common in poetry). تا is the 'until' sense introducing the limit of motion/result, and به جای نبرد = 'to the place of battle' (به + جای = 'to the place', نبرد = 'battle').
segments.s272-g243: عمودی is an adjective 'vertical/upright' placed before فروهشت (a past/participle-like form 'thrown/scattered'), so the phrase is descriptive: 'a vertical (line) thrown/scattered'. بر is the preposition 'upon' and گستهم (here the proper name Gostaham) is its object: 'upon Gostaham'.
segments.s272-g244: که ... تا introduces a purposive/result clause: که + تا = 'so that / in order that'. بگسلاند is a causative/past-form verb 'to break apart / to rend', with میانش 'between him/them' (میان + -ش). ز هم = 'from one another' (ز = 'from', هم = 'together/each other'), indicating being torn apart among them.
segments.s272-g245: به تیغش برآمد is a verbal phrase meaning 'met/was met by his sword' (به + instrument/person + verb). بدو is the dative/locative 'to him/upon him'; نیم گشت is a concise poetic expression 'became half' = 'was halved' (نیم used predicatively). The verb گشت 'became' completes that nominal phrase.
segments.s272-g246: دل گستهم is a possessive/NOM phrase 'the heart of Gostaham' (گستهم = the hero's name here); زو = 'because of him / from him'. پر از بیم گشت is a standard predicate: پر (full) + از + بیم (fear) + گشت (became) = 'became full of fear'.
segments.s272-g247: به پشت یلان is a locative phrase 'to the rear/back of the heroes' (پشت = rear/behind, یلان = warriors/heroes). اندر آمد = 'came in/among' (اندر = in/among, آمد = came). هجیر (proper name) appears at the end as the subject/agent (typical poetic word order: locative + verb + subject).
segments.s272-g248: ابر is used metaphorically 'a cloud (of...)'. When followed by a name (اندریمان) it reads as a genitive/attributive phrase 'a cloud of Andariman' (i.e. Andariman's cloud). ببارید is the past tense 'rained/poured', and تیر 'arrows' is the object: 'a cloud (from Andariman) rained arrows.'
segments.s272-g417: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. برگشته is a participial/ adjectival perfect meaning 'turned back' or 'reversed' (i.e. 'turned'). بخت is the noun 'fortune' and او is the third‑person pronoun 'his'. شد is the past form 'became' and نگون is an adjective 'ill‑fated / downfallen' describing his changed state.
segments.s272-g418: بریدش is the past form 'cut (him/it) off' with the object clitic -ش. سر 'head' + از 'from' + تن 'body' form the idiom 'cut the head from the body.' به سان is the fixed simile 'like/as' and هیون is 'a beast/animal', completing the simile.
segments.s272-g419: نباید is the impersonal modal 'one must not / it must not be'; شکوهید here functions as a verb 'to complain, lament' (classic usage). ز ایشان 'about/from them' marks the object/topic, and به جنگ means 'in battle' or 'during the fighting'. The clause expresses a general prohibition.
segments.s272-g420: نشاید is another impersonal evaluative 'it is not fitting / it should not be'. کشیدن is the infinitive 'to draw/pluck' (here governing playing a stringed instrument). ز پیکار links that action to 'from/within the battle' and چنگ is the harp—literally 'it is not fitting to pluck a harp in the midst of battle.'
segments.s272-g421: ور is the conditional 'if'. ایدونک is an archaic/poetic temporal/attitudinal particle (here glossed as 'then' / 'at that time' — its exact force is uncertain). پیران is 'the elders' and بخواهد is the present/subjunctive 'should want / wish', governing نبرد 'battle/ fighting' as the object of their desire.
segments.s272-g422: به اندوه sets the manner 'with sorrow / grieving', لشکر is 'army/host'. بیارد here appears in the text where one would expect a verb 'bring' (بیاورد); the provided lemma/senses do not match that verb, so its lexical sense is uncertain in the supplied list (see note). چو گرد is a simile 'like a dust/cloud' describing the army's movement.
segments.s272-g423: همیدون is a proper name (here treated as the name of a person/leader). به انبوه ما 'with our multitude / great host' uses به to mark accompaniment/association. همچو کوه is the simile 'like a mountain', modifying the following predicate.
segments.s272-g424: بباید شدن is a periphrastic modal 'must become / must stand' (بباید = 'it must / ought to', شدن = 'become / stand in a state'), پیش او 'before him / in his presence' locates the action, and همگروه means 'companions / fellow‑members', so the clause says they must present themselves before him as comrades.
segments.s272-g497: تو is the subject pronoun 'you'. باید is a modal 'must/ought' that takes a subordinate clause introduced by the complementizer که; the clause ends with the present-subjunctive verb کنی (you do), which yields an obligation. پیکار مردان is a noun phrase 'battle of/with the men' functioning as the content of the action expressed by کنی (i.e. 'to wage battle with the men').
segments.s272-g498: به جنگ is a prepositional phrase 'into/toward battle' and اندر is the locative 'in/within'; آهنگ گردان (آهنگ = pace/formation/movement; گردان = troop/division) is the phrase meaning to array or set into a marching/war-formation. The final کنی is the same present-subjunctive verb as before; the clause literally says 'make (them) into a troop in the march/formation for war.'
segments.s272-g499: ور is the conditional 'if' and ایدونک is a temporal particle 'now/at this very moment'. The phrase از ما بدین رزمگاه functions as a circumstantial phrase tied to the following verb (literally something like 'to/for us at this battlefield'); word order is poetic and elements that mark direction or participants appear before the main verb that follows in the next segment.
segments.s272-g500: بد آگاهی is a noun phrase 'bad news / ill-omened information'. آید is the third-person singular present (here narrative) 'comes' and is followed by a post-verbal origin phrase ز توران سپاه ('an army from Turan'), a common poetic order where the verb precedes the originating/agent phrase.
segments.s272-g501: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that/so that'. ما را is the accusative 'us' (را marks the definite object). به آوردگه is a directional phrase 'to the place of bringing/arrival' and بر + کشند (here written separately as بر and کشند) together form a verb meaning 'lift/carry away' or 'take up' (3rd-person plural, narrative/subjunctive/optative sense in epic contexts). The verb is placed after its object phrases in this poetic word order.
segments.s272-g502: تن بی‌سرانمان is a noun phrase 'our headless bodies' (تن = body; بی‌سرانمان = our headless ones). به توران indicates destination 'to Turan' and کشند is the same 3rd-person-plural verb 'they carry/drag away/lead off'. The clause states the fate (carrying the headless bodies to Turan) as parallel to the previous action.
segments.s272-g503: نگر is an imperative 'look!/consider!/see to it' used to give counsel. تا introduces the consequence or admonition ('that/so that' / 'lest'), followed by سپه را (the army, with را marking it as object/topic). نیاری is a 2nd-person negative-subjunctive/imperative form meaning 'do not bring/lead' (poetic/archaic form of 'نیاوری'); به جنگ marks the direction 'into battle'. The clause is a warning: 'Make sure you do not lead the army into battle.'
segments.s272-g504: سه روز gives the duration 'three days'. اندر این is a locative 'in/at this (place)'. کرد باید درنگ is an inverted, poetic order equivalent to باید درنگ کرد ('it is necessary to make/detain a delay'); کرد is the verbal noun/infinitive 'to do', باید is the modal 'must/it is necessary', and درنگ is the noun 'delay / tarrying'. Together they mean 'you must delay (here) three days.'
segments.s272-g337: نخستین is an ordinal/adverb meaning “first.” که here is the relative/connecting particle (‘that/who/when’) introducing the clause about ضحاک. ضحاک بیدادگر is a noun phrase: the proper name ضحاک followed by the epithet بیدادگر (“the oppressor/tyrant”).
segments.s272-g338: This is a compact poetic clause. ز is the poetic preposition ‘from/over’ marking domain; گیتی is the domain ‘the world.’ به شاهی برآورد is a verbal phrase ‘raised to kingship / assumed rule’ (به + شاهی + برآورد), where برآورد is the past of the causative/performative verb ‘to raise/bring about.’ سر (literally ‘head’) is used idiomatically with برآورد: ‘to lift one’s head’ = ‘to assume power.’
segments.s272-g339: جهان را places ‘the world’ as the topical/definite object (را). چه مایه is an exclamatory phrase ‘what a measure/what a lot’ (چه + مایه = ‘what amount/what cause’). به سختی بداشت is a poetic past construction meaning ‘it bore / it experienced hardship’ (بداشت used here as a past verb ‘bore/experienced’), so the whole segment reads as an exclamation about how much suffering the world endured.
segments.s272-g340: جهان‌آفرین is the subject ‘the world‑creator’ (God). ز + و here functions as a poetic contracted/from-phrase (in the manuscript tradition this often corresponds to از او = ‘from him’), and همه is the object ‘all/everything.’ درگذاشت is a classical verb meaning ‘left/abandoned/departed’ (here in the sense ‘abandoned/withdrew’), so the clause says the Creator left/withdrew all in relation to that (i.e., because of him).
segments.s272-g341: به داد is a prepositional/circumstantial phrase ‘by/under the justice’ (داد = ‘justice’). آنک is a relative pronoun ‘he who/that which’ introducing the agent clause. آورد پیدا is a verbal sequence ‘brought to light/made manifest’ (آورد = brought/caused; پید ا = ‘apparent/manifest’ used adverbially). ستم is the object ‘oppression.’ Grammatically, the clause says ‘by the justice of him who caused oppression to become manifest.’
segments.s272-g342: ز باد is a simile/agent phrase literally ‘by the wind’ (باد = ‘wind’); آمد is the past ‘came.’ آن پادشاهی is the nominal subject ‘that kingship’ (آن = ‘that/the’ + پادشاهی = ‘kingship’). به دم is an adverbial phrase ‘in a moment’ (دم = ‘breath/moment’)—together: ‘that kingship came like the wind, in an instant.’
segments.s272-g343: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when.’ بیداد او is the noun phrase ‘his oppression/tyranny’ (بیداد = oppression, او = ‘his’). دادگر here is the common noun ‘a just/righteous person’ (دادگر = just one). برنداشت is the past verb ‘did not tolerate / did not allow to remain’ (literally ‘did not lift/leave’ in this construction), so the clause means ‘when his tyranny would not tolerate a just man.’
segments.s272-g344: یکی is an indefinite ‘one/someone.’ دادگر را is the (indefinite) object/topic ‘a just man’ (را marks him as definite/emphatic here). بر means ‘against/over/on’ and او is ‘him’ (the tyrant); برگماشت is a causative/past verb ‘set/appointed/placed’ (i.e., ‘someone set a just ruler over him’). The clause describes that, in response, a just ruler was put over the tyrant.
segments.s272-g489: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آغازی از جنگ پرداختن is a nominal phrase formed with آغازی ('beginning') + از جنگ ('of the war/fight') + the verbal noun پرداختن used here to indicate the action of taking up or setting about the fighting; together they mean 'the beginning of engaging in battle.' The whole segment is a time clause headed by چو.
segments.s272-g490: بود is a past/state auxiliary ('was' / indicates habitual/past tendency) that combines with the infinitival/masdar sense of the following verb. خواب را is topicalized with را ('sleep' as the theme). بر تو بر تاختن is a verbal phrase where بر تو ('upon you') marks the target and بر تاختن ('to charge/rush upon') is the compound verb; together the clause means 'sleep would rush upon you.' The doubled بر reflects a prepositional object (بر تو) plus the prefixed preposition of the verbal compound (بر تاختن).
segments.s272-g491: همان چون here functions as 'just as' / 'then when'. سر آری is an older poetic expression: سر (head) + آری, where آری is an archaic/poetic 2nd-person verb-form meaning 'you raise' or 'you lift' (not the interjection 'yes'); this yields 'as you raise your head'. به سوی نشیب is a directional prepositional phrase 'toward the slope/decline.'
segments.s272-g492: ز ناخفتگان 'from the wakeful ones' (source/source-of-action). بر تو is the prepositional phrase 'upon you' marking the target. آید is the verb 'comes' (present/past-narrative sense), and نهیب is the noun 'cry, shout' (placed after the verb as subject/topic in poetic order). The clause therefore means 'a cry from the wakeful comes upon you.'
segments.s272-g493: یکی دیده‌بان بر سر کوه is 'one watchman on the top of the mountain' (یکی = a certain/one; دیده‌بان = sentinel; بر سر کوه = on the mountain-top). The final دار here is best read as a verbal element belonging to an imperative/optative construction ('keep' / 'let there be') that connects into the next segment; it is an archaic/poetic form and not the common noun 'abode' in this position (the lemma list gives noun senses, so the exact verbal sense is archaic).
segments.s272-g494: سپه را is the definite object 'the army/host' (marked by را). ز دشمن is a prepositional phrase 'from the enemy.' بی‌اندوه‌دار is a predicative phrase meaning 'without sorrow / untroubled' (literally 'having no sorrow'); with the verbal sense carried by the previous دار it yields an imperative request: 'keep the army free from grief about the enemy' or 'let the host be untroubled by the foe.'
segments.s272-g495: ور is the conditional 'if'. ایدونک is a small poetic/emphatic particle (here roughly 'perhaps' / 'if, then') that softens or frames the condition. آید is 'comes' (present/attendent). ز توران زمین is the source phrase 'from the land of Turan' (توران = Turan; زمین = land). The verb + source phrase order is typical in terse poetic narration.
segments.s272-g496: شبی ناگهان is an adverbial cluster 'one night, suddenly' (شبی = by/one night; ناگهان = suddenly). تاختن‌گر is an agentive noun 'attacker/charger' (one who charges), and کمین is 'ambush' — together they describe 'a sudden nighttime attacker in ambush' or more simply 'a sudden nighttime raider who springs an ambush.' The phrase strings modifiers before the noun in typical poetic compression.
segments.s272-g233: بزد is a past-tense (perfect) verb in the third person (he struck). The preposition بر introduces the target ('on/against') and is followed by سر ('head') as its object. The conjunction و links a second object phrase; ترگ here is a noun modified by the following name فرشیدورد (possession/association by simple juxtaposition typical of older Persian), so the structure is 'he struck on the head and on Farshidvard's cloth/rag.'
segments.s272-g234: زمین را uses the object marker را to mark 'the earth' as definite object; بدرید is a past-tense verb 'tore' (perfect). The following noun ترک functions here as the resultative noun 'a crack/fissure' (i.e., 'a crack was made in the earth'), and از نبرد is a prepositional phrase 'from the battle/clash' giving the source. The sentence thus links an action (tore the earth) with the resulting crack caused by the fight.
segments.s272-g235: همی کرد is a durative/continuous past construction ('kept doing' or 'was doing'); همی is an older persistent/continuous particle and کرد is the past verb 'did/placed'. بر introduces location ('on/against') and is followed by بارگی — a poetic/archaic noun (here functioning as 'hem, fold, or edge of a garment') — then دست راست 'right hand' as the body part placed there. Word order is typical: verb phrase + locative phrase.
segments.s272-g236: به + اسب + اندر آمد is a directional phrase: به (toward/onto) + اسب (horse) with اندر آمد 'entered/approached/mounted' (an older two-word verb meaning 'came into' or 'mounted'). نبود is the past existential/negative 'there was not' and takes a following relative/indefinite آنچ ('that which') as its object; خواست is the past of 'to want'. Together this yields a verbless clause in English: 'what he wanted was not (there).'
segments.s272-g237: پس is a temporal connective 'then/after that'. بیژن is the subject. اندر + دمان gives a locative phrase 'in/within the damaan' (دمان here is a poetic word for 'skirt, hem, edge' — often used figuratively for the fringe/edge of something, e.g. edge of the battlefield). گستهم is an archaic poetic past form meaning 'wandered' (morphologically looks like a first-person form but functions here in narrative as a past action).
segments.s272-g238: ابا here is the older orthography/usage for the conjunction 'با' = 'with' (a poetic variant); نامداران نامداران ایران means 'the renowned/famed men of Iran' (نامداران modifying ایران). به هم is an idiomatic pair meaning 'together' or 'in company'. The whole phrase indicates Bijan moved together with Iran's celebrated men.
segments.s272-g239: به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'near' or 'toward the vicinity of'; توران specifies the place (Turan). سپاه is the subject 'army/host' and آمدند is the past tense third-person plural 'they came/arrived'. Word order places the locative phrase up front for emphasis: 'near Turan the army came.'
segments.s272-g240: This is a nominal coordination: two epithets/compounds (خلیده‌دل 'withdrawn-hearted/reticent' and کینه‌خواه 'vindictive, revenge-seeking') joined by و and followed by آمدند 'they came' (3rd plural past). The adjectives act as nouns/epithets describing the people who arrived.
segments.s272-g201: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. گیو is the proper name (subject). اندر این بود is a locative/state predicate ('was in this/that state') where اندر (in/within) + این (this/the foregoing) precede the past copula بود. لهاک زود is an adverbial phrase following the clause describing Lahak's haste: لهاک ('haste, eagerness') used adverbially and زود ('quickly/soon') intensifies it and links forward to the verbal action in the next segment.
segments.s272-g202: نشست is the simple past 'sat/settled' (here 'dismounted, sat down'). از بر is the compound prepositional idea 'from off / from upon' (از + بر), locating the action relative to the mount. بادپای is a descriptive noun 'the swift‑stepping / swift‑footed (mount)' and چو دود is a simile 'like smoke' (چو = 'like/as', دود = 'smoke'), i.e. he alighted as swiftly or lightly as a wisp of smoke.
segments.s272-g203: ابا here appears as an archaic/metrical form (see note) and functions like the preposition 'با' ('with'); the phrase therefore lists weapons: گرز (mace) coordinated with نیزه (spear) by و ('and') and the prepositional با ('with'). بر سان شیر is a prepositional/qualifying phrase where سان means 'like' and شیر 'lion' forms a simile characterizing the attackers ('like lions').
segments.s272-g204: بر + proper name (گیو) gives the target of the attack ('against/upon Giv'). رفتند is the past plural 'they went / they moved' used here as 'they charged/attacked'. هر دو is a distributive/collective 'each/both' and دلیر is a predicate adjective 'brave' attached to the two attackers ('both brave').
segments.s272-g205: چه مایه is an exclamatory quantifier 'what an amount / what a mass' (classical poetic usage). ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. چنگ here means 'claw/hand/grip' metaphorically for blows or grasp, and دلاور سران is a genitive phrase 'of the valiant chiefs' (دلاور modifying the plural noun سران), so the whole phrase means roughly 'what a multitude from the hands/clutches of the valiant leaders.'
segments.s272-g206: بر او is 'upon him' (target = او). The doubled بر here is emphatic/poetic framing before the verb. ببارید is the past tense of باریدن 'to fall / to shower' (used figuratively: 'rained down'). گرز گران is a noun + adjective 'maces heavy' = 'heavy maces' that are the items raining down; word order is noun then adjective, typical in poetry.
segments.s272-g207: به زین is a locative/instrumental phrase 'on/at the saddle' (به + زین). خدنگ اندورن places a dagger 'خدنگ' 'within/inside' (اندورن = 'within/inside') the saddle area — a compact way to say 'a dagger at/in the saddle'. بد سوار is an adjectival phrase 'bad/ill-mounted rider' (بد qualifying سوار), i.e. an ill‑equipped or unfortunate horseman; overall this segment sets up the rider's equipment/condition.
segments.s272-g208: ستوهی is the noun 'weariness' (or adjective 'weary'); نیامدش is the negative past 'did not come to him' (the enclitic -ش = 'to him'). از کارزار is the source phrase 'from the battle/struggle'. Together the clause means literally 'weariness did not come to him from the battle' → 'he did not grow weary from the fighting.'
segments.s272-g553: گر is the conditional particle 'if'. ایدونک is an archaic/poetic particle meaning 'thus/like this' that qualifies the condition. پیمان is the object 'the pact/pledge'. به جای آوردید is a verbal phrase: به جای آوردن = 'to fulfill/accomplish'; آورید here is a 2nd-person plural/formal verb (subjunctive/imperative in classical Persian) used after گر to express 'if you perform/fulfil'.
segments.s272-g554: سران 'the chiefs' is the direct object and را is the definite/object marker. ز لشکر is a source phrase 'from the army' (ز = from). به پای is a goal/locative phrase literally 'to the foot' (i.e. 'to their feet' / 'to the ground'). آورید repeats the 2nd-person plural verb form 'bring' (imperative/subjunctive) — the clause commands bringing the chiefs from the host to the speaker's feet.
segments.s272-g555: وگر 'but if / and if' introduces the conditional. همگروه is 'a fellow-comrade / comrade'. اندر آید = 'enters / comes in' (آید is 3rd-singular present/subjunctive 'comes'), and به جنگ = 'into battle' or 'to battle' (prepositional phrase marking direction/scene).
segments.s272-g556: نباید is an impersonal prohibition 'one must not / it must not be'. کشیدن is a verbal noun/infinitive 'to draw/ to pull' — in the collocation کشیدنِ چنگ it means 'to play (the) harp' (i.e. draw the strings). ز پیکار is a temporal/locative phrase 'during/from battle' (classical z often marks milieu), and چنگ is the object 'the harp' placed after the clause. The whole clause prohibits playing the harp amid combat.
segments.s272-g557: اگر introduces a hypothetical. سر 'head' is the direct object, همه quantifies it 'every/all', سوی خنجر literally 'toward the dagger' (direction/instrument), and بریم is a 1st-person-plural verb form (classical subjunctive/hortative) expressing the hypothetical action 'we cut/ we were to cut off'. The clause is a conditional protasis ('if we cut off every head with the dagger').
segments.s272-g558: به روزی here is idiomatic: روزی can mean 'one day / at one time' or 'fortune/sustenance' (the lemma's listed senses are verbal, so the noun sense is not provided); بزادیم is a 1st-person-plural past/optative 'we produced / we begot' (here used hypothetically), و is 'and', then روزی again and مریم (a name). The clause reads elliptically — 'one day we produced X and another day Maryam' — and is proverb-like/elliptical in the poem. Several word meanings are archaic/poetic, so the precise referent is literary.
segments.s272-g559: وگرنه = 'otherwise'. سرانشان is 'their chiefs' (سران + possessive suffix شان). برآرم is 1st-singular present/subjunctive/optative 'I will lift/bring up' or 'I will put up' — here idiomatically 'I will hang' when combined with به دار. به دار = 'to the gallows' (دار in poetic/epic context = gallows). So the clause threatens to hang the leaders if the earlier condition fails.
segments.s272-g560: دو رویه 'two-faced' or 'having two manners/sides' (دو = two, رویه = manner/face). بود is the past copula 'was' (here rendered as a general statement 'is/was'). گردش روزگار is a genitive phrase 'the turning/rotation of time' i.e. 'the vicissitudes of fate/time'. Together: 'the turnings of fate have two faces' — a poetic way of saying fortune is changeable / two‑faced.
segments.s272-g377: 'که' introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('that' / 'because'); 'چندان' is a quantifier 'so/many'; 'پسر' here means 'young men' (not just literal sons); 'پیش من' = 'in my presence' ('پیش' + pronoun), 'کشته شد' is a passive past construction ('were killed') with 'شد' as auxiliary.
segments.s272-g378: Subject phrase 'دل نامداران همه' = 'the hearts of the famed, all of them'; 'گشته شد' is a compound passive/perfect-like expression ('were made/turned/ became'), where 'گشته' is the participle 'turned/been made' and 'شد' functions as auxiliary 'were'.
segments.s272-g379: 'کنون' = 'now'; 'با سپاهی' = 'with a host/army' (prepositional phrase); word order is slightly emphatic/poetic: 'چنین' ('such') modifies the adjective 'کینه‌جوی' ('vengeful, revenge-seeking').
segments.s272-g380: 'بیامد' is simple past 'he came'; the following mini-clause 'به روی اندر آورد روی' is an idiomatic, somewhat redundant phrasing meaning 'he turned/ brought his face (forward) / showed his face': 'به' marks direction, 'روی' = 'face', 'اندر آورد' = 'brought/turned in', and the final 'روی' is the object 'his face'.
segments.s272-g381: چو introduces a conditional ('if'); با ما is a prepositional phrase 'with us' that marks the party presumed to be insufficient to satisfy him; بسنده is a predicate meaning 'sufficient, satisfying'; نخواهد is the negative future/modal auxiliary ('will not [be/want]') that applies to بسنده; بدن is literally 'body' used metonymically for the person. Word order follows classical Persian (particle + prepositional phrase + predicate + auxiliary).
segments.s272-g382: همی is an adverb giving an ongoing/continuous sense ('still/continues to'); داستانها is the direct object 'stories/tales'; بخواهد is the modal/auxiliary 'will/wishes to' governing the infinitive زدن; here زدن is used idiomatically with داستان (داستان زدن) meaning 'to tell/relate tales' (an idiomatic sense not listed in the provided senses).
segments.s272-g383: همی here is a discourse adverb ('thus/so'); چاره 'remedy/plan' is the object of سازد ('he makes/frames'); سازد is a present/habitual-form verb used in epic narration meaning 'makes/arranges'; the sequence ب + دان in the manuscript functions together as بدان ('for that' / 'to that end') — the two pieces are shown separately in the segmentation but combine semantically; تا introduces the boundary clause 'until' followed by the subject سپاه.
segments.s272-g384: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; توران is the place-name 'Turan'; بیاید is the subjunctive/optative form of آمدن used under تا ('until … comes/may come'); the following ب + دین together form بدین ('to this'), marking direction/target, and رزمگاه is the noun 'battlefield'. (Note: the segmentation shows ب and دین separately, but they function as the prepositional particle + demonstrative 'to this'.)
segments.s272-g73: ز is the poetic preposition 'from.' گردان (troop, company) is the head noun, followed by the numeral phrase دو صد ('two hundred') in postnominal position (poetic/older word order). با introduces an accompaniment 'with,' and درفشی here indicates a banner/standard-bearer. چو باد is a simile ('like the wind'), with چو = 'like/as' and باد = 'wind.'
segments.s272-g74: به marks the indirect object ('to/for'). فرخنده is an adjective 'auspicious/fortunate' modifying گرگین, which here functions as a proper name (Gorgin). میلاد in this line is used as a proper name (Milad) rather than the lexical noun 'birth' (hence not matched to the provided noun senses). داد is the past tense verb 'gave/presented/brought'; the subject is omitted (poetic ellipsis) and is understood from the narrative context.
segments.s272-g75: بدو = 'to him' (compound clitic meaning 'to him/unto it') followed by گفت (here functioning as the past-tense verb 'said' — the supplied senses list shows noun senses, so the verbal use is not in that list). ز ایدر = 'from here' or 'hereabouts,' with ز as a poetic locative/prepositional element and ایدر = 'here.' بگردان عنان is an imperative: بگردان (turn!) + عنان (the reins) as the direct object.
segments.s272-g76: This is a coordinated imperative phrase. The first word (ابا) in the manuscript here functions as an imperative/verb-like element introducing the weapon to take up; the provided dictionary senses (reluctance) do not capture this verbal/imperative use, so its sense is flagged as unknown. گرز = 'mace' (direct object). و = 'and.' با = 'with' marking accompaniment. آبداده literally 'water-tested' or 'well-tempered' (a common epithet for a spear, i.e. tempered by water) modifies سنان, which is 'spear.'
segments.s272-g77: کنون = 'now.' رفت is the past-form stem of رفتن ('to go'), here used with باید to express obligation; in this poetic ordering the lexical verb appears before باید: 'went/must' -> 'must go.' باید = 'must/it is necessary.' بر آن رزمگاه = 'to that battlefield,' with بر marking direction ('to/toward') and آن = 'that' modifying رزمگاه 'battlefield.'
segments.s272-g78: جهان here means 'the world' or 'this life.' کرد is the verb 'made/did' (causative/basic 'to make/do'). باید again marks necessity: 'must make.' بر ایشان marks the target ('upon/against them'), and سیاه is 'black/dark' used metaphorically ('to bring darkness/gloom upon them'). The clause means 'the world must be made dark for them' (i.e. misfortune must come upon them).
segments.s272-g79: که introduces a result or explanatory clause ('so that' / 'because'). پشت = 'back' (here 'rear' of the army). سپهشان = سپه + possessive suffix = 'their army/host.' به هم = a compound adverb meaning 'together/one upon another.' بر شکست uses شکست ('defeat/breaking') to indicate that the army's back was broken — بر here is part of the verbal-nominal construction showing the action inflicted ('was broken').
segments.s272-g80: دل پهلوانان = 'the hearts of the champions' (دل + genitive noun). شد = 'became' (copular/predicative auxiliary). از marks cause ('from/because of'): از درد = 'from pain' or 'with sorrow'; درد = 'pain/grief.' پست = 'low/abject' describing the state of the hearts (i.e. their spirits were humbled/dismayed).
segments.s272-g225: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' introducing material or source; پولاد here means 'steel' (a material). در چنگ is a prepositional phrase 'in the grip/claw' (در = in, چنگ = claw/grip), and سیمین ستون is a noun phrase 'silver column/shaft' (سیمین 'silvery', ستون 'shaft/column') — the whole chunk names a steel shaft held in a silver grip.
segments.s272-g226: به زیر اندرون = 'down into the interior' (به = toward/into, زیر اندرون = under/inside). باره‌ای is an indefinite noun 'a charge/throw/blow' (باره + -ای making it 'a certain/one blow/charge'), and چون هیون is a simile 'like a savage beast' (چون = like, هیون = beast/monster). Together: 'he drove it down inside in a charge like a beast.'
segments.s272-g227: چو here = 'when'; گرازه is the animal 'boar/sow' (the combatant). بگشاد is an archaic/poetic past form of گشودن 'opened/swelled/shot forth' (the b- prefix is common in older Persian verbal forms). از باد دست is a condensed figurative expression: از = 'with/from', باد = 'wind' (here a metaphor for a sudden force) and دست = 'stroke/hand' — together meaning 'with a gust-like stroke' or 'as if struck by the wind'.
segments.s272-g228: به زین بر شد is an idiomatic sequence meaning 'he mounted/put (his hand) on the saddle' (به = toward/onto, زین = saddle, بر شد = lit. 'became upon' → 'mounted/placed upon'). آن ترگ پولاد بست is a clause with آن = 'that', ترگ = 'rag/strip', پولاد = 'steel' (compound 'steel-strip'), and بست = past of بستن 'he fastened/tied' — 'he fastened that steel strip.'
segments.s272-g229: بزد is simple past 'he struck' (transitive). نیزه‌ای is an indefinite accusative/instrument 'a spear' (نیزه + -ای), and بر کمربند اوی marks the target 'against his belt' (بر = against/on, کمربند = belt, اوی = his). The phrase means 'he struck with/a spear against his belt (i.e. aimed at his belt).'
segments.s272-g230: زره بود = 'the armor was/held' (زره = armor, بود = past copula 'was'). نگسست is the past (here negative sense: ن- + گسست) of گسستن and means 'did not break/was not torn' in context; پیوند اوی = 'its fastening/strap' (پیوند = bond/fastening, اوی = his/its). Together: 'his armor stood firm; its fastening did not break.'
segments.s272-g231: یکی here means 'a certain/one' used like 'a' or 'one'; تیغ = 'sword'. در چنگ = 'in (his) hand/grip' (در = in, چنگ = claw/hand used metaphorically), and بیژن is the subject's name. چو شیر is a simile 'like a lion' modifying Bijan's action — the whole front phrase sets the scene 'a sword in Bijan's grasp, like a lion.'
segments.s272-g232: به پشت گرازه درآمد is a verbal phrase 'entered into the boar's back' (به = into/toward, پشت = back, گرازه = the boar). درآمد here is used as a past action 'entered/penetrated'. دلیر is a postpositive adjective/adverb 'bravely/boldly' describing the action. So: 'he bravely drove it into the boar's back.'
segments.s272-g441: همه هرچ is a fronted quantifier/indefinite doublet meaning “everything/whatever.” It precedes the subject شاه (the king). از فریبرز is a prepositional phrase marking the source or object of seeking, and جست is the simple past (classical/poetic) of جستن ‘to seek’ placed at the end (typical S…V order in older Persian).
segments.s272-g442: ز طوس آن groups origin/identification: ز marks origin and طوس refers to the Tus man (proper name), with آن ‘that/the one.’ کنون is a temporal adverb ‘now.’ از تو is a prepositional complement ‘in/from you,’ and بیند is the third-person singular classical present/optative form of دیدن meaning ‘sees’ (here used as a general/presentive). درست is an adverb/adjective ‘right/correct,’ modifying the seeing.
segments.s272-g443: همه سر به سر is an idiomatic adverbial phrase meaning “one and all / altogether.” It precedes the person marked by ترا (to you) and the verb بنده‌ایم ‘we are servants.’ مر and the clitic ترا form an archaic/pronominal cluster indicating the relation to the addressee; the main predicate is the 1st-person plural copula/nominal predicate بنده‌ایم.
segments.s272-g444: به here marks cause/ground (‘by/under/at’). فرمان و رایت are two coordinated nouns (‘command/decree and standard/banner’). سرافگنده‌ایم is a present-tense 1st-person plural adjectival predicate ‘we are abashed/ashamed,’ completing the clause and giving the reason or state (we are abashed under your command and banner).
segments.s272-g445: گر is the conditional particle ‘if.’ ایدونک is a small temporal/intonational particle (poetic/colloquial) meaning roughly ‘then’/‘at that time.’ پیران is here the plural noun (the elders/old men or leaders) and ز توران سپاه places them as originating from Turan’s host (‘from Turan’s army’); the phrase sets up the protasis of the condition.
segments.s272-g446: سران is the plural noun ‘leaders’ (the direct object of آورد when the subject is understood from the previous phrase). آورد is the simple past 3rd-person singular ‘brought.’ پیش ما is a prepositional phrase ‘before us / in our presence.’ کینه‌خواه is an adjectival/nominal modifier ‘vengeful / bent on revenge’ that characterizes the actors or their intent.
segments.s272-g447: This is a distributive count: ز ما ده مبارز means ‘from us ten fighters’ (ز marks source, ده is the numeral, مبارز the noun). وز ایشان هزار is the coordinated portion ‘and from them a thousand’ (وز here functions as the older fused form of و + ز ‘and from’). The construction lists contributions from each side.
segments.s272-g448: نگر is an imperative ‘look!’ or ‘see!’ introducing an injunction. تا که here introduces the subordinate clause ‘until / so that’ and is followed by پیچد, a third-person (subjunctive/presentive) form of پیچیدن ‘to twist/turn’ used idiomatically of a head being tossed off or struck away. سر is the patient (‘a head’) and از کارزار ‘from the battlefield’ marks source/context of the action.
segments.s272-g425: "که" introduces a subordinate clause meaning "that/for"; "چندان" is a degree adverb "to that extent / so"; "دلیران" is the plural noun "brave ones" and "همه" emphasizes "all/everyone"; "خسته‌دل" is a predicate adjective/compound meaning "heart‑weary / disheartened". The clause is verbless (typical poetic compression) and describes the state of the heroes.
segments.s272-g426: "ز" (poetic) = "from / because of" introduces the causes "تیمار و اندوه" ("care and sorrow"); "پیوسته" is an adverb "constantly" and "دل" ("heart") is the affected noun. Word order is inverted for poetic effect — literally "from care and sorrow constantly (the) heart" — yielding "the heart is ever heavy with care and sorrow."
segments.s272-g427: "بر آنم که" is an idiom meaning "I am of that mind / I intend that"; "ما را" marks the beneficiary/affected party (here "for us"/emphatic "we"), and "بود دستگاه" literally "there would be a device/plan" — "بود" is a copular/auxiliary past form used here in a desiderative/optative sense: "that we should have a plan."
segments.s272-g428: "از ایشان" = "from them"; "برآریم" is 1st‑person plural optative/subjunctive "let us raise / we may raise" (classical/poetic present‑subjunctive form); the object "گرد سیاه" = "black dust/cloud" (گرد here = dust). The clause expresses the intended action to be performed against "them."
segments.s272-g429: "بگفت" is an archaic/past preverb meaning "he said" (verb first, as in classical style); "این سخن" = "these words/this speech"; "سر به سر" is an adverbial idiom meaning "throughout / straight through / completely"; "پهلوان" is the subject "the champion." The order is V‑O‑adv‑S, common in older poetic diction.
segments.s272-g430: "به پیش" = "in the presence of / before"; "جهاندیده فرخ گوان" is a titled name/epithet: "جهاندیده" (worldly‑wise) modifies the name element "فرخ گوان". The whole phrase means "in the presence of the worldly‑wise Farrokh‑gūān."
segments.s272-g431: چو is temporal 'when' introducing the clause; سالارشان is the possessed noun 'their leader/commander' (سالار + possessive suffix); مهربانی is the object/compliment 'kindness, mercy'; نمود is the classical past/perfective verb 'showed' (نمود = 'displayed/shown'), fitting the narrative past.
segments.s272-g432: همه 'everyone' is the subject; پاک here is adverbial 'completely/altogether' qualifying the action; بر پای is a fixed phrase 'to (their) feet' (بر + پای); جستند is the past plural 'they leaped/sprung'; زود is an adverb 'quickly/at once' — together: 'all quickly leaped to their feet.'
segments.s272-g537: This is a fronted adverbial + verb phrase: برون (an adverb 'out/forth') comes first for emphasis, followed by کرد (past stem of زدن/کردن used with infinitival sense) and بايد ('must'), so the construction means 'one must cause/drive out'. ز دلها is a source phrase ('from the hearts') and نهیب is the noun object ('a loud cry' — the thing to be driven out). Word order is emphatic/poetic (adverb + verb + modal + source + object).
segments.s272-g538: گزیدن here is used in the sense 'to pierce/sting/afflict' rather than the common 'to choose'; مر is the archaic oblique pronoun 'me' (to/for me); این غمگنان را is the marked (را) definite object meaning 'these sorrowful ones/people'; شکیب is vocative/adjectival 'patient' (addressing someone as 'O patient one') or a nominal 'patience' used poetically. The clause reads as an address: 'O patient one, these sorrowful ones afflict/pierce me.'
segments.s272-g539: چنین ('thus/such') modifies داستان ('tale, story'). داستان زدن is a fixed verbal phrase meaning 'to tell a tale'; زد is the past-stem of زدن used here with موبدان as the agent. شه (king) is the indirect object/recipient ('to the king') — classical Persian often omits a preposition for the dative. Word order places the object ('such a tale') first for emphasis.
segments.s272-g540: که is the subordinating conjunction 'that/who' introducing reported content. پیروز یزدان is a nominal phrase 'victorious God' (پیروز modifying یزدان). بود is the past copula 'was' and جاودان is a predicate adjective 'eternal'. The clause therefore reports the content of the tale: 'that the victorious God was eternal.'
segments.s272-g541: جهان سر به سر is an idiom meaning 'the world throughout / from end to end' (سر به سر formed by repeating سر); با فراز و نشیب is a prepositional phrase 'with rise(s) and fall(s)' describing the world's ups and downs. The series of nouns is descriptive and held together by با and و.
segments.s272-g542: چنینست is the copular phrase 'it is thus / such is' summarizing the preceding description. تا here functions temporally 'until', and رفتن is the infinitive 'to go' (literal 'going'); اندر is the preposition 'into/in', and نهیب (again) denotes a loud/reproachful cry or tumult. Together the clause reads 'such is [the world] until going into the (tumultuous) cry' — a poetic way to say the world has ups and downs until one is cast into turmoil.
segments.s272-g543: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. از introduces the domain 'concerning/of' and بر و بوم is a binomial idiom meaning 'lands and homeland' (بر here is part of the idiom rather than a separate locative), followed by و فرزند خویش 'and his children' where خویش is followed by the clitic ش marking possession 'his'. The whole phrase lists what is at stake: his realm, his home, and his offspring.
segments.s272-g544: که introduces a relative/question clause 'who/that'. اندیشد is the classical 3rd-person-present/subjunctive form of اندیشیدن meaning 'thinks/considers' (here rhetorical: 'who will think of...'). از before جان and پیوند carries the sense 'about / concerning' (of one's life and bonds). خویش + ش again marks 'his own' (his kin). The clause therefore reads 'who will think of his life and his bonds/kin.'
